<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List and briefly explain any three interaction modalities used in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Tactile / Touch Interaction (1 mark):** Uses capacitive or resistive sensors to detect finger contact on a surface. The most common modality in smartphones and smart-home wall panels. Resolution is limited by the **sensor electrode pitch** (typically **5 – 10 mm**).

2. **Voice / Speech Interaction (1 mark):** Employs a **MEMS microphone** feeding an **ASR + NLU pipeline**. The system converts acoustic signals to text and then to a structured intent. The accuracy is benchmarked by the **Word Error Rate (WER)**, ideally **< 8 %** for IoT commands.

3. **Gesture Interaction (1 mark):** Captures motion via an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — accelerometer + gyroscope — or via a **vision-based hand-pose estimator** (e.g., MediaPipe Hands). The interaction event is fired when the recognised gesture matches a pre-registered template.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative accepted modalities:** *Gaze*, *Haptic*, *Tangible*. Any three correctly explained modalities fetch full marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between MQTT and CoAP as remote-control protocols in IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Attribute | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | **TCP** (port 1883 / 8883) | **UDP** (port 5683 / 5684) |
| Pattern | Publish / Subscribe | Request / Response |
| Header size | 2 bytes minimum | 4 bytes minimum |
| Security | TLS | DTLS |
| Best for | Cloud, unreliable networks | Constrained, LAN devices |

(1.5 marks for *any three* valid contrasts; 1.5 marks for a concluding sentence selecting the right protocol for a given use case.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** ESE Part B carries 14-mark questions with a compulsory internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is worth 7 marks.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **end-to-end architecture of a cloud-mediated remote-control system** for a smart home. Your answer must include a labelled block diagram and a description of the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **end-to-end latency budget** for a voice-commanded IoT actuation. State the formula, substitute realistic values, and identify the bottleneck. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The architecture is decomposed into **four logical layers** (drawn below) and a **fifth physical layer**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[User Interaction Layer] --> L2[Mobile / Web Application Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Cloud Broker Layer]
    L3 --> L4[Home Gateway Layer]
    L4 --> L5[Actuator Layer]
    style L1 fill:#e3f2fd
    style L5 fill:#ffcdd2
```

- **Layer 1 — User Interaction Layer (1 mark):** Captures user intent via a modality — touch, voice, or gesture. Sensor sampling and local pre-processing occur here.
- **Layer 2 — Mobile / Web Application Layer (1 mark):** The user-facing app encodes the intent into a **structured payload** (typically JSON), applies a **device-id header**, and initiates a **secure session** (OAuth 2.0 + TLS 1.3).
- **Layer 3 — Cloud Broker Layer (2 marks):** The **MQTT broker** (e.g., HiveMQ) receives the publish on a hierarchical topic (e.g., `home/livingroom/ac/cmd`), authenticates via **X.509 client certificates**, and fans the message out to all subscribers. Persistence is provided by the **retained message** and **Last-Will-and-Testament (LWT)** features.
- **Layer 4 — Home Gateway Layer (1.5 marks):** A **Raspberry Pi / ESP32 gateway** runs a persistent MQTT client, subscribes to the device topic, decodes the JSON, and issues an **OS-level command** (e.g., `os.system("irsend SEND_ONCE ac KEY_POWER")` or a GPIO toggle via `libgpiod`).
- **Layer 5 — Actuator Layer (1.5 marks):** The actuator (relay, motor, IR LED) performs the physical effect and reports back via a `…/status` topic, completing the feedback loop.

#### Model Solution — (b)

Reusing the derivation from Section 3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{end2end} &= T_{cap} + T_{uplink} + T_{asr} + T_{pub} + T_{sub} + T_{act} \\
&= 120 + 65 + 340 + 55 + 70 + 90 \\
&= 740 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bottleneck identification (3 marks):** $T_{asr} = 340$ ms dominates, contributing **45.9 %** of the total latency. The optimisation pathway is *on-device keyword spotting*, which can reduce $T_{asr}$ by a factor of **8× – 10×**, bringing the total to $\approx 440$ ms.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of **Fitts' Law**, design a smart-light wall-switch button. Assume a user reaches from a **standing position 0.5 m away** and the button must accommodate a **movement time of ≤ 400 ms**. Use $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit. Justify your final choice of diameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **MQTT QoS levels 0, 1, and 2** with respect to a remote door-lock actuation. Which level is the *minimum acceptable* for safety, and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

Setting $MT \leq 400$ ms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \\
400 &= 50 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{W} + 1\right) \\
\frac{350}{150} &= \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{W} + 1\right) \\
2.333 &= \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{W} + 1\right) \\
2^{2.333} &= \frac{0.5}{W} + 1 \\
5.04 &= \frac{0.5}{W} + 1 \\
4.04 &= \frac{0.5}{W} \\
W &= \frac{0.5}{4.04} \\
W &\approx 0.1237 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Design Choice (1 mark):** A **diameter of 0.13 m (13 cm)** or larger meets the movement-time constraint. KTU evaluators also reward the engineering judgement of *selecting a slightly larger size* (e.g., 0.15 m) to compensate for elderly users and to follow the **WCAG 2.5.5 target-size guideline** of $\geq 44 \times 44$ CSS pixels.

#### Model Solution — (b)

| QoS | Name | Delivery | Use Case for Door-Lock |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget, **no ack** | **Unsafe** — packets can be silently lost. |
| **1** | At least once | PUBACK; possible duplicates | **Acceptable** if the lock is **idempotent** (lock command is safe to repeat). |
| **2** | Exactly once | 4-step handshake (PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP) | **Safest**; eliminates duplicates. |

**Minimum acceptable = QoS 1 (3 marks)** because it guarantees *at least one* delivery. The duplicate risk is mitigated by the lock firmware treating the *same* "lock" command as a no-op. QoS 2 is preferred for **bank-vault or nuclear-shield** scenarios where even a duplicate must be suppressed at the protocol level.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Do **not** answer "QoS 2 only" without justifying the cost. The QoS 2 handshake is **2× to 3× slower** than QoS 1 and doubles broker load. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if you ignore this trade-off.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Interaction and Remote Control** is the *contract* between the human and the physical world in an IoT system.
- The **five canonical stages** are *Capture → Encode → Transport → Brokerage → Actuation*; the master latency equation is $T_{end2end} = T_{cap} + T_{enc} + T_{net} + T_{brk} + T_{act}$.
- **Three architectural patterns** recur: *Direct LAN*, *Cloud-Mediated*, *Edge/Fog*. Cloud-mediated is the **KTU favourite**.
- **Six interaction modalities**: Touch, Voice, Gesture, Gaze, Haptic, Tangible — know the *sensor* and the *limitation* of each.
- **Fitts' Law** is the *only* tested equation for interaction design: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ where $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$ is in **bits**.
- **MQTT QoS 0 / 1 / 2**: know the **ack handshake**, the **default ports (1883, 8883)**, and the **TLS / X.509** security wrapper.
- **CoAP** runs over **UDP 5683 / 5684** with **DTLS**; it is preferred for *constrained* devices.
- **Latency budgets**: < 100 ms for safety-critical, < 1 s for human-in-the-loop home automation.
- **Voice command pipelines** always need both **ASR** (acoustic model) and **NLU** (intent model); on-device keyword spotting reduces latency by 8×–10×.
- **Idempotency** of the actuator command is the *key* to safely running QoS 1.
- **Security triad to memorise**: *TLS for MQTT, DTLS for CoAP, X.509 / OAuth 2.0 for identity*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Cheat Sheet:** Memorise **Fitts' Law**, the **end-to-end latency equation**, the **three QoS handshakes**, the **default ports**, and the **five-stage pipeline**. These *five items* alone cover **≈ 80 %** of the marks allotted to this topic across the past three KTU ESE papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PHY/MAC Layer (3GPP MTC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIT601 - IOT – ARCHITECTURE, PROTOCOLS AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** PHY/MAC Layer (3GPP MTC) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) The **3GPP Machine-Type Communication (MTC) PHY/MAC Layer** refers to the optimized Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layer protocols standardized by the **3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP)** to enable efficient, low-power, and wide-area cellular connectivity for **Massive IoT (mMTC)** deployments. Within Release 13 and beyond, 3GPP introduced two principal cellular IoT radio access technologies: **LTE-M (Long-Term Evolution for Machines, also called eMTC — enhanced Machine-Type Communication)** and **NB-IoT (Narrowband Internet of Things)**. Both technologies re-engineer the LTE PHY/MAC stack to support low data rates, deep coverage, and massive device densities. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The PHY/MAC layer for 3GPP MTC focuses on three pillars — **(a)** Coverage Enhancement (CE), **(b)** Low Power Operation (PSM/eDRX), and **(c)** Reduced Device Complexity (single-antenna, half-duplex, narrowband RF). ### Conceptual Analogy: The "Truck Lane" on a Highway Imagine a busy **6-lane superhighway** (the standard LTE network) where sports cars (smartphones, high-speed users) zoom through carrying huge loads of data at high speeds. Now imagine you need to send **millions of tiny postcards** (small MTC packets) at low cost, from devices that don't move fast — like a **parking sensor**, a **water meter**, or a **livestock tracker**. You don't need the sports car. You need a **dedicated narrow lane** with a **slow, fuel-efficient three-wheeler** (NB-IoT) that can crawl into a deep basement to deliver that postcard. 3GPP MTC essentially **carves out a special narrowband truck lane** on the cellular highway so that low-data, low-power IoT devices can communicate efficiently without disturbing the broadband traffic. The PHY layer defines **how the radio signal is shaped** to fit in that lane, while the MAC layer **decides when each truck (device) is allowed to enter the lane** without crashing into another. ## 1.2 Key PHY/MAC Layer Sub-Components | Layer Component | Function in 3GPP MTC | |---|---| | **Downlink (DL) PHY** | OFDMA-based transmission with reduced bandwidth (**1.4 MHz** for LTE-M, **180 kHz** for NB-IoT) | | **Uplink (UL) PHY** | Supports both **SC-FDMA** (LTE-M) and **single-tone / multi-tone** transmission (NB-IoT) | | **MPDCCH / NPDCCH** | New Physical Downlink Control Channel carrying scheduling info for MTC devices | | **PRACH / NPRACH** | Modified Random Access Channel with repetitions for coverage extension | | **PUSCH / NPUSCH** | Physical Uplink Shared Channel supporting subcarrier spacing of **3.75 kHz** (NB-IoT) and **15 kHz** | | **MAC Layer** | Handles scheduling, HARQ with extensive **repetition coding**, and Radio Resource Control (RRC) signalling | > [!NOTE] > **Constants & Metrics to Memorize (Bold for KTU High-Yield):** > - **LTE-M Bandwidth = 1.4 MHz** (6 Resource Blocks) > - **NB-IoT Bandwidth = 180 kHz** (1 Resource Block) > - **NB-IoT Subcarrier Spacing options = 3.75 kHz (UL) and 15 kHz (DL/UL)** > - **Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL) = 164 dB** for NB-IoT, **156 dB** for LTE-M > - **Maximum Repetitions = 128 (LTE-M) / 128–2048 (NB-IoT)** > - **Latency target = 10 seconds** (for exception reports) > - **Battery life target = 10 years** on a single 5 Wh battery ## 1.3 Why PHY/MAC Optimization is Mandatory for IoT Standard LTE was designed for **human-centric broadband**. A typical smartphone consumes around **2–5 W** during transmission. However, an IoT sensor (like a soil moisture probe) must survive **10+ years** on a coin-cell battery. This **10⁵× power consumption gap** cannot be bridged by simply using LTE — the **air-interface (PHY)** and **scheduling logic (MAC)** must be fundamentally redesigned. > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires students to understand the **physical-channel-level changes** introduced by 3GPP to address IoT constraints. The answer key typically awards marks for quoting specific bandwidth values, repetition counts, and subcarrier spacing values. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 PHY Layer Architecture: LTE-M vs NB-IoT The PHY layer in 3GPP MTC handles the **electromagnetic transmission of bits over the radio interface**. It is organized into **channels** (logical), **transport channels** (how data is multiplexed), and **physical channels** (the actual radio resources). ### 2.1.1 LTE-M (eMTC) — Physical Layer Specifics LTE-M operates within a **1.4 MHz** bandwidth — exactly **6 Resource Blocks (RBs)** — regardless of the wider system bandwidth. This is achieved by *restricting* the device to use only 6 RBs, even if the eNodeB broadcasts over 20 MHz. - **Downlink:** Reuses standard LTE **OFDMA** with normal **15 kHz** subcarrier spacing. - **Uplink:** Uses **SC-FDMA** with **15 kHz** subcarrier spacing (1 Resource Block = 12 subcarriers × 1 subframe = 180 kHz). - **Single Antenna** at the device (no MIMO) to reduce cost and power. - **Half-Duplex (HD) FDD** operation supported (Type B) — the device cannot transmit and receive simultaneously, saving a **duplexer filter** worth ~USD 1–2 in BoM cost. - **Reference signals:** Cell-specific Reference Signals (CRS) and Demodulation Reference Signals (DMRS) are both supported, but CRS is optional for MTC devices to save power. ### 2.1.2 NB-IoT — Physical Layer Specifics NB-IoT is a **radically different PHY**, occupying only **180 kHz** — either: 1. **In-band:** inside an LTE carrier's RBs (using one of the unused RBs), 2. **Guard-band:** in the unused guard band of an LTE carrier, or 3. **Standalone:** in refarmed GSM spectrum. - **Downlink:** OFDMA with **15 kHz** subcarrier spacing, using all 12 subcarriers of the single RB. - **Uplink:** Supports **two subcarrier spacing options**: - **3.75 kHz** (single subcarrier only) — for ultra-low-power, deep-coverage devices. - **15 kHz** (multi-tone, up to 12 subcarriers) — for higher throughput applications. - **Single-tone transmission** is the default to reduce Peak-to-Average Power Ratio (PAPR) and improve power amplifier efficiency. ## 2.2 Coverage Enhancement (CE) — The PHY/MAC Pillar ### 2.2.1 Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL) MCL is the **single most important metric** in 3GPP MTC. It represents the maximum path loss (in dB) the radio link can tolerate while still maintaining connectivity. $$ \text{MCL (dB)} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - P_{RX,\min} - \text{Implementation Margin} - \text{Fade Margin} $$ For comparison: - **Standard LTE:** MCL ≈ **140 dB** - **LTE-M (CE Mode A):** MCL ≈ **155.7 dB** - **LTE-M (CE Mode B):** MCL ≈ **156 dB** - **NB-IoT (CE Level 0):** MCL ≈ **164 dB** > [!NOTE] > The 20+ dB improvement in NB-IoT enables signals to penetrate **3 extra walls** or reach **basements** two floors deeper than standard LTE. ### 2.2.2 Repetition Coding (The Heart of CE) Repetition coding is the **simplest, most elegant** CE technique: **transmit the same information bits N times**, and the receiver combines them to extract the original data. This provides a processing gain of: $$ G_{\text{rep}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(N) \;\; \text{(dB)} $$ **KTU High-Yield Table — Repetition Gain:** | Repetition Count $N$ | Processing Gain $G_{\text{rep}}$ (dB) | Use Case | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0 dB | Normal coverage (CE Level 0) | | 8 | 9.03 dB | Light CE (Level 1) | | 32 | 15.05 dB | Moderate CE (Level 2) | | 128 | 21.07 dB | Deep CE (Level 3) | | 2048 | 33.11 dB | Extreme CE (NB-IoT only) | The **link-budget gain** from repetition is: $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{eff}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{per-rep}} + 10 \cdot \log_{10}(N) \;\; \text{(dB)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off:** More repetitions = better coverage BUT **higher latency** and **lower battery life**. NB-IoT allows up to **2048 repetitions on the NPUSCH**, which can stretch a single uplink transmission to **8+ seconds**. ### 2.3 Low Power Operation — PHY/MAC Level Mechanisms #### 2.3.1 Power Saving Mode (PSM) In PSM, the device **signals the network that it will be asleep for an extended duration** (e.g., days, weeks, months). The device: 1. Informs eNodeB/MME of an **Active Timer** (T3324) and **Periodic TAU Timer** (T3412). 2. Turns off **all radio circuitry** (PHY transmitter, receiver, oscillator). 3. Wakes up only at the pre-agreed TAU (Tracking Area Update) interval to check for downlink data. $$ \text{PSM Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{TAU}}} \times 100\% $$ For a soil sensor reporting once per day with 3 seconds of activity: $$ \text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{3 \text{ s}}{86400 \text{ s}} \times 100\% = 0.0035\% $$ This **99.9965% sleep time** is what enables the **10-year battery life** target. #### 2.3.2 Extended DRX (eDRX) In eDRX, the device sleeps for a **configured hyper-frame** duration (up to **10.24 seconds in LTE-M** and **~2.91 hours in NB-IoT**) between paging opportunities. It is a lighter sleep than PSM — the device still checks for paging, but only at extended intervals. $$ \text{eDRX Cycle}_{\text{NB-IoT}} = 2^k \times 10.24 \text{ s}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, 7 $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Processing gain from repetition | $G = 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | dB | $N$ = number of repetitions | | 2 | Effective SNR with repetition | $\text{SNR}_{\text{eff}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{rep}} + 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | dB | Linear repetition gain | | 3 | Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL) | $\text{MCL} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - P_{RX,\min} - M_{impl} - M_{fade}$ | dB | $M$ = margins | | 4 | NB-IoT Bandwidth | $B = 180$ kHz | Hz | Exactly 1 LTE RB | | 5 | LTE-M Bandwidth | $B = 1.4$ MHz | Hz | Exactly 6 LTE RBs | | 6 | Subcarrier spacing (NB-IoT UL) | $\Delta f \in \{3.75, 15\}$ kHz | Hz | 3.75 kHz = single-tone only | | 7 | Subcarrier spacing (LTE-M) | $\Delta f = 15$ kHz | Hz | Same as LTE | | 8 | Resource Block bandwidth | $B_{RB} = 12 \times \Delta f = 180$ kHz | Hz | 12 subcarriers per RB | | 9 | Symbol duration (with CP) | $T_s = \frac{1}{\Delta f} + T_{CP}$ | seconds | 66.67 µs + 4.69 µs (normal CP) | | 10 | PSM duty cycle | $\text{DC} = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{TAU}}} \times 100\%$ | % | For 10-year battery life | | 11 | eDRX cycle (NB-IoT) | $T_{\text{eDRX}} = 2^k \times 10.24$ s | seconds | $k \in [0,7]$, max ≈ 2.91 h | | 12 | PAPR (single-tone NB-IoT) | PAPR ≈ **0–3 dB** | dB | Vs 8–12 dB for multi-tone | | 13 | Max repetitions (NB-IoT UL) | $N_{\max} = 2048$ | — | For extreme CE | | 14 | Max repetitions (LTE-M) | $N_{\max} = 128$ | — | For both DL and UL | | 15 | Half-duplex FDD savings | $\text{BoM saving} \approx 1$–$2$ | USD | No duplexer filter needed | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Strategy:** When asked to compute processing gain, ALWAYS express the answer in **decibels (dB)** and explicitly state the trade-off (latency vs. coverage). KTU evaluators award 1 bonus mark for explicitly mentioning the trade-off. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The PHY/MAC optimizations in 3GPP MTC are not academic — they are deployed at **billion-device scale** by operators worldwide: - **Smart Metering:** 100+ million water/gas meters run on NB-IoT, with one uplink per day from basements. - **Asset Tracking:** LTE-M is preferred because it supports **mobility (handover)** for vehicle fleets. - **Agricultural IoT:** Soil sensors in remote fields use **NB-IoT CE Level 2 (32 repetitions)** to overcome rural path loss. - **Smart Parking:** Embedded NB-IoT sensors underground in concrete-covered parking lots — a real-world **MCL ≈ 164 dB** scenario. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Link-Budget Extension via Repetition Coding **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Question):** A soil-moisture sensor is deployed in a basement where the path loss is **160 dB**. Standard LTE requires a minimum SNR of **−7 dB** at the receiver. If the sensor's transmitter outputs **+23 dBm** and the receiver antenna gain is **2 dBi**, how many **uplink repetitions** are required to achieve NB-IoT coverage? (Implementation margin = 2 dB, Fade margin = 3 dB.) ### Step 1: Define Given Quantities $$ P_{TX} = +23 \text{ dBm}, \quad G_{TX} = 0 \text{ dBi (device)}, \quad G_{RX} = 2 \text{ dBi} $$ $$ \text{SNR}_{\min} = -7 \text{ dB}, \quad M_{impl} = 2 \text{ dB}, \quad M_{fade} = 3 \text{ dB} $$ $$ L_{\text{path}} = 160 \text{ dB}, \quad N_0 = -174 \text{ dBm/Hz}, \quad B = 180 \text{ kHz} $$ ### Step 2: Compute Receiver Sensitivity The thermal noise floor in the 180 kHz NB-IoT bandwidth is: $$ P_N = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(180 \times 10^3) = -174 + 52.55 = -121.45 \text{ dBm} $$ The minimum received signal power needed is: $$ P_{RX,\min} = P_N + \text{SNR}_{\min} = -121.45 + (-7) = -128.45 \text{ dBm} $$ ### Step 3: Compute MCL Without Repetition $$ \text{MCL}_{\text{std}} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - P_{RX,\min} - M_{impl} - M_{fade} $$ $$ \text{MCL}_{\text{std}} = 23 + 0 + 2 - (-128.45) - 2 - 3 = 148.45 \text{ dB} $$ ### Step 4: Compute Required Additional Processing Gain The path loss exceeds the standard MCL by: $$ \Delta G = L_{\text{path}} - \text{MCL}_{\text{std}} = 160 - 148.45 = 11.55 \text{ dB} $$ ### Step 5: Solve for Repetition Count Using the repetition-gain formula: $$ G_{\text{rep}} = 10 \log_{10}(N) = \Delta G = 11.55 \text{ dB} $$ $$ N = 10^{1.155} = 14.29 $$ ### Step 6: Apply the KTU Integer-Round-Up Rule Since repetitions must be a power-of-2 in 3GPP (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, ...), we **round up to the next valid value**. $$ N = 16 \text{ repetitions} $$ ### Step 7: Verify Final MCL $$ \text{MCL}_{\text{achieved}} = 148.45 + 10 \log_{10}(16) = 148.45 + 12.04 = 160.49 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{16 repetitions are sufficient to cover the 160 dB path loss.}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Battery Life Estimation for PSM-Enabled NB-IoT Sensor **Problem Statement:** An NB-IoT sensor draws **200 mA** during active transmission (lasting 3 s per day) and **5 µA** in deep sleep. The battery capacity is **5 Wh at 3.6 V** (≈ 1389 mAh). Estimate the battery life in years. ### Step 1: Compute Daily Energy Consumption Active mode current-time product per day: $$ Q_{\text{active}} = 200 \text{ mA} \times 3 \text{ s} = 600 \text{ mA·s} = \frac{600}{3600} \text{ mAh} = 0.1667 \text{ mAh} $$ Sleep mode current-time product per day (24 h = 86400 s): $$ Q_{\text{sleep}} = 5 \,\mu\text{A} \times 86400 \text{ s} = 0.432 \text{ mA·s} = 0.00012 \text{ mAh} $$ ### Step 2: Total Daily Charge Consumption $$ Q_{\text{daily}} = Q_{\text{active}} + Q_{\text{sleep}} = 0.1667 + 0.00012 \approx 0.1668 \text{ mAh/day} $$ ### Step 3: Compute Battery Life $$ \text{Battery Life} = \frac{Q_{\text{capacity}}}{Q_{\text{daily}}} = \frac{1389 \text{ mAh}}{0.1668 \text{ mAh/day}} \approx 8327 \text{ days} $$ $$ \text{Battery Life in years} = \frac{8327}{365.25} \approx 22.8 \text{ years} $$ ### Step 4: Apply Real-World Derating In practice, batteries self-discharge (~2–3% per year) and capacity derates with temperature. Apply a **70% derating factor**: $$ \text{Realistic Battery Life} = 22.8 \times 0.7 \approx 16 \text{ years} $$ This still comfortably exceeds the 3GPP target of **10 years** — proving that PSM-based NB-IoT designs meet the KTU syllabus's energy-efficiency goal. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: LTE-M vs NB-IoT | Parameter | **LTE-M (eMTC)** | **NB-IoT** | |---|---|---| | Bandwidth (DL/UL) | **1.4 MHz** (6 RB) | **180 kHz** (1 RB) | | DL Modulation | QPSK, 16-QAM | QPSK (up to 16-QAM in later releases) | | UL Modulation | QPSK, 16-QAM | BPSK, QPSK | | DL Subcarrier Spacing | **15 kHz** | **15 kHz** | | UL Subcarrier Spacing | 15 kHz | **3.75 kHz or 15 kHz** | | Max Coupling Loss | **156 dB** | **164 dB** | | Max Repetitions | 128 | 2048 | | Duplexing | Half or Full Duplex FDD, TDD | Half Duplex FDD (most common) | | Peak Data Rate (DL) | ~300 kbps (CE) / 1 Mbps (no CE) | ~26 kbps (multi-tone) / ~170 kbps (no CE) | | Peak Data Rate (UL) | ~375 kbps | ~62 kbps (multi-tone) | | Power Saving | PSM + eDRX | PSM + eDRX (longer) | | Mobility Support | **Full handover** (connected mode) | Cell reselection only (idle mode) | | Voice Support | **Yes (VoLTE)** | No | | Antenna | 1 RX (no MIMO) | 1 RX (no MIMO) | | Chip Cost (relative) | 1.2–1.5× LTE | 0.8–1.0× LTE | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PHY/MAC Layer Functional Architecture (3GPP MTC) The following Mermaid diagram maps the **block-level interaction** between PHY and MAC layer entities in a 3GPP MTC device, showing how data flows from the application layer down to the RF antenna and back. This is a high-fidelity functional block diagram suitable for the KTU board exam. ```mermaid flowchart TB appLayer["Application Layer - Sensor Data, App Packets"] ipLayer["IP or Non-IP Stack - IPv6, UDP, CoAP, MQTT"] rrcLayer["RRC Layer - Connection Management, System Info"] pdcpLayer["PDCP Layer - Ciphering, Header Compression"] rlcLayer["RLC Layer - ARQ, Segmentation, Reassembly"] macLayer["MAC Layer - HARQ with Repetitions, Scheduling, Random Access"] phyLayer["PHY Layer - Channel Coding, Modulation, OFDMA SC-FDMA, Repetition Coding"] rfFront["RF Front End - Antenna, Duplexer Half Duplex FDD, Power Amplifier"] antenna["Air Interface Uu to eNodeB / gNodeB"] harqBuf["HARQ Buffer - Up to 8 Processes with Repetition Combining"] ceModule["Coverage Enhancement Module - CE Level 0 to 3 Selection"] psmModule["PSM and eDRX Controller - Sleep Wake Cycle Manager"] prachMod["PRACH or NPRACH - Random Access with Repetitions"] syncMod["Synchronization - NPSS NSSS for NB-IoT, PSS SSS for LTE M"] syncMod -.-> phyLayer prachMod -.-> macLayer psmModule -.-> macLayer ceModule -.-> phyLayer harqBuf -.-> macLayer appLayer --> ipLayer --> rrcLayer --> pdcpLayer --> rlcLayer --> macLayer --> phyLayer --> rfFront --> antenna ``` ## 4.2 Repetition Coding & CE Level Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR dataIn["Input Transport Block"] modSel{"CE Level Selection
Based on RSRP"} lvl0["CE Level 0
1 Repetition"] lvl1["CE Level 1
2 to 8 Repetitions"] lvl2["CE Level 2
16 to 32 Repetitions"] lvl3["CE Level 3
64 to 128 LTE M
128 to 2048 NB IoT"] dataOut["Combined Output
Chase Combining Gain"] dataIn --> modSel modSel -->|RSRP > -90 dBm| lvl0 modSel -->|-90 to -100 dBm| lvl1 modSel -->|-100 to -110 dBm| lvl2 modSel -->|RSRP < -110 dBm| lvl3 lvl0 --> dataOut lvl1 --> dataOut lvl2 --> dataOut lvl3 --> dataOut ``` ## 4.3 PSM and eDRX Sleep-Wake Timeline ```mermaid flowchart LR t0["T0 - Active Transmission
3 seconds"] t1["T0 to T1 - Inactivity Timer T3324
10 seconds optional"] t2["T1 to T2 - PSM Deep Sleep
23 hours 59 minutes 47 seconds"] t3["T2 - TAU Tracking Area Update
Wake to check downlink"] t4["T2 to T3 - Back to PSM"] loop["Periodic TAU Cycle T3412
Configurable days to weeks"] psmVs["PSM Device is unreachable except at TAU"] edrxVs["eDRX Device listens only at eDRX paging window"] t0 --> t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> loop ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** In the KTU board exam, students often confuse **PSM** with **eDRX**. The key distinction is: **PSM = unreachable from the network** (the network buffers downlink data), whereas **eDRX = reachable at extended intervals** (the network can page the device every hyper-frame). Always draw the timeline and label the reachability state. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define Machine-Type Communication (MTC) and list any two PHY layer features standardized by 3GPP to support MTC.** **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80–100 words):** Machine-Type Communication (MTC), standardized by 3GPP in Release 13 onwards, refers to **data communication between IoT/M2M devices without human intervention**, primarily over cellular networks. Two key PHY layer features introduced to support MTC are: 1. **Narrowband Operation:** NB-IoT uses only **180 kHz** of bandwidth (1 LTE Resource Block), reducing device complexity and power consumption. 2. **Repetition Coding:** The same transmission is repeated up to **128 times (LTE-M)** or **2048 times (NB-IoT)** to provide **processing gain** of up to **33 dB**, enabling deep indoor coverage with MCL of **164 dB**. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip:** Always quote **specific numbers** (180 kHz, 128, 2048, 164 dB). KTU evaluators award the 3rd mark specifically for **quantitative data**. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between Power Saving Mode (PSM) and Extended DRX (eDRX) in 3GPP MTC.** **Model Answer (3 marks, tabular format preferred):** | Aspect | PSM | eDRX | |---|---|---| | **Device Reachability** | Unreachable from network during sleep | Reachable only at paging window | | **Typical Sleep Duration** | Hours to **weeks/months** | Up to **2.91 hours (NB-IoT) / 10.24 s (LTE-M)** | | **Power Saving** | Maximum (deepest sleep) | Moderate | | **Downlink Latency** | Very high (waits for next TAU) | Bounded by eDRX cycle | | **Use Case** | Periodic uplinks only (smart meter) | Both uplink and downlink needed (alarm panel) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain the Physical Layer (PHY) design choices in 3GPP **NB-IoT** with reference to bandwidth, subcarrier spacing, and modulation. Justify why NB-IoT uses single-tone transmission in the uplink. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A NB-IoT device is deployed in a basement. The path loss is **150 dB**, the device transmits at **+23 dBm**, the receiver antenna gain is **3 dBi**, and the minimum required SNR is **−7 dB**. The thermal noise floor is **−121 dBm** in the 180 kHz NB-IoT bandwidth. The implementation margin is **2 dB** and the fade margin is **2 dB**. Compute: (i) the receiver sensitivity, (ii) the achievable MCL without repetition, and (iii) the minimum number of repetitions needed to close the link. *(7 marks)* ### Model Answer to Question A #### Part (a) — PHY Design in NB-IoT (7 marks) The 3GPP Release 13 NB-IoT standard re-engineers the LTE PHY layer for **ultra-low-power, narrowband, deep-coverage** IoT. The key design choices are: **1. Bandwidth (1 mark):** NB-IoT occupies only **180 kHz** of spectrum, which is exactly **one LTE Resource Block (RB)**. This narrow bandwidth can be deployed: - *In-band* (inside an LTE carrier) - *Guard-band* (in the unused guard band of an LTE carrier) - *Standalone* (in refarmed GSM spectrum) **2. Subcarrier Spacing (2 marks):** - **Downlink:** 15 kHz subcarrier spacing (same as LTE), 12 subcarriers per RB. - **Uplink:** Two options are supported: - **15 kHz** multi-tone (up to 12 subcarriers) — higher throughput. - **3.75 kHz** single-tone (only 1 subcarrier) — lower PAPR, longer range, better for deep-coverage. **3. Modulation (2 marks):** - DL: **QPSK** (Release 13); 16-QAM introduced later for higher throughput. - UL: **BPSK** and **QPSK** (Release 13). Single-tone with BPSK is the most power-efficient choice. **4. Justification for Single-Tone UL (2 marks):** Single-tone uplink transmission is justified by: - **Lower PAPR (≈ 0–3 dB):** Single-tone signals have constant envelope, allowing the power amplifier to operate in its most efficient (saturated) region. Multi-tone SC-FDMA can have PAPR of **8–12 dB**, wasting 70% of battery as heat. - **Extended Range:** A 3.75 kHz subcarrier is **4× narrower** than LTE's 15 kHz, increasing symbol duration to ~266.67 µs and improving the **energy per bit (Eb/N0)**. - **Simpler RF frontend:** No need for complex multi-carrier signal processing, reducing chip cost and active power. --- #### Part (b) — Link-Budget Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** - $L_{\text{path}} = 150$ dB - $P_{TX} = +23$ dBm - $G_{RX} = 3$ dBi - $\text{SNR}_{\min} = -7$ dB - $P_N = -121$ dBm - $M_{impl} = 2$ dB, $M_{fade} = 2$ dB **Step 1: Receiver Sensitivity (2 marks)** $$ P_{RX,\min} = P_N + \text{SNR}_{\min} = -121 + (-7) = -128 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 2: Achievable MCL Without Repetition (2 marks)** $$ \text{MCL}_{\text{std}} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - P_{RX,\min} - M_{impl} - M_{fade} $$ $$ \text{MCL}_{\text{std}} = 23 + 0 + 3 - (-128) - 2 - 2 = 150 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3: Required Additional Gain (2 marks)** $$ \Delta G = L_{\text{path}} - \text{MCL}_{\text{std}} = 150 - 150 = 0 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 4: Number of Repetitions (1 mark)** $$ N = 10^{\Delta G / 10} = 10^{0/10} = 1 $$ $$ \boxed{N = 1 \text{ repetition (no repetition needed).}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes in Link-Budget Questions:** > 1. **Sign Error in Subtraction:** Students often write $P_{TX} - P_{RX,\min}$ as **$+128$** instead of **$-(-128) = +128$** — losing 1 mark. **Always** treat dBm values as algebraic quantities. > 2. **Forgetting the Antenna Gain:** $G_{RX}$ is often missed. The device antenna gain is usually **0 dBi** (omni-dipole); the base-station antenna gain is **+2 to +18 dBi** depending on sector configuration. > 3. **Rounding of Repetitions:** Even if the computed $N$ is fractional (e.g., 14.3), the answer must be the **next-higher power of 2** (i.e., 16). The 3GPP standard does not support arbitrary $N$ values. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **(a)** Describe the **Coverage Enhancement (CE)** mechanism in 3GPP MTC. Explain how **repetition coding** improves coverage and derive the processing gain expression. Mention the maximum repetition counts for both LTE-M and NB-IoT. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **Power Saving Mode (PSM)** in NB-IoT. An NB-IoT soil sensor operates in PSM with the following parameters: - Active mode current: 150 mA for 2 seconds per day - Sleep current: 3 µA - Battery: 2400 mAh at 3.6 V Compute the **battery life in years**, assuming a 70% derating factor. *(7 marks)* ### Model Answer to Question B #### Part (a) — Coverage Enhancement (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Coverage Enhancement (CE) is a set of PHY/MAC techniques standardized in 3GPP Releases 13–15 to **extend the maximum coupling loss (MCL)** from ~140 dB (standard LTE) to **156 dB (LTE-M)** and **164 dB (NB-IoT)**. **Repetition Coding (3 marks):** The same transport block is transmitted **N times** in succession, and the receiver uses **chase combining** to additively combine the energy of all N copies. The processing gain is: $$ G_{\text{rep}} = 10 \log_{10}(N) \;\; \text{dB} $$ For $N = 128$ repetitions (LTE-M maximum): $G_{\text{rep}} = 10 \log_{10}(128) = 21.07$ dB. For $N = 2048$ repetitions (NB-IoT maximum): $G_{\text{rep}} = 10 \log_{10}(2048) = 33.11$ dB. **CE Level Classification (2 marks):** - **CE Level 0:** 1 repetition (no CE) — normal coverage - **CE Level 1:** 2–8 repetitions — light CE - **CE Level 2:** 16–32 repetitions — moderate CE - **CE Level 3:** 64–128 (LTE-M) / 128–2048 (NB-IoT) — deep/extreme CE **Max Repetitions Summary (1 mark):** - LTE-M: **128** (DL: MPDCCH, UL: PUSCH) - NB-IoT: **128** (DL: NPDCCH/NPDSCH), **2048** (UL: NPUSCH) --- #### Part (b) — PSM and Battery Life (7 marks) **PSM Operation (3 marks):** Refer to the diagram below. In PSM, the device: 1. After active transmission, enters an **Inactivity Timer (T3324)** for fast wake-up. 2. After T3324 expiry, enters **deep sleep** — turning off RF, oscillator, and baseband. 3. Wakes up only at the **TAU (T3412)** cycle to register location and listen for paging. 4. Returns to deep sleep. The network **buffers downlink data** until the next TAU. **PSM Timeline Diagram (2 marks):** ``` |—Active Tx (2 s)—|—T3324 (10 s)—|———PSM Sleep (23h 59m 48s)———|—TAU Wake (1 s)—|—Back to PSM— <-----------------------------------------------------------------T3412 Cycle-----------------------------------------------------------------> ``` **Battery Life Calculation (2 marks):** Daily active charge: $$ Q_{\text{active}} = 150 \text{ mA} \times 2 \text{ s} = 300 \text{ mA·s} = 0.0833 \text{ mAh} $$ Daily sleep charge: $$ Q_{\text{sleep}} = 3 \,\mu\text{A} \times 86400 \text{ s} = 0.000072 \text{ mAh} $$ Total daily charge: $$ Q_{\text{daily}} = 0.0833 + 0.000072 \approx 0.0834 \text{ mAh/day} $$ Ideal battery life: $$ T_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{2400}{0.0834} \approx 28777 \text{ days} \approx 78.8 \text{ years} $$ With 70% derating: $$ \boxed{T_{\text{realistic}} = 78.8 \times 0.7 \approx 55.2 \text{ years}} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme):** - [ ] **3GPP MTC = LTE-M (eMTC) + NB-IoT (C-IoT)**. EC-GSM-IoT was the third option for GSM refarm. - [ ] **LTE-M Bandwidth = 1.4 MHz (6 RB)**; **NB-IoT Bandwidth = 180 kHz (1 RB)**. - [ ] **NB-IoT Subcarrier Spacing = 15 kHz (DL/multi-tone UL) and 3.75 kHz (single-tone UL)**. - [ ] **Single-tone NB-IoT UL uses BPSK/QPSK with low PAPR (≈ 0–3 dB)** for power efficiency. - [ ] **MCL: Standard LTE = 140 dB; LTE-M = 156 dB; NB-IoT = 164 dB.** - [ ] **Repetition coding gain = $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB.** Always round up to the next power-of-2. - [ ] **Max repetitions: LTE-M = 128; NB-IoT = 2048 (UL only).** - [ ] **PSM = deepest sleep, device unreachable; eDRX = reachable at paging windows.** - [ ] **PSM target battery life = 10 years** on a **5 Wh** battery. - [ ] **NB-IoT eDRX max cycle ≈ 2.91 hours; LTE-M eDRX max = 10.24 s.** - [ ] **Half-Duplex FDD** is supported in both LTE-M and NB-IoT to save the duplexer cost. - [ ] **MTC devices use 1 RX antenna (no MIMO)** to minimize BoM cost and current draw. - [ ] **MPDCCH (LTE-M) and NPDCCH (NB-IoT)** are the new control channels for MTC. - [ ] **NPRACH** in NB-IoT uses **single-tone** 3.75 kHz subcarriers with repetitions. - [ ] **Chase Combining** is the receiver-side technique that exploits repetitions. - [ ] **Trade-off in CE:** More repetitions → ↑ coverage, ↑ latency, ↓ battery life. - [ ] **NB-IoT supports only cell reselection** (no connected-mode handover); **LTE-M supports full handover** for mobility. - [ ] **PSM Active Timer = T3324; Periodic TAU Timer = T3412** (memorize these 3GPP timer names for 3-mark questions). > [!WARNING] > **Most-Common Mark-Losing Mistakes (from Past KTU Valuation Keys):** > 1. Confusing **MCL** (dB) with **SNR** (dB) — they are different quantities. > 2. Forgetting to **add the antenna gain** in the link-budget equation. > 3. Stating "**NB-IoT uses 200 kHz**" instead of **180 kHz** — wrong, loses marks. > 4. Saying "**PSM uses 128 repetitions**" — repetitions apply to data channels, not to PSM. > 5. Drawing **incomplete diagrams** without labelling all timers (T3324, T3412, eDRX cycle).

topics.title: IEEE 802.11
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT601 - IOT – ARCHITECTURE, PROTOCOLS AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IEEE 802.11

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# IEEE 802.11 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 802.11** is a family of standards developed by the **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)** that define the **Physical (PHY) layer** and **Medium Access Control (MAC) sub-layer** specifications for implementing **Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN)** communication. It is the technical foundation of commercially branded **Wi-Fi** technology and is administered by the **Wi-Fi Alliance** for interoperability certification.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 IoT syllabus**, IEEE 802.11 is treated as one of the principal **short-range, high-throughput wireless link-layer technologies** used to connect IoT end-nodes, gateways, and edge devices to local IP networks.

The standard operates in the **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz** unlicensed industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) radio bands, with later amendments (e.g., 802.11ah) targeting the **sub-1 GHz** band specifically for IoT and sensor networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — "Wi-Fi" vs "IEEE 802.11":** The IEEE 802.11 working group *defines* the standard. The Wi-Fi Alliance *certifies interoperability* of products against that standard. They are NOT the same entity, although the terms are colloquially interchangeable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Café Conversation" Model

Imagine a crowded café where many small groups are sitting at different tables, all speaking at the same time, but **without** a centralized microphone system.

- **The café** = the shared wireless medium (the air).
- **Each table group** = a **Basic Service Set (BSS)** — one Access Point (AP) and its associated client stations.
- **The unspoken rule "wait until no one is talking, then speak, and if two of you start at once, both stop and wait a random time"** = **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** — the MAC protocol of 802.11.
- **The café manager** = the **Access Point (AP)** — it announces the network, hands out "tickets" (beacons), and buffers traffic for sleeping devices.
- **The polite gesture "I am about to speak" (raising your hand)** = the **RTS/CTS handshake** — used to prevent the *hidden node problem* in wireless networks.

This analogy directly maps to how 802.11 coordinates multiple devices on a single shared radio channel.

## 1.3 The 802.11 Family Tree (Standards Evolution)

The KTU 2024 syllabus places strong emphasis on the evolution of PHY amendments. Memorize the following timeline:

| Standard | Year | Band | Max Data Rate | Modulation | KTU Relevance |
|----------|------|------|---------------|------------|----------------|
| **802.11 (legacy)** | 1997 | 2.4 GHz | **2 Mbps** | DSSS, FHSS | Historical baseline |
| **802.11a** | 1999 | 5 GHz | **54 Mbps** | OFDM | High-density indoor |
| **802.11b** | 1999 | 2.4 GHz | **11 Mbps** | DSSS | Long range IoT legacy |
| **802.11g** | 2003 | 2.4 GHz | **54 Mbps** | OFDM | Most deployed legacy |
| **802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)** | 2009 | 2.4/5 GHz | **600 Mbps** | MIMO-OFDM | Current IoT baseline |
| **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)** | 2013 | 5 GHz | **6.93 Gbps** | MU-MIMO | High-throughput gateways |
| **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E)** | 2019/2020 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | **9.6 Gbps** | OFDMA | Massive IoT deployments |
| **802.11ah (Wi-Fi HaLow)** | 2016 | **Sub-1 GHz** | **347 Mbps** | OFDM | **⭐ IoT-specific extension** |
| **802.11be (Wi-Fi 7)** | 2024 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | **46 Gbps** | 320 MHz, MLO | Future high-end |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The amendment **IEEE 802.11ah (Wi-Fi HaLow)** is explicitly called out in the IoT syllabus because it was *specifically designed* for long-range, low-power IoT — supporting up to **8191 clients per AP** with star topology over distances of **1 km+**.

## 1.4 Architectural Building Blocks

A wireless LAN built on IEEE 802.11 is described using a small, precise vocabulary:

- **STA (Station):** Any device containing an 802.11-conformant MAC and PHY interface (laptop, phone, sensor).
- **AP (Access Point):** A station that provides the relay function between the DS and the BSS — the "router" of Wi-Fi.
- **BSS (Basic Service Set):** The fundamental building block — a set of STAs controlled by a single AP.
- **IBSS (Independent BSS) / Ad-hoc Network:** A peer-to-peer BSS with **no AP** — STAs talk directly.
- **DS (Distribution System):** The backbone (usually Ethernet) that interconnects multiple BSSs.
- **ESS (Extended Service Set):** Two or more BSSs interconnected via a DS — what users see as a single "Wi-Fi network."
- **SSID (Service Set Identifier):** The human-readable network name (e.g., "KTU_Hostels_5G").

## 1.5 Why 802.11 Matters in IoT

In an IoT stack, IEEE 802.11 typically serves the **link / access layer**, sitting just above the physical radio and just below the IP network layer. It is preferred when:

- The IoT device needs **IP-native connectivity without extra gateway translation** (unlike Zigbee/BLE).
- Throughput requirements exceed those of low-rate LPWAN (LoRa, NB-IoT).
- The deployment is **indoor / short-range** (smart home, industrial automation, video surveillance).
- The IoT node has **mains power or frequent recharging** (because 802.11 is power-hungry).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# IEEE 802.11 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MAC Sub-Layer — Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)

The DCF is the **mandatory, contention-based** channel access mechanism. It is rooted in **CSMA/CA** to mitigate the impossibility of detecting collisions in a wireless medium (a transmitter cannot hear a remote transmitter *and* its own signal simultaneously — the "near-far problem").

### 2.1.1 Interframe Spaces (IFS) — The Layered Waiting System

Before any station transmits, it must wait for a specific, *standardized* quiet period. These intervals give priority to specific frame types. **Shorter IFS = higher priority.**

| IFS Name | Symbol | Relative Duration | Used By | KTU Priority Level |
|----------|--------|-------------------|---------|---------------------|
| **SIFS** | Short IFS | 10 µs (802.11b) | ACK, CTS, fragmented frames, Poll responses | **Highest** |
| **PIFS** | PCF IFS | 30 µs (802.11b) | PCF contention-free traffic | High |
| **DIFS** | DCF IFS | 50 µs (802.11b) | Normal data frames in DCF | Medium |
| **EIFS** | Extended IFS | SIFS + ACK_tx + DIFS | Error recovery | **Lowest** |

$$T_{DIFS} = T_{SIFS} + 2 \cdot T_{Slot}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "DIFS = SIFS + 2·Slot" ?** In 802.11b: SIFS = 10 µs and Slot = 20 µs, so DIFS = 10 + 2(20) = 50 µs. This arithmetic is a common KTU sub-part question.

### 2.1.2 Binary Exponential Backoff

After the channel is found busy during DIFS, the station **defers** and chooses a random backoff counter. The counter is drawn uniformly from:

$$CW = \text{uniform}[0,\ CW]$$

where $CW$ is the current **Contention Window**. After every *unsuccessful* transmission, the window doubles:

$$CW_{new} = \min\bigl((CW_{old} + 1) \cdot 2 - 1,\ CW_{max}\bigr)$$

For 802.11b:
- $CW_{min} = 31$ (i.e., values 0 to 31)
- $CW_{max} = 1023$

The backoff counter decrements by 1 after every idle slot time. When it reaches zero, the station transmits. This **BEB (Binary Exponential Backoff)** algorithm is what gives DCF its collision-spreading behavior under load.

### 2.1.3 RTS / CTS — Solving the Hidden Node Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hidden Node Problem:** Station A can reach AP, and station C can reach AP, but A and C are out of radio range of each other. Carrier-sensing at A fails to detect C's transmission, so they collide *at the AP*. This is a classic KTU 14-mark question scenario.

**Solution — The Four-Way Handshake (RTS/CTS / DATA / ACK):**

1. Sender → AP: **RTS** (Request To Send) — *reserves the medium.*
2. AP → all stations: **CTS** (Clear To Send) — *announces the reservation duration.*
3. Sender → AP: **DATA**
4. AP → Sender: **ACK**

The CTS contains a **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** timer — a virtual carrier-sense field — which freezes *all* stations (including the hidden node) for the duration of the upcoming exchange. This is the **single most-tested 802.11 mechanism** at KTU.

## 2.2 The PHY Layer — OFDM as the Dominant Modulation

From 802.11a/g onward, the PHY uses **Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)**, which splits the channel into many parallel narrowband subcarriers.

For 802.11a/g (20 MHz channel):
- **52 subcarriers** total: 48 data + 4 pilot
- Subcarrier spacing: $\Delta f = 312.5$ kHz
- Useful symbol duration: $T_{useful} = 3.2\ \mu s$
- Cyclic prefix (guard interval): $T_{cp} = 0.8\ \mu s$ (¼ of useful)
- Total OFDM symbol time: $T_{OFDM} = 4\ \mu s$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 312.5 kHz spacing?** It equals the inverse of the useful symbol time: $1/3.2\ \mu s = 312.5$ kHz. This orthogonality is the *defining property* of OFDM and is examinable.

### 2.2.1 Shannon Capacity Bound (the Theoretical Ceiling)

The maximum achievable data rate over an AWGN channel is given by Shannon-Hartley:

$$C = B \cdot \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \ \text{bits/sec}$$

where $B$ is bandwidth (Hz), $S$ is signal power, $N$ is noise power. For 802.11a at **SNR = 30 dB** in a 20 MHz channel:

$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1001) \approx 199.2\ \text{Mbps}$$

Real 802.11a achieves only **54 Mbps** because practical coding/modulation cannot reach the Shannon limit — a KTU-favorite observation point.

### 2.2.2 Free-Space Path Loss (Coverage Planning)

Used to estimate the receive power at distance $d$:

$$PL_{dB}(d) = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(d) + 20 \cdot \log_{10}(f) + 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

where $f$ is frequency in Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $d$ in meters.

**Shorter reference form for 2.4 GHz:**

$$PL_{dB}(d) \approx 40 + 20 \cdot \log_{10}(d)\ \text{[dB]}$$

For 5 GHz the first constant becomes ~46 dB. This explains the well-known rule: **higher frequency = shorter range** — a direct KTU short-answer item.

## 2.3 The 802.11 MAC Frame Format

The 802.11 MAC frame is a precise structure; the KTU 2024 syllabus tests your ability to draw and label it.

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|-------|--------------|---------|
| **Frame Control** | 2 | Type, subtype, ToDS, FromDS, retry, power mgmt |
| **Duration / ID** | 2 | NAV duration (µs) or association ID |
| **Address 1 (DA)** | 6 | Destination address (or RA in RTS) |
| **Address 2 (SA)** | 6 | Source address (or TA in RTS) |
| **Address 3** | 6 | BSSID, SA, or DA depending on To/FromDS |
| **Sequence Control** | 2 | Fragment number + sequence number |
| **Address 4** | 6 | Only used in WDS (4-address mode) |
| **Frame Body** | 0–2312 | MSDU payload |
| **FCS (CRC-32)** | 4 | Error detection |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid — "F-D-S-S-S-S-S-S-B-F":** FrameCtrl, Duration, SeqCtrl + the **4 Address fields**, Body, FCS. Memorize the field order — it is asked in 3-mark sub-parts almost every year.

## 2.4 Power Management in 802.11

IoT deployments are power-sensitive, so 802.11 defines two power states:

- **CAM (Continuously Aware Mode):** Station's radio is always on. High power, low latency.
- **PS (Power Save) Mode:** Station's radio sleeps; it wakes only at the **TBTT (Target Beacon Transmission Time)** to hear a beacon from the AP. Buffered frames are advertised via the **TIM (Traffic Indication Map)** in the beacon.

A related IoT amendment — **802.11ah (Wi-Fi HaLow)** — adds **TIM segmentation** and **restricted access window (RAW)** to scale up to 8191 clients.

## 2.5 Security Evolution (WEP → WPA3)

This is a top-3 KTU short-answer topic.

| Protocol | Year | Algorithm | Key Length | Status | KTU Point |
|----------|------|-----------|------------|--------|-----------|
| **WEP** | 1999 | RC4 stream cipher | 40 / 104 bit | **Broken (2001)** | Vulnerable to IV reuse |
| **WPA** | 2003 | RC4 + TKIP | 128 bit | Legacy | Transitional patch |
| **WPA2** | 2004 | AES-CCMP | 128 bit | Current standard | **Mandatory in modern 802.11** |
| **WPA3** | 2018 | AES-GCMP, SAE | 128 / 192 bit | Latest | Resistant to offline dictionary attack |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WPA3 key feature — SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals):** Replaces the 4-way PSK handshake with a **Dragonfly key exchange** that is resistant to offline dictionary attacks. This is the most-likely WPA3 question.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in Part A (3 marks) derivations and Part B (14 marks) sub-parts.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $T_{DIFS} = T_{SIFS} + 2 \cdot T_{Slot}$ | Interframe space relation | Timing diagrams |
| 2 | $CW_{new} = 2(CW_{old} + 1) - 1$ | Binary exponential backoff | Collision recovery |
| 3 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon capacity | Theoretical max rate |
| 4 | $PL_{dB} = 32.4 + 20\log d + 20\log f$ | Free-space path loss (km, MHz) | Range estimation |
| 5 | $T_{OFDM} = T_{useful} + T_{cp}$ | OFDM symbol time | PHY timing |
| 6 | $\Delta f = 1/T_{useful}$ | OFDM subcarrier spacing | 312.5 kHz in 802.11a/g |
| 7 | $N_{sub} = 52$ (802.11a/g) | Total subcarriers in 20 MHz | Data + pilot = 48+4 |
| 8 | $R_{max} = 54$ Mbps | Highest 802.11a/g rate (64-QAM, 3/4) | Modulation-coding |
| 9 | $NAV = \text{CTS duration} - \text{current time}$ | Virtual carrier sense | Hidden-node fix |
| 10 | $N_{clients} \le 8191$ (802.11ah) | Max clients per AP | IoT scalability |
| 11 | $EIFS = SIFS + ACK_{tx} + DIFS$ | Error recovery IFS | Frame-error handling |

> **Note on notation:** Absolute value is rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.7 Why 802.11 in Engineering / IoT

In real engineering deployments, 802.11 underpins:

- **Smart homes:** Wi-Fi is the dominant IP-stack bearer for cameras, plugs, speakers.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) with **OFDMA + BSS Coloring** allows hundreds of sensors to share a cell with deterministic latency.
- **Healthcare IoT:** 802.11 provides the bandwidth needed for real-time medical telemetry and imaging.
- **Smart agriculture:** 802.11ah HaLow reaches into fields where cellular coverage is poor and battery replacement is costly.
- **Edge gateways:** A Wi-Fi-equipped MCU (e.g., ESP32) is the simplest IP-node an engineer can prototype in a day.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# IEEE 802.11 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Backoff Time for a Single Transmission Attempt

**Problem.** A 802.11b station is initiating a transmission when the channel is idle. The current contention window is $CW = 31$. Compute the **expected backoff time** $E[BO]$ in microseconds.

### Step 1 — Identify the slot time

> For IEEE 802.11b, the standard slot time is fixed at:
> $$\sigma = T_{Slot} = 20\ \mu s$$

### Step 2 — Identify the backoff distribution

> The standard mandates a uniform distribution on integers in $[0,\ CW]$:
> $$BO \sim \text{Uniform}\{0, 1, 2, \dots, CW\}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the expected count

> The expected number of slot-times is the midpoint of the inclusive range:
> 
> $$E[BO_{slots}] = \frac{0 + CW}{2} = \frac{CW}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by slot duration

> $$E[BO_{time}] = E[BO_{slots}] \cdot \sigma = \frac{CW \cdot \sigma}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute numerical values

> For $CW = 31$ and $\sigma = 20\ \mu s$:
> 
> $$E[BO_{time}] = \frac{31 \cdot 20}{2} = 310\ \mu s$$

> **Result:** The average backoff delay for the first transmission attempt is **310 µs**.

### Step 6 — Generalize for retry r

> After $r$ failed retries, the contention window is:
> 
> $$CW_r = \min\bigl(2^{r+4} - 1,\ 1023\bigr)$$
> 
> and the average backoff becomes:
> 
> $$E[BO_r] = \frac{CW_r \cdot \sigma}{2} = \frac{2^{r+4} - 1}{2} \cdot 20\ \mu s$$

| Retry $r$ | $CW_r$ | $E[BO_r]$ (µs) |
|------------|--------|-----------------|
| 0 | 31 | 310 |
| 1 | 63 | 630 |
| 2 | 127 | 1270 |
| 3 | 255 | 2550 |
| 4 | 511 | 5110 |
| 5 | 1023 | 10230 |
| ≥6 | 1023 (capped) | 10230 |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Capacity vs. Practical 802.11a Throughput

**Problem.** Compare the Shannon limit with the practical 802.11a peak throughput, given:
- $B = 20$ MHz
- $\text{SNR} = 30$ dB  $\Rightarrow S/N = 10^{30/10} = 1000$

### Step 1 — Apply Shannon-Hartley

> $$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

> $$C = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000)$$
> $$C = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1001)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm

> $$\log_2(1001) = \frac{\ln(1001)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{6.908}{0.693} \approx 9.966$$

### Step 4 — Compute final capacity

> $$C \approx 20 \times 10^6 \cdot 9.966 \approx 199.3\ \text{Mbps}$$

### Step 5 — Compare with 802.11a specification

> The 802.11a/g standard advertises a **maximum physical-layer data rate of 54 Mbps** using **64-QAM** with a convolutional code rate of **3/4**.

### Step 6 — Compute the spectral efficiency ratio

> $$\eta = \frac{R_{practical}}{C_{Shannon}} = \frac{54}{199.3} \approx 0.271$$

> **Interpretation:** The 802.11a system achieves only **~27% of the Shannon limit** — a typical ratio for practical wireless systems of that era. Modern 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) with 1024-QAM in 160 MHz pushes this ratio above **70%**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Path Loss for an Outdoor 802.11 Link

**Problem.** An 802.11a AP at 5 GHz is mounted on a pole. A client station is located 200 m away, in line-of-sight. Compute the free-space path loss.

### Step 1 — Use the general FSPL formula

> $$PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the constant term (frequency-independent)

> $$20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right) = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right)$$
> $$= 20\log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{-8}) \approx -147.55$$

### Step 3 — Plug in d = 200 m and f = 5 GHz = $5 \times 10^9$ Hz

> $$20\log_{10}(200) = 46.02\ \text{dB}$$
> $$20\log_{10}(5 \times 10^9) = 20 \cdot (9 + \log_{10}5) = 20 \cdot 9.699 = 193.98\ \text{dB}$$

### Step 4 — Sum the three terms

> $$PL_{dB} = 46.02 + 193.98 - 147.55 = 92.45\ \text{dB}$$

> **Result:** Free-space path loss over 200 m at 5 GHz is approximately **92.5 dB**.

### Step 5 — Receive power estimation (optional, KTU bonus)

> If transmit power $P_t = 20$ dBm and AP/client antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 12$ dBi:
> 
> $$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL = 20 + 12 + 12 - 92.45 = -48.45\ \text{dBm}$$
> 
> The receiver sensitivity of 802.11a at 54 Mbps is approximately **−65 dBm**, so this link has a margin of ≈ 16.5 dB and works reliably.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — OFDM Parameter Validation for 802.11a/g

**Problem.** Prove that with 20 MHz channelization, the 802.11a/g OFDM subcarrier spacing is 312.5 kHz and there are 52 subcarriers in total.

### Step 1 — Recall the channel allocation

> The 20 MHz channel is divided into 64 sub-bands of equal width.

### Step 2 — Compute spacing

> $$\Delta f = \frac{20\ \text{MHz}}{64} = 312.5\ \text{kHz}$$

### Step 3 — Verify with useful symbol time

> $$T_{useful} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{312.5 \times 10^3} = 3.2\ \mu s$$

### Step 4 — Add cyclic prefix

> $$T_{cp} = \frac{1}{4} T_{useful} = 0.8\ \mu s$$

### Step 5 — Total OFDM symbol time

> $$T_{OFDM} = 3.2 + 0.8 = 4\ \mu s$$

### Step 6 — Identify used subcarriers

> Out of 64 sub-bands, the central DC is null and 11 are guard bands. Therefore:
> $$N_{used} = 64 - 12 = 52$$

### Step 7 — Sub-divide the 52

> $$N_{used} = N_{data} + N_{pilot} = 48 + 4$$

> **Conclusion:** 48 subcarriers carry data (modulated by 64-QAM symbols) and 4 are fixed pilots used for channel estimation and tracking of residual frequency offset.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — 802.11 Amendments

| Feature | 802.11b | 802.11a | 802.11g | 802.11n | 802.11ac | 802.11ax | 802.11ah |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|----------|----------|----------|
| **Band (GHz)** | 2.4 | 5 | 2.4 | 2.4 / 5 | 5 | 2.4/5/6 | <1 (e.g., 0.9) |
| **Channel BW (MHz)** | 22 | 20 | 20 | 20 / 40 | 20–160 | 20–160 | 1 / 2 / 4 / 8 / 16 |
| **Modulation peak** | DSSS-CCK | 64-QAM | 64-QAM | 64-QAM | 256-QAM | 1024-QAM | 256-QAM |
| **Max PHY rate (Mbps)** | 11 | 54 | 54 | 600 | 6933 | 9608 | 347 |
| **MIMO streams** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 4 | 8 | 8 | 4 |
| **Range (typical)** | ~35 m | ~25 m | ~35 m | ~70 m | ~35 m | ~30 m | **~1000 m** |
| **Max stations/AP** | ~32 | ~32 | ~32 | ~2007 | ~2007 | ~2007 | **8191** |
| **Multi-user OFDMA** | No | No | No | No | DL MU-MIMO | **Yes (UL+DL)** | No (but RAW) |
| **IoT Relevance** | Legacy | Indoor | High | Common | Gateways | Massive | **⭐ Native IoT** |

## 3.6 Total Transmission Time (DCAF) — Comprehensive Numerical Example

**Problem.** Compute the total DCF channel-access + transmission time for a 1500-byte MSDU using 802.11g at 54 Mbps, with RTS/CTS enabled.

### Step 1 — Frame components and their durations (802.11g, 802.11a-style timing)

| Component | Size | Time at 54 Mbps (µs) | Notes |
|-----------|------|----------------------|-------|
| DIFS | — | 34 | = SIFS(10) + 2·Slot(9) for OFDM |
| Average backoff (CW=15) | 7.5 slots | 7.5 × 9 = 67.5 | Slot=9 µs |
| RTS | 20 bytes | 20·8/54 ≈ 2.96 + PLCP preamble | ~ 24 µs with preamble |
| SIFS | — | 10 | |
| CTS | 14 bytes | ~ 24 µs with preamble | |
| SIFS | — | 10 | |
| DATA | 1500 B + 34 B MAC+IP+TCP+34B Fcs = ~1542 B | 1542·8/54 ≈ 228.4 | + PLCP ≈ 236 µs |
| SIFS | — | 10 | |
| ACK | 14 bytes | ~ 24 µs with preamble | |
| **Total** | | **≈ 380 µs** | |

### Step 2 — Throughput efficiency

> $$S = \frac{L_{payload}}{T_{total}} = \frac{1500 \cdot 8}{380 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 31.6\ \text{Mbps}$$

> The **goodput** is ~31.6 Mbps while the **PHY rate** is 54 Mbps → efficiency ≈ 58.5%. The remainder is consumed by overhead: DIFS, backoff, SIFS, RTS/CTS/ACK frames and preambles.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** This shows why the 802.11 MAC is considered *overhead-heavy* — only ~60% of the raw air-rate translates to user data, even in best case. This motivates the OFDMA + shorter preambles introduced in 802.11ax.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# IEEE 802.11 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The IEEE 802.11 Network Architecture (ESS / BSS / DS)

This diagram captures the canonical 802.11 topology — a must-draw for any 14-mark architecture question.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["STA 1<br/>Client Device"]
    nodeB["STA 2<br/>Client Device"]
    nodeC["STA 3<br/>Client Device"]
    nodeD["AP 1<br/>Access Point BSS 1"]
    nodeE["AP 2<br/>Access Point BSS 2"]
    nodeF["DS<br/>Distribution System Ethernet"]
    nodeG["Portal<br/>Gateway to Internet"]
    nodeH["IoT Sensor A<br/>STA 4"]
    nodeI["IoT Sensor B<br/>STA 5"]

    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeD --- nodeF
    nodeE --- nodeF
    nodeH --- nodeD
    nodeI --- nodeE
    nodeF --- nodeG

    %% Subgraph grouping
    subgraph BSS1["BSS 1 - Group 1"]
        nodeA
        nodeB
        nodeC
        nodeD
        nodeH
    end

    subgraph BSS2["BSS 2 - Group 2"]
        nodeE
        nodeI
    end

    subgraph Backbone["Distribution Backbone"]
        nodeF
        nodeG
    end
```

**How to read this in a KTU answer:**
- **BSS 1** is the radio cell of **AP 1**; the dashed circle in textbook drawings represents the **coverage area**.
- **DS** is the wired backbone that interconnects BSSs to form an **ESS**.
- **IoT Sensor A** is an IoT endpoint that has joined BSS 1 just like a laptop would.

## 4.2 DCF Channel-Access Timing Diagram (CSMA/CA + Backoff)

This timing sequence is the single most-asked 802.11 question in KTU Part A (3 marks) and often appears in Part B sub-questions.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant STA1 as Station A Sender
    participant CH as Wireless Channel
    participant STA2 as Station B Receiver
    participant AP as Access Point

    Note over CH: Channel is initially BUSY
    STA1->>CH: Carrier Sense BUSY
    Note over STA1: Defer transmission
    CH-->>STA1: Channel becomes IDLE
    STA1->>STA1: Wait DIFS interval
    STA1->>STA1: Choose backoff counter from CW
    Note over STA1: Backoff counter decrements per slot

    loop For each idle slot
        STA1->>STA1: Decrement backoff
    end

    STA1->>CH: Transmit RTS
    CH-->>STA2: RTS forwarded
    STA2->>CH: Transmit CTS after SIFS
    Note over STA1: Sender sees virtual NAV
    CH-->>STA1: CTS received
    STA1->>CH: Wait SIFS then Transmit DATA
    CH-->>AP: DATA forwarded
    AP->>CH: Wait SIFS then Transmit ACK
    Note over CH: Exchange complete, NAV resets
```

**Time-axis translation (for a drawn version in an exam):**
- DIFS precedes RTS.
- Backoff slots are random integers between 0 and CW.
- SIFS separates RTS → CTS, CTS → DATA, DATA → ACK.
- NAV is set by the Duration field in RTS/CTS to freeze other stations.

## 4.3 The 802.11 MAC Frame Structure (General Frame)

```mermaid
graph LR
    f1["Frame Control<br/>2 bytes"]
    f2["Duration or ID<br/>2 bytes"]
    f3["Address 1 DA<br/>6 bytes"]
    f4["Address 2 SA<br/>6 bytes"]
    f5["Address 3<br/>6 bytes"]
    f6["Sequence Control<br/>2 bytes"]
    f7["Address 4 Optional<br/>6 bytes"]
    f8["Frame Body MSDU<br/>0 to 2312 bytes"]
    f9["FCS CRC-32<br/>4 bytes"]

    f1 --> f2 --> f3 --> f4 --> f5 --> f6 --> f7 --> f8 --> f9
```

> [!TIP]
> **In the exam:** When asked to "draw and label the 802.11 MAC frame," draw this exact sequence of boxes. Mark "Address 4" with a dashed border and label it "**Only in WDS / 4-Address Mode**".

## 4.4 Hidden Node Problem and the RTS/CTS Solution

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Station A<br/>Sender"]
    nodeB["AP Access Point<br/>Central Receiver"]
    nodeC["Station C<br/>Hidden Node"]
    nodeR1["RTS broadcast"]
    nodeR2["CTS broadcast heard by A and C"]
    nodeR3["DATA only from A"]
    nodeR4["C freezes its NAV timer"]

    nodeA -- "Wireless range" --- nodeB
    nodeC -- "Wireless range" --- nodeB
    nodeA -. "Out of range" .-> nodeC

    nodeA -- "1 Send RTS" --> nodeR1
    nodeR1 -- "2 Heard by AP" --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "3 Send CTS to all" --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 -- "4 Hears CTS, sets NAV" --> nodeC
    nodeR2 -- "5 Hears CTS" --> nodeA
    nodeA -- "6 Sends DATA after SIFS" --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 -- "No collision at AP" --> nodeB
    nodeC -- "7 Stays silent" --> nodeR4
```

**Key takeaway for the diagram caption:** The CTS frame is broadcast at the AP's higher power and is heard by *all* stations in the BSS, including the hidden node C, which then defers for the duration of the entire DATA/ACK exchange.

## 4.5 802.11ah Wi-Fi HaLow IoT Network Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP["Wi-Fi HaLow AP<br/>Sub 1 GHz"]
    nodeS1["IoT Sensor Group A<br/>Soil moisture 50 nodes"]
    nodeS2["IoT Sensor Group B<br/>Temperature 50 nodes"]
    nodeS3["IoT Sensor Group C<br/>Battery meter 50 nodes"]
    nodeR1["RAW Slot 1"]
    nodeR2["RAW Slot 2"]
    nodeR3["RAW Slot 3"]
    nodeGW["Cloud Gateway"]

    nodeP -- "Up to 1 km line of sight" --> nodeS1
    nodeP -- "Up to 1 km line of sight" --> nodeS2
    nodeP -- "Up to 1 km line of sight" --> nodeS3

    nodeS1 -- "Restricted Access Window" --> nodeR1
    nodeS2 -- "Restricted Access Window" --> nodeR2
    nodeS3 -- "Restricted Access Window" --> nodeR3

    nodeR1 -- "TIM segmented beacons" --> nodeP
    nodeR2 -- "TIM segmented beacons" --> nodeP
    nodeR3 -- "TIM segmented beacons" --> nodeP
    nodeP -- "Backhaul link" --> nodeGW
```

**Reading guide:** The 802.11ah AP uses **Restricted Access Windows (RAW)** to time-slice the channel among thousands of sensors. **TIM segmentation** allows the AP to direct individual stations to listen only to their specific beacon slice, drastically reducing wake-time energy.

## 4.6 Power-Save (PS) Mode Beacon / Wake Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant STA as IoT Station Power Save
    participant AP as Access Point
    participant BUF as AP Buffer

    Note over AP: Time axis, TBTT marked
    AP-->>STA: Beacon with TIM bit set
    Note over STA: STA wakes only at TBTT
    STA-->>AP: PS-Poll frame
    AP-->>BUF: Retrieve buffered frame
    AP-->>STA: DATA frame
    STA-->>AP: ACK
    Note over STA: Returns to sleep until next TBTT
```

**Engineering insight:** This is the core power-management mechanism in 802.11 IoT deployments. The IoT node is awake for only a few milliseconds per beacon interval (typically 100 ms), keeping average power draw in the µA range.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
