It operates primarily in the sub-1 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio frequency bands (typically around **908.42 MHz** in North America and **868.42 MHz** in Europe/India) and is governed by the **Z-Wave Alliance** (formerly Zensys / Sigma Designs / Silicon Labs). Z-Wave was designed from the ground up as a **closed, interoperable, low-data-rate control protocol** for residential and light commercial automation — covering lighting, HVAC, security, access control, and appliance control — as opposed to high-bandwidth protocols like Wi-Fi or BLE. > [!NOTE] > **Key Slogan (Industry Standard):** *Z-Wave is "the smart home's wireless nervous system."* ## 1.2 Key Technical Specifications (High-Yield) | Parameter | Specification | | :--- | :--- | | **Frequency Band (ISM)** | **908.42 MHz** (US), **868.42 MHz** (EU/India), 921.42 MHz (AU) | | **Modulation** | Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (**GFSK**) | | **Data Rate** | **9.6 kbps** (legacy, R1), **40 kbps** (R2), **100 kbps** (R3, 4x) | | **Range** | ~30 m indoor, ~100 m outdoor (line of sight) | | **Topology** | **Mesh** (up to **232 nodes** per network) | | **Encryption** | **AES-128** (mandatory from Z-Wave Plus / 500-series onward) | | **Max Hops** | **4 hops** between source and destination | | **Channels (EU/India)** | 1 primary channel (868.42 MHz) | | **Channels (US)** | 1 primary channel (908.42 MHz) | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postman in a Housing Complex" > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a large gated housing community (your smart home). The post office (controller/hub) wants to deliver a letter (command) to House 42 (a smart bulb in the basement). The postman doesn't know the basement, so he hands the letter to House 15, who hands it to House 23, who finally hands it to House 42. Every house that *passes along* a letter is acting as a **repeater/router**. This is exactly how Z-Wave forms a **mesh** — every mains-powered device relays messages, ensuring the command reaches even the most distant or RF-shadowed device. **Key takeaway from the analogy:** - The **hub** = the primary controller. - The **smart bulb at the far end** = an "end device" that cannot reach the hub directly. - **Houses that pass the letter** = routing nodes (repeaters). - The **letter** = a Z-Wave frame. - **Security** = a sealed envelope with an AES-128 lock. ## 1.4 Why Sub-1 GHz? (KTU Board Favourite Question) Z-Wave deliberately avoids the crowded 2.4 GHz band (used by Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, microwaves) for three engineering reasons: 1. **Better propagation** — Lower frequencies diffract around walls and furniture more efficiently. 2. **Lower attenuation through obstacles** — Brick, concrete, and plaster attenuate sub-1 GHz signals less than 2.4 GHz. 3. **Less interference** — The 2.4 GHz band is saturated; sub-1 GHz is comparatively quiet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** Always remember — Zigbee uses 2.4 GHz (worldwide), Z-Wave uses sub-1 GHz (region-specific). This is the single most-asked comparison point in KTU exams for this module. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Z-Wave Network Architecture — The Three Logical Device Types A Z-Wave network has a strict hierarchical model with **three logical device classes**: ### (a) Primary Controller - **One per network** (by design). - Holds the network's **Home ID** and the topology table. - Initiates "Inclusion" (adding devices) and "Exclusion" (removing devices). - Stores the last known route to every node in the network. ### (b) Secondary Controller - An *additional* controller (e.g., a portable remote or a voice-assistant bridge). - Can control devices and replicate the network topology from the primary. - Used for multi-room control or as a backup. ### (c) Slave Nodes (End Devices) - The actual sensors and actuators (light dimmer, door lock, thermostat, PIR sensor). - Can be either: - **Routing Slave** (mains-powered, can repeat frames for others). - **Non-Routing Slave / "FLiRS"** (battery-powered, uses **Frequently Listening Receiver Slot** — wakes briefly every ~1 s to listen for a "beam" signal, then sleeps). FLiRS devices **cannot** repeat frames. > [!NOTE] > **FLiRS = Frequently Listening Receiver Slave.** These are the sleepy, battery-powered devices (door sensors, window contacts) that wake up just long enough to hear "any mail for me?" and go back to sleep to save power. ## 2.2 Z-Wave Protocol Stack (OSI Mapping) | OSI Layer | Z-Wave Implementation | | :--- | :--- | | **7 – Application** | Device Class Commands (e.g., `BASIC SET`, `SWITCH BINARY`, `METER GET`) | | **6 – Presentation** | Device Classes, Command Class encapsulation | | **5 – Session** | *Not explicitly separated* | | **4 – Transport** | Singlecast, Multicast, Broadcast frame addressing | | **3 – Network** | Routing, Explorer frames, last-working-route cache | | **2 – Data Link** | MAC — CSMA/CA, frame structure, ACK, HomeID/NodeID | | **1 – Physical** | GFSK modulation, sub-1 GHz RF, Manchester encoding (legacy) | ## 2.3 Z-Wave Frame Structure (Deep Dive) A Z-Wave frame consists of the following byte sequence (R1/R2 framing): | Field | Size (Bytes) | Purpose | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **Preamble** | 8 bytes | Synchronization (alternating 0x55 / 0xAA pattern) | | **Start of Frame (SoF)** | 1 byte | **0x01** for standard frames | | **Length** | 1 byte | Length of the payload | | **Header (Request/Response)** | 1 byte | R/R bit, Sequence number | | **HomeID** | 4 bytes | Unique network identifier (assigned by primary controller) | | **Source NodeID** | 1 byte | Sender's address (1–232) | | **Frame Header (R1)** | 2 bytes | Frame Control, Length, Destination NodeID, etc. | | **Application Payload** | n bytes | Encapsulated Command Class data | | **Checksum** | 1 byte | XOR-based integrity check (length + header + payload) | | **End of Frame (EoF)** | 1 byte | **0x03** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For Z-Wave Plus / 500-series (ZWP frames)**, the framing is wrapped in a **2-byte Header (HDR)** + **2-byte HomeID** is preserved, but additional fields like **Speed-Modified CRC**, **Security 2 (S2) encapsulation**, and **Explorer HomeID (0x00000000)** appear. ## 2.4 Routing & Topology Management Z-Wave implements a **Source Routing** scheme with adaptive learning: 1. **Initial route discovery** — The controller sends a **Explorer Frame** flooded through the mesh. 2. Every routing node appends its NodeID to the route list and rebroadcasts. 3. The destination responds with a **Return Route Frame** containing the full path. 4. The controller caches this as the **Last Working Route (LWR)**. 5. On subsequent transmissions, the controller uses the cached route — saving significant latency. 6. If the LWR fails (3 retries with no ACK), the controller falls back to a new Explorer Frame. > [!NOTE] > **Max Hop Count = 4.** A frame can be relayed by at most 4 intermediate routing slaves. With 232 nodes and 4 hops, the theoretical Z-Wave network can cover a very large residential installation. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Math Quick-Reference (the only quantitative model required in KTU for this topic):** | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Time on Air (R1, 9.6 kbps)** | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{L_{frame} \times 8}{96000}$ | seconds; $L_{frame}$ in bits | | **Time on Air (R2, 40 kbps)** | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{L_{frame} \times 8}{40000}$ | seconds | | **Network Capacity (Max Nodes)** | $N_{max} = 232$ | hard-coded in protocol | | **Max Hop Count** | $H_{max} = 4$ | limits path length | | **Max Logical Routes cached per node** | $R_{cache} = 5$ | (controller) | | **FLiRS Wake Interval** | $T_{wake} \approx 1000 \text{ ms}$ | battery save duty cycle | | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL \; (\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | d, f in km & MHz — at 908.42 MHz | | **Effective Range Derivation** | $d_{max} = 10^{\tfrac{FSPL - P_t + G_t + G_r - S_{min}}{20}} \times \dfrac{1}{f_{MHz}}$ | practical indoor ~30 m | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Home Hubs:** Samsung SmartThings, Aeotec, Fibaro, Vera — use Z-Wave radios. - **Insurance Discounts:** Many insurers (US/EU) give 5–15 % discounts on home insurance for Z-Wave-certified leak/motion sensors. - **Hospitality Industry:** Hotels (Marriott, Hilton) use Z-Wave for keyless entry, lighting, and HVAC control. - **Energy Harvesting:** Z-Wave's low duty cycle enables energy-harvesting devices (e.g., battery-less window sensors using miniature solar cells). ## 2.7 Z-Wave Plus (ZWP / 500-series) — Enhancements | Feature | Classic Z-Wave (100/200-series) | Z-Wave Plus (500-series+) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Encryption** | Optional AES-128 | Mandatory AES-128 | | **Routing** | Explorer + LWR | LWR++ + Network Wide Inclusion | | **Beam Frames** | Not supported | **Beam Frames** for FLiRS wake-up | | **Frame Exploration** | Yes | Yes, but with reduced overhead | | **Power Budget Reporting** | No | Yes (battery devices report levels) | | **Network Health** | Limited | CC "Supervision" + "Notification" reporting | > [!NOTE] > **Z-Wave 700-series (2019)** added **S2 Security**, **SmartStart** (QR-code based inclusion), and 100 kbps over-the-air speed. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Air-Time vs. Frame Size at 9.6 kbps vs. 40 kbps Z-Wave's effective throughput is dominated by the **Time-on-Air (ToA)** of a single frame. The KTU board often asks students to compare R1 vs. R2 transmission times. Below is a complete worked derivation. **Problem (KTU-style):** A Z-Wave controller transmits a 40-byte frame to a routing slave. Compare the air-time if the network operates in R1 (9.6 kbps) mode versus R2 (40 kbps) mode. Assume 2-byte preamble, 1-byte SoF, 1-byte length, 1-byte header, 4-byte HomeID, 1-byte source, 2-byte frame header, 1-byte checksum, 1-byte EoF are *overhead* on top of the 40-byte payload. ### Step 1 — Total Frame Length The total number of bytes on the air-interface is the sum of the payload plus the protocol overhead: $$ L_{total} \; = \; L_{payload} \; + \; L_{overhead} $$ Let $L_{payload} = 40$ bytes, and $$ L_{overhead} = 2_{preamble} + 1_{SoF} + 1_{len} + 1_{hdr} + 4_{HomeID} + 1_{src} + 2_{frmHDR} + 1_{cs} + 1_{EoF} = 14 \text{ bytes} $$ Therefore: $$ L_{total} = 40 + 14 = 54 \text{ bytes} $$ ### Step 2 — Convert to Bits Each byte is 8 bits: $$ N_{bits} = 54 \times 8 = 432 \text{ bits} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Time-on-Air at R1 (9.6 kbps) The time-on-air is bits divided by bit-rate: $$ T_{R1} = \frac{N_{bits}}{R_1} = \frac{432}{9600} \text{ seconds} $$ $$ T_{R1} = 0.045 \text{ s} = 45 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Time-on-Air at R2 (40 kbps) $$ T_{R2} = \frac{N_{bits}}{R_2} = \frac{432}{40000} \text{ seconds} $$ $$ T_{R2} = 0.0108 \text{ s} = 10.8 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 5 — Speed-up Ratio $$ \eta = \frac{T_{R1}}{T_{R2}} = \frac{45}{10.8} \approx 4.17 \times $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** R2 is ~4.17× faster than R1, but **still not enough for high-bandwidth media** (audio/video). Z-Wave is fundamentally a **control protocol**, not a streaming protocol. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Z-Wave vs. Zigbee vs. Wi-Fi vs. BLE This matrix is a **KTU high-yield "must-memorize" table** for any comparison question: | Parameter | **Z-Wave** | Zigbee | Wi-Fi (802.11n) | BLE (5.0) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Frequency** | Sub-1 GHz (868/908 MHz) | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | | **Data Rate** | 9.6 / 40 / 100 kbps | 250 kbps | 72–600 Mbps | 1–2 Mbps | | **Topology** | Mesh (232 nodes) | Mesh (65k) | Star | Star / Mesh | | **Modulation** | GFSK | O-QPSK | OFDM / DSSS | GFSK | | **Range (Indoor)** | ~30 m | ~10–20 m | ~35 m | ~10 m | | **Power** | Very low (FLiRS) | Low | High | Very low | | **Encryption** | AES-128 (S2) | AES-128 | WPA2/WPA3 | AES-128 | | **Application** | Home automation | Industrial / Home | Internet | Wearables / Audio | | **Standard Body** | Z-Wave Alliance | IEEE 802.15.4 + Zigbee Alliance | IEEE 802.11 | Bluetooth SIG | | **Max Hops** | 4 | Many (15+) | N/A (1) | N/A (1) | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Z-Wave Command Classes (Sample) > [!NOTE] > **Command Class** is Z-Wave's equivalent of Zigbee's Cluster. Each CC defines a set of commands (GET/SET/REPORT) for a specific functionality. | Command Class (Hex) | Name | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `0x20` | **BASIC** | On/Off and level control | | `0x25` | **SWITCH BINARY** | Binary on/off (relay) | | `0x26` | **SWITCH MULTILEVEL** | Dimmer / motor control | | `0x30` | **SENSOR BINARY** | PIR / door sensor | | `0x31` | **SENSOR MULTILEVEL** | Temperature, humidity, lux | | `0x40` | **THERMOSTAT MODE** | HVAC control | | `0x70` | **CONFIGURATION** | Vendor-specific parameters | | `0x98` | **SECURITY** | Legacy security encapsulation | | `0x9F` | **SECURITY 2 (S2)** | Modern S2 encapsulation | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Inclusion Procedure (Network Formation) A KTU favourite sub-question is to *describe* how a new device joins a Z-Wave network. The full procedure is: 1. **User puts primary controller in "Learn Mode"** (button press / app). 2. **User triggers "Inclusion" on the new device** (usually a triple-tap of a button). 3. The new device broadcasts a **Node Information Frame (NIF)**. 4. The controller assigns a fresh **NodeID** (1–232) and broadcasts the new **HomeID**. 5. The new device stores the HomeID in non-volatile memory and replies with ACK. 6. The controller updates the **routing table** with the new node. 7. The new device's **Command Classes** (capabilities) are read using the `NIF` so the UI can render appropriate controls. > [!TIP] > **"Inclusion" = Add, "Exclusion" = Remove. "Learn Mode" = factory reset + pair.** Memorize this triad. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Z-Wave Mesh Network Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Primary Controller
HomeID: 0xC0A80101
NodeID: 01] --> B[Routing Slave 1
Smart Light Switch
NodeID: 02] A --> C[Routing Slave 2
HVAC Thermostat
NodeID: 03] B --> D[Routing Slave 3
Smart Plug
NodeID: 04] C --> D C --> E[FLiRS End Device
Door Sensor
NodeID: 05] D --> F[Non-Routing End Device
Battery PIR
NodeID: 06] D --> G[Routing Slave 4
Window Shade
NodeID: 07] G --> H[FLiRS End Device
Leak Sensor
NodeID: 08] B -.Ack and Repeat.-> I[MESH ROUTING PATH
Hop 1 of max 4] C -.Ack and Repeat.-> I D -.Ack and Repeat.-> I G -.Ack and Repeat.-> I ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **A** is the primary controller. - **B, C, D, G** are routing slaves (mains-powered; repeat frames). - **E, F, H** are end devices. **E and H** are FLiRS (sleepy/battery). **F** is a non-routing battery device. - Solid arrows = direct RF link. Dotted arrows = mesh repeating path. ## 4.2 Z-Wave Protocol Stack Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TD L7[Application Layer
Command Classes BASIC SWITCH METER] --> L6[Presentation Layer
Device Class Encapsulation] L6 --> L4[Transport Layer
Singlecast Multicast Broadcast] L4 --> L3[Network Layer
Routing Explorer LWR Cache] L3 --> L2[Data Link Layer MAC
HomeID NodeID CSMA-CA ACK] L2 --> L1[Physical Layer
GFSK Modulation 908.42 MHz Sub-1 GHz] ``` ## 4.3 Z-Wave Frame Transmission Flow (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD A[Application Command] --> B[Command Class Encapsulation] B --> C{Security Class?} C -->|None| D[Plain Frame Assembly] C -->|S2| E[AES-128 Encryption with Network Key] C -->|Legacy S0| F[AES-128 Encryption with Encrypted Key] D --> G[MAC Header: HomeID SourceNodeID DestinationNodeID] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Checksum Computation XOR] H --> I[GFSK Modulation at 908.42 MHz] I --> J[RF Transmission over the Air] J --> K[Receiver: CSMA-CA Channel Check] K --> L[Frame Decoded and Verified] L --> M{ACK Required?} M -->|Yes| N[Transmit ACK to Source] M -->|No| O[Process Application Command] N --> O ``` ## 4.4 Inclusion Procedure State Diagram ```mermaid graph TD S1[IDLE: Controller in Normal Mode] -->|User Presses Button| S2[Controller enters LEARN MODE
Broadcasts INCLUDE_REQUEST] S2 --> S3{New Device Triple-Tap Detected?} S3 -->|No| S2 S3 -->|Yes| S4[New Device sends NODE_INFORMATION_FRAME] S4 --> S5[Controller assigns NodeID and HomeID] S5 --> S6[New Device stores HomeID in NVM and ACKs] S6 --> S7[Controller updates Routing Table] S7 --> S8[INCLUSION COMPLETE: Network is updated] S1 -.User triggers EXCLUDE.-> S9[Controller enters EXCLUDE MODE] S9 --> S10[Target device acknowledges removal] S10 --> S1 ``` > [!TIP] > **Diagram Interpretation for KTU:** When asked to "draw" or "explain" the Z-Wave network, always include: (1) one primary controller, (2) at least two routing slaves, (3) at least one FLiRS end device, and (4) show the multi-hop path with arrows. This is what KTU examiners expect. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **List any three features of the Z-Wave protocol.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. Z-Wave operates in the **sub-1 GHz ISM band** (908.42 MHz in the US, 868.42 MHz in EU/India) and uses **GFSK modulation** with a maximum data rate of **100 kbps** (Z-Wave 700-series) or **40 kbps** (R2). 2. It supports a **mesh topology** with up to **232 nodes** per network and a maximum of **4 hops** between source and destination. 3. It provides **AES-128 encryption** (mandatory from Z-Wave Plus) via the **S2 Security** class, ensuring confidentiality and integrity of frames. 4. (Bonus mark) It supports **FLiRS** (Frequently Listening Receiver Slaves) for ultra-low-power battery devices such as door/window sensors. > [!TIP] > Award 1 mark per feature. Total 3 marks. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between a routing slave and a FLiRS device in Z-Wave.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | **Routing Slave** | **FLiRS Device** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Power** | Mains-powered (always on) | Battery-powered (sleeps) | | **Can repeat frames?** | Yes | **No** — cannot repeat | | **Wake-up** | Always listening | Wakes every ~1000 ms to listen for "beam" | | **Role** | Acts as a repeater in the mesh | End-point sensor (door, PIR) | > Award 1 mark per relevant row, total 3 marks. Mentioning "beam frames" gets a bonus. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE-Style Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. There is internal choice: you must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` #### (a) Describe the Z-Wave protocol stack with reference to the OSI model. Briefly explain the responsibilities of the MAC layer. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The Z-Wave protocol stack is a layered architecture that maps to a subset of the OSI 7-layer model. The mapping is as follows: 1. **Physical Layer (Layer 1):** Defines the RF characteristics — sub-1 GHz frequency band, GFSK modulation, channel selection, transmit power, and bit-encoding (Manchester in legacy, NRZ in 700-series). The physical layer is responsible for converting digital bits into RF energy and vice versa. 2. **Data Link / MAC Layer (Layer 2):** This is the most critical layer for Z-Wave. Its responsibilities include: - **Frame assembly / disassembly** (Preamble, SoF, Length, Header, Payload, Checksum, EoF). - **Addressing** using the 4-byte **HomeID** (network identifier) and 1-byte **NodeID** (1–232). - **CSMA/CA channel access** — the node senses the medium before transmitting to avoid collisions. - **ACK generation** — receivers send a short acknowledgement frame for reliable delivery. - **Encryption encapsulation** — the MAC layer wraps application data in S2 security headers. - **Checksum** — XOR-based integrity check (CRC-8 in newer revisions). 3. **Network Layer (Layer 3):** Handles **routing**, **explorer frame flooding**, **Last Working Route (LWR)** caching, and **hop-count management** (max 4). 4. **Transport Layer (Layer 4):** Provides **singlecast, multicast, and broadcast** addressing semantics. 5. **Application / Presentation Layers (Layers 5–7):** Encapsulate **Command Classes** (BASIC, SWITCH BINARY, METER, CONFIGURATION, etc.) and the device-class-specific data model. This is where user-facing commands live. > Award 1 mark per correct layer explanation; 2 marks specifically for MAC-layer responsibilities. #### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the Z-Wave mesh network topology. Discuss how routing is performed in Z-Wave. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *(Draw the mesh diagram as in Section 4.1 above.)* A Z-Wave mesh network is composed of: - **One Primary Controller** (the hub, e.g., SmartThings). - **Multiple Routing Slaves** (mains-powered devices that can repeat). - **End Devices** — either **FLiRS** (battery, wake every ~1 s) or **non-routing** (e.g., wall-wart devices that don't repeat). **Routing procedure:** 1. **Route discovery** — When a controller wants to talk to a destination whose route is unknown, it broadcasts an **Explorer Frame** (HomeID = 0x00000000, special destination). 2. Every routing node **appends its NodeID** to the route and rebroadcasts. 3. The **destination** returns a **Return Route Frame** containing the full path. 4. The controller **caches** this as the **Last Working Route (LWR)** in its routing table. 5. On subsequent transmissions, the controller uses the **cached LWR** directly — drastically reducing latency and air-time consumption. 6. If the destination **fails to ACK 3 times**, the controller invalidates the LWR and re-initiates an Explorer Frame. > Award 2 marks for the diagram, 3 marks for the routing steps, 2 marks for the LWR/failure mechanism. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` #### (a) Compare Z-Wave and Zigbee protocols on the following parameters: frequency band, data rate, topology, number of nodes, encryption, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | **Z-Wave** | **Zigbee** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Frequency Band** | Sub-1 GHz — 868/908 MHz | 2.4 GHz (worldwide) | | **Data Rate** | 9.6 / 40 / 100 kbps | 250 kbps | | **Topology** | Mesh (max 232 nodes) | Mesh (max 65 535 nodes) | | **Max Hops** | 4 | 15+ | | **Modulation** | GFSK | O-QPSK | | **Encryption** | AES-128 (S2) | AES-128 (CCM mode) | | **Range (Indoor)** | ~30 m | ~10–20 m | | **Typical Applications** | Residential smart home | Industrial + smart home | | **Standard Body** | Z-Wave Alliance (proprietary) | IEEE 802.15.4 + Zigbee Alliance | > Award 1 mark per correctly filled row (use any 6 rows for 6 marks); 1 mark for the concluding statement. **Conclusion:** Z-Wave is preferred in **residential** settings due to its longer range and lower interference in the sub-1 GHz band. Zigbee is preferred in **industrial** settings due to its higher data rate, larger node count, and open standard nature. #### (b) Explain the Z-Wave frame structure. Mention the fields HomeID, NodeID, and Checksum in detail. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A Z-Wave frame (R1/R2) is a serial byte-stream with the following structure: 1. **Preamble (8 bytes)** — Alternating pattern used by the receiver for bit-clock synchronization. 2. **Start of Frame / SoF (1 byte)** — Fixed value `0x01` for standard data frames. 3. **Length (1 byte)** — Number of bytes following this field, excluding the trailing checksum and EoF. 4. **Header (1 byte)** — Contains the R/R (Request/Response) bit and the 4-bit sequence number for duplicate detection. 5. **HomeID (4 bytes)** — A **globally unique 32-bit identifier** assigned by the primary controller during inclusion. Frames with mismatched HomeID are silently dropped — providing an implicit network-isolation security layer. 6. **Source NodeID (1 byte)** — Identifies the sender (1–232). NodeID 0xFF is reserved for broadcasts. 7. **Frame Header (2 bytes)** — Contains the **Destination NodeID** and frame-control bits (e.g., routing, low-power, ACK request). 8. **Application Payload (n bytes)** — Encapsulated Command Class data. 9. **Checksum (1 byte)** — An 8-bit **XOR of all preceding bytes** (from Length to payload). Provides single-byte error detection; modern revisions upgrade to **CRC-8** with polynomial 0xB8. 10. **End of Frame / EoF (1 byte)** — Fixed value `0x03`. > Award 2 marks for the diagram/table; 2 marks for HomeID; 1 mark for NodeID; 2 marks for Checksum. --- ## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically LOSE marks in Z-Wave questions:** > 1. **Confusing Z-Wave with Zigbee frequencies** — Z-Wave is **sub-1 GHz**, Zigbee is **2.4 GHz**. Many students write the wrong frequency and lose 1–2 marks instantly. > 2. **Forgetting the HomeID field** — Examiners specifically test whether you know that **HomeID is the network identifier** and that frames with mismatched HomeID are dropped. Many students skip this in their frame-structure answers. > 3. **Not mentioning FLiRS** — When asked about battery-operated devices, **"FLiRS"** is the magic word. If you just say "battery device" without mentioning FLiRS, you lose 1 mark. > 4. **Saying "Z-Wave uses Wi-Fi"** — A fatal error. Z-Wave is **NOT** Wi-Fi; it is a **proprietary sub-1 GHz mesh** protocol. > 5. **Forgetting the 232-node limit** — Z-Wave networks are hard-capped at **232 nodes**; Zigbee can support 65 535. Examiners love this comparison. > 6. **Mixing up encryption generations** — Old Z-Wave used **optional S0 (AES-128)**; Z-Wave Plus and beyond use **mandatory S2 (AES-128 with ECDH key exchange)**. Writing just "AES-128" is partial credit only. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Z-Wave = sub-1 GHz, GFSK, low-data-rate, mesh home-automation protocol.** - **Frequencies:** 908.42 MHz (US), 868.42 MHz (EU/India) — **never 2.4 GHz**. - **Data rates:** 9.6 kbps (R1), 40 kbps (R2), 100 kbps (700-series). - **Topology:** Mesh; **max 232 nodes**; **max 4 hops**. - **Three device types:** Primary Controller, Secondary Controller, Slave (Routing or FLiRS). - **FLiRS = Frequently Listening Receiver Slave** (battery-powered, ~1 s wake-up, **cannot repeat**). - **Security:** **AES-128** (mandatory in Z-Wave Plus / S2) with **ECDH key exchange**. - **Routing:** Source routing with **Explorer Frames** + **Last Working Route (LWR)** caching. - **Inclusion:** Primary controller in *Learn Mode* + device triple-tap → controller assigns **HomeID + NodeID**. - **Frame fields:** Preamble, SoF, Length, Header, **HomeID (4 B)**, **Source NodeID**, Frame Header, Payload, Checksum (XOR/CRC-8), EoF. - **Standards Body:** **Z-Wave Alliance** (formerly Zensys → Sigma Designs → Silicon Labs). - **Standard:** **ITU-T G.9959** (physical & MAC), Z-Wave Application Layer specifications. - **Comparison hot-spots:** sub-1 GHz vs 2.4 GHz; 232 vs 65 535 nodes; 4 hops vs 15+; proprietary vs IEEE 802.15.4. - **Z-Wave 700-series (2019):** adds S2 Security, SmartStart inclusion, 100 kbps, longer battery life. - **Not for:** video/audio streaming, large-scale industrial control — use Wi-Fi/Zigbee/LoRa instead. - **Perfect for:** smart lighting, door locks, leak sensors, thermostats, in-home mesh automation.

topics.title: Bluetooth Low Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT601 - IOT – ARCHITECTURE, PROTOCOLS AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bluetooth Low Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)**, formally standardized as **Bluetooth Smart** under the IEEE 802.15.1 specification and maintained by the Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG), is a wireless personal area network (WPAN) technology designed from the ground up for **ultra-low power**, **low data-rate**, and **short-range** communication. It operates in the globally available, license-exempt **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio band**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** BLE is a protocol stack optimized for **machine-to-machine (M2M)** and **Internet of Things (IoT)** communication, where coin-cell battery-powered sensors must operate for **months to years** without intervention. It is *not* an evolution of Classic Bluetooth; it is an entirely re-engineered protocol that shares only the **2.4 GHz frequency band and frequency-hopping concept**.

Key physical-layer metrics standardized by the Bluetooth Core Specification (v5.x):

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Frequency Band | 2400 – 2483.5 MHz (ISM) |
| Number of RF Channels | 40 (2 MHz spacing) |
| Advertising Channels | 3 (Channels 37, 38, 39) |
| Data Channels | 37 (Channels 0 – 36) |
| Modulation | Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK) |
| LE 1M PHY Data Rate | 1 Mbps |
| LE 2M PHY Data Rate | 2 Mbps |
| LE Coded PHY | 500 kbps / 125 kbps |
| Tx Power (typical) | -20 dBm to +20 dBm |
| Range (outdoor) | ~100 m (LE Coded) / ~50 m (LE 1M) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whispering Mailbox"

Imagine a **post office where every neighbour has a tiny, battery-operated mailbox** (the BLE peripheral). Instead of standing outside shouting to be heard (like Classic Bluetooth, which maintains a continuous loud "voice call"), the mailbox **briefly shouts once every few hundred milliseconds** — *"Is anyone there? I have a temperature reading!"* (this is called **Advertising**).

A **central device** (say, your smartphone) that is actively listening (in **Scanning** state) hears this whisper, opens a quick **short envelope** (a connection request), and from then on, the two devices agree on a **private, scheduled handshake** (a **connection event**) every 7.5 ms to 4 seconds. The peripheral sleeps deeply in between, sipping almost no current from its coin cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Insight:** Classic Bluetooth is like a phone call (always on, continuous stream, drains battery). BLE is like **SMS with scheduled delivery** — short, infrequent, and energy-frugal. This is why BLE enables coin-cell devices to last **1–5 years**, whereas Classic Bluetooth devices last days.

## 1.3 Why BLE Matters in the IoT Stack

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** IoT reference architecture, BLE sits in the **Perception / Connectivity layer**, alongside Zigbee, LoRa, and Wi-Fi. Its unique value proposition is:

1. **Ubiquity** — every modern smartphone has a BLE radio, making the phone a free gateway.
2. **Low Power** — peak current ~15 mA, average ~1 µA.
3. **Mesh Capability** (BLE Mesh, v5.0+) — many-to-many communication.
4. **Beaconing** — one-way indoor positioning (Apple iBeacon, Google Eddystone).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** BLE was **not** part of the original Bluetooth 1.0 spec. It was introduced in **Bluetooth 4.0 (June 2010)** as a parallel stack. Bluetooth 5.0 (2016) doubled data rate and quadrupled range. Bluetooth 5.3/5.4 (2022/2023) added **Periodic Advertising with Responses (PAwR)** and **Encrypted Advertising Data** — both important for large-scale IoT.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The BLE Protocol Stack — Architecture

The BLE stack is divided into three logical partitions, each with distinct hardware/software responsibilities.

### 2.1.1 Controller Subsystem (Lower Layers)

1. **Physical Layer (PHY)**
   - Owns the 2.4 GHz radio.
   - Modulates bits into GFSK waveforms.
   - Performs channel selection, frequency hopping, and transmit/receive switching.

2. **Link Layer (LL)**
   - The "traffic policeman" of BLE.
   - Manages the **five device states**: Standby, Advertising, Scanning, Initiating, Connection.
   - Handles **advertising, scanning, connection establishment, encryption, and connection parameter updates**.

3. **Host Controller Interface (HCI)**
   - A standardized command/response interface (typically UART, USB, or SPI) between the Controller (radio chip) and the Host (MCU software).

### 2.1.2 Host Subsystem (Upper Layers)

4. **Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)**
   - Performs **protocol multiplexing** (channels with Channel IDs/CIDs).
   - Performs **segmentation and reassembly (SAR)** of larger packets.
   - Supports **LE Credit-Based Flow Control** for connection-oriented channels.

5. **Attribute Protocol (ATT)**
   - The "language" used to read, write, and discover small data items called **Attributes** stored on the peer device.
   - Operates on a strict **client-server** model.

6. **Generic Attribute Profile (GATT)**
   - Defines a hierarchical data model on top of ATT: **Services → Characteristics → Descriptors**.
   - Most IoT application profiles (Heart Rate, Environmental Sensing, etc.) are defined here.

7. **Generic Access Profile (GAP)**
   - Defines the **roles** (Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central).
   - Defines **discovery, connection, and bonding procedures**.

8. **Security Manager (SM)**
   - Handles **pairing, bonding, and encryption key distribution** (LE Secure Connections).

9. **Generic Access Profile Application Layer (Applications / Profiles)**
   - Higher-level use cases like Heart Rate Profile (HRP), Proximity Profile, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You are *very* likely to be asked to draw or explain the **GATT data hierarchy** (Services contain Characteristics, which contain Descriptors and Values). Memorize the *Client* vs *Server* distinction: **GATT Client = reads/writes**; **GATT Server = holds the data** (typically the sensor/peripheral).

## 2.2 BLE Device Roles and States

| Layer | Role | Function |
|---|---|---|
| GAP | **Broadcaster** | Sends advertisements only (e.g., a beacon). |
| GAP | **Observer** | Scans for advertisements only. |
| GAP | **Peripheral** | Advertises and accepts connections. |
| GAP | **Central** | Scans and initiates connections. |
| LL | **Standby** | Default, no radio activity. |
| LL | **Advertising** | Transmitting advertising packets on the 3 adv channels. |
| LL | **Scanning** | Listening for advertisements. |
| LL | **Initiating** | Listening + sending Connection Request. |
| LL | **Connection** | Master/Slave exchanging data on data channels. |

## 2.3 BLE Channel Architecture — Mathematical Foundation

BLE defines **40 RF channels** numbered 0 to 39, each occupying a **2 MHz** slice of the 2.4 GHz ISM band.

The center frequency of channel $k$ is given by:

$$f_k = 2402 + 2k \quad \text{[MHz]}$$

$$k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 39\}$$

For example:
- Channel 0: $f_0 = 2402 \text{ MHz}$
- Channel 39: $f_{39} = 2480 \text{ MHz}$
- Channel 37 (adv): $f_{37} = 2476 \text{ MHz}$
- Channel 38 (adv): $f_{38} = 2478 \text{ MHz}$
- Channel 39 (adv): $f_{39} = 2480 \text{ MHz}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **three advertising channels are deliberately placed in the gaps** between Wi-Fi channels 1, 6, and 11. This minimizes co-channel interference in dense 2.4 GHz environments — a critical engineering decision worth remembering for the KTU exam.

## 2.4 Frequency Hopping — Adaptive Channel Selection

Once connected, the **Central (master)** and **Peripheral (slave)** hop across the **37 data channels** using a deterministic pseudo-random sequence. The hop at connection event number $n$ is computed as:

$$chn_{n+1} = (chn_n + \text{hopIncrement}) \mod 37$$

Where:
- $chn_n$ is the current data channel index (0 to 36),
- $\text{hopIncrement}$ is a value in $\{5, 6, \ldots, 16\}$ chosen at connection setup,
- The result is then **remapped** to avoid the three advertising channels using a re-mapping algorithm defined in the Bluetooth Core Spec.

The hop increment and access address are exchanged in the **CONNECT_REQ** packet.

> [!NOTE]
> The remapping step ensures that the *output* of the modulo-37 sequence is always one of the 37 *data* channel indices, never 37, 38, or 39.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Channel center frequency | $f_k = 2402 + 2k$ | MHz, $k \in [0, 39]$ |
| Channel spacing | $\Delta f = 2$ | MHz |
| Total RF bandwidth | $B = 40 \times 2 = 80$ | MHz |
| Energy per bit (LE 1M) | $E_b = 1 \text{ µs}$ | bit duration |
| Connection interval range | $7.5 \text{ ms} \leq CI \leq 4000 \text{ ms}$ | in multiples of 1.25 ms |
| Slave latency | $0 \leq SL \leq 499$ | number of skipped events |
| Connection event duration | $CE \leq CI$ | non-overlapping events |
| Supervision timeout | $T_{sup} = (1 + SL) \times CI \times 6$ | typical KTU exam formula |
| Max throughput (LE 1M) | $R_{max} = 1 \text{ Mbps}$ | raw, ATT payload is much lower |
| Practical ATT throughput | $\approx 100 \text{ kbps}$ | with overhead |
| Range (free space, +20 dBm) | $d \propto \sqrt{P_t}$ | Friis-style intuition |
| Link budget | $LB = P_t + G_t + G_r - S_r$ | dB; $S_r$ = sensitivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The supervision timeout must be **strictly greater** than $(1 + SL) \times CI$, otherwise the link will time out even when healthy. A common ratio used in industry: $T_{sup} = 6 \times (1 + SL) \times CI$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Wearables** — Fitness bands (Mi Band, Fitbit) use BLE to stream heart rate.
- **Smart Home** — Apple HomeKit, Google Home accessories (locks, lights, thermostats).
- **Asset Tracking** — Apple AirTag uses BLE beacons for crowd-sourced positioning.
- **Industrial IoT** — Wireless sensor nodes in factories, BLE Mesh for lighting control.
- **Medical Devices** — Glucose meters, pulse oximeters, blood pressure cuffs (FDA-approved BLE Medical Profile).
- **Automotive** — Tire Pressure Monitoring Systems (TPMS), digital car key (BLE + UWB).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: BLE Channel Frequency Table

We begin with the Bluetooth Core Specification definition and derive the center frequency for *every* BLE channel.

**Step 1 — Anchor Channel Definition**

By specification, Channel 0 sits at the lower edge of the 2.4 GHz ISM band, with its center at 2402 MHz. This is the **anchor** of the channelization plan.

$$f_0 = 2402 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Channel Spacing**

Adjacent channels are separated by exactly 2 MHz to allow non-overlapping 1 MHz bandwidths (since GFSK with BT=0.5 occupies ~1 MHz).

$$\Delta f = 2 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Write the Linear Equation**

For any channel $k$:

$$f_k = f_0 + k \cdot \Delta f$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Constants**

$$f_k = 2402 + 2k \quad \text{[MHz]}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bounds**

Since $k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 39\}$:

- $f_{min} = f_0 = 2402$ MHz
- $f_{max} = f_{39} = 2402 + 2 \times 39 = 2402 + 78 = 2480$ MHz

**Step 6 — Verify Total Occupied Bandwidth**

$$B_{total} = f_{max} - f_{min} + \Delta f = 2480 - 2402 + 2 = 80 \text{ MHz}$$

This matches the upper edge of the 2.4 GHz ISM band (2400 – 2483.5 MHz, with a 3.5 MHz guard at the top).

## 3.2 Derivation: Advertising Channel Frequencies

The Bluetooth Core Specification fixes the three advertising channels at indices 37, 38, and 39. Using the formula above:

**Step 1 — Apply Formula for k = 37**

$$f_{37} = 2402 + 2 \times 37 = 2402 + 74 = 2476 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Formula for k = 38**

$$f_{38} = 2402 + 2 \times 38 = 2402 + 76 = 2478 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Formula for k = 39**

$$f_{39} = 2402 + 2 \times 39 = 2402 + 78 = 2480 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Wi-Fi Avoidance**

Standard Wi-Fi channels (20 MHz wide) are centered at:
- Channel 1: 2412 MHz
- Channel 6: 2437 MHz
- Channel 11: 2462 MHz

The 22 MHz-wide Wi-Fi bands end approximately at 2412 + 11 = 2423 MHz (Ch 1 top edge), 2448 MHz (Ch 6), and 2473 MHz (Ch 11). The BLE advertising channels at **2476, 2478, 2480 MHz** sit *above* Wi-Fi channel 11's upper edge — a clean RF neighborhood. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** The 3-channel advertising scheme is *not* arbitrary — it is a **deliberate RF coexistence engineering decision** to avoid Wi-Fi's high-traffic sub-bands. This is a favourite KTU viva question.

## 3.3 Derivation: Connection Event Timing Mathematics

The most important timing relationship in a BLE connection is the **supervision timeout formula**. We derive it.

**Step 1 — Define Connection Interval (CI)**

The connection interval is the time between two consecutive connection events. Range:

$$CI_{min} = 7.5 \text{ ms}, \quad CI_{max} = 4 \text{ s}$$

It is a multiple of **1.25 ms**.

**Step 2 — Define Slave Latency (SL)**

A peripheral may skip up to SL connection events to save power. Range: $0 \le SL \le 499$.

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Time Between Two Packets**

In the worst case, the peripheral sleeps through SL events, then waits one full CI, then waits one more CI for the supervision counter to elapse:

$$T_{worst} = (1 + SL) \times CI$$

**Step 4 — Supervision Timeout Definition**

The supervision timeout $T_{sup}$ is the maximum allowed gap between two valid received packets. To be safe, the spec requires:

$$T_{sup} > (1 + SL) \times CI$$

The recommended industry value is:

$$T_{sup} = 6 \times (1 + SL) \times CI$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)**

Suppose $CI = 100$ ms, $SL = 4$. Calculate the recommended supervision timeout.

**Solution:**

$$T_{sup} = 6 \times (1 + 4) \times 100 \text{ ms} = 6 \times 5 \times 100 = 3000 \text{ ms} = 3 \text{ s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** Problems like the above are commonly asked. Memorize the formula, the units, and the constraint $T_{sup} > (1 + SL) \times CI$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Effective Data Throughput (LE 1M PHY)

The "1 Mbps" headline rate is the *raw air bit rate*. Effective application throughput is much lower due to protocol overhead. We compute it.

**Step 1 — LE 1M Air Bit Rate**

$$R_{air} = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 2 — Per-Packet Overhead**

A single LE data packet consists of:
- Preamble: 1 byte = 8 bits
- Access Address: 4 bytes = 32 bits
- PDU Header: 2 bytes = 16 bits
- PDU Payload (max): 27 bytes = 216 bits (LE 1M)
- MIC (encrypted): 4 bytes = 32 bits
- CRC: 3 bytes = 24 bits

Total packet size (unencrypted, max payload):

$$L_{pkt} = 8 + 32 + 16 + 216 + 24 = 296 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Per-Packet Throughput**

$$\eta_{pkt} = \frac{216}{296} \approx 0.73 \quad \text{or} \quad 73\%$$

**Step 4 — Connection Event Overhead**

Between packets, the radio must perform a T_IFS (Inter-Frame Space) of 150 µs and a frequency hop. Real-world duty is reduced further to ~**100 kbps** of usable ATT data.

**Step 5 — Application-Throughput Estimate**

$$R_{app} = R_{air} \times \eta_{pkt} \times \eta_{duty} = 10^6 \times 0.73 \times 0.15 \approx 110 \text{ kbps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 1 Mbps BLE link delivers only ~110 kbps of usable application data. This is why BLE is for **sensors and notifications**, not for audio/video streaming (though LE Audio with LC3 codec changed this in BT 5.2).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: BLE vs Classic Bluetooth

| Parameter | Classic Bluetooth (BR/EDR) | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz ISM | 2.4 GHz ISM |
| Number of Channels | 79 (1 MHz) | 40 (2 MHz) |
| Modulation | GFSK, π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK | GFSK only |
| Peak Current | ~30–40 mA | ~15 mA |
| Sleep Current | ~1 mA | ~1 µA |
| Connection Setup | ~100 ms – several seconds | ~3 ms (advertising → connected) |
| Max Data Rate | 3 Mbps (EDR) | 2 Mbps (LE 2M) |
| Min Connection Time | Continuous | As low as 7.5 ms |
| Profile Concept | 25+ legacy profiles | GATT-based (universal) |
| Use Case | Audio (headsets, speakers) | Sensors, beacons, wearables |
| Coexistence with Wi-Fi | Moderate | Good (adv. channels in cleanest band) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** You may be asked to *"Compare and contrast BLE and Classic Bluetooth"* or *"Explain why BLE is preferred for IoT"*. This table is your definitive answer scaffold.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: BLE Connection Establishment Procedure

| Step | Action | Initiator | Responder | Channel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Advertiser sends ADV_IND | — | Peripheral (Slave) | 37/38/39 |
| 2 | Scanner listens | Central (Master) | — | 37/38/39 |
| 3 | Scanner receives ADV_IND | Central | Peripheral | — |
| 4 | Scanner sends CONNECT_REQ | Central | Peripheral | — |
| 5 | Slave enters Connection state | — | Peripheral | 0–36 |
| 6 | First connection event | Central → Peripheral | Peripheral → Central | 0–36 |
| 7 | Slave may sleep between events | — | — | — |
| 8 | Termination via LL_TERMINATE_IND | Either | Either | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Math:** A peripheral advertising at 100 ms intervals with a 3-byte payload consumes roughly **0.5–1 mA average current**, rising to ~15 mA only for the ~3 ms transmit burst. This is the magic of BLE.

```mermaid
    STANDBY --> SCANNING : LL Set Scan Enable

    ADVERTISING --> CONNECTION : Receive CONNECT_REQ

```
