| Channel Hopping | No | Yes | **Yes** |
| Cell Negotiation | N/A | Centralized | **Distributed (6P) or Centralized (PCE)** |
| Energy Budget | Poor | Good | **Excellent** |
| Vendor Lock-in | Low | High | **None** |

## 3.5 Derivation: Duty Cycle and Battery Lifetime

**Problem:** A node is active (TX or RX) in $N_{active} = 2$ cells out of $N_{ts} = 101$ cells, with $T_{slot} = 10$ ms. Battery capacity $C_{bat} = 2400$ mAh, average active current $I_{active} = 25$ mA, sleep current $I_{sleep} = 0.005$ mA. Estimate battery lifetime in days.

**Step 1 — Compute the duty cycle.**

$$
D = \frac{N_{active}}{N_{ts}} = \frac{2}{101} \approx 0.0198 = 1.98\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average current consumption.**

$$
I_{avg} = D \cdot I_{active} + (1-D) \cdot I_{sleep}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
I_{avg} = (0.0198 \times 25) + (0.9802 \times 0.005)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term.**

$$
I_{avg} = 0.495 \text{ mA} + 0.0049 \text{ mA} = 0.4999 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute battery lifetime in hours.**

$$
t_{bat} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2400 \text{ mAh}}{0.4999 \text{ mA}} \approx 4800 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to days.**

$$
t_{bat} = \frac{4800}{24} = 200 \text{ days}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** 6TiSCH's low duty cycle (<2%) enables **multi-year battery life** on standard cells — a critical requirement for IIoT deployments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 6TiSCH Network Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    appL["Application Layer CoAP MQTT-SN"]
    transL["Transport Layer UDP"]
    netL["Network Layer RPL 6LoWPAN"]
    tischL["6TiSCH Sublayer 6P Scheduling"]
    macL["IEEE 802.15.4e TSCH MAC"]
    phyL["IEEE 802.15.4 PHY 2.4 GHz"]

    appL --> transL --> netL --> tischL --> macL --> phyL

    pce["PCE Path Computation Element"]
    jrc["JRC Join Registrar Coordinator"]
    tschL -.Centralized Schedule.-> pce
    tischL -.Neighbor Negotiation.-> jrc
```

> **Reading guide:** Solid arrows = strict protocol layering. Dashed arrows = optional auxiliary control-plane components.

## 4.2 Slotframe and Timeslot Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title 6TiSCH Slotframe Timeline (N_ts = 8 shown for illustration)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Timeslot 0
    CCA :a1, 0, 1
    TX   :a2, 1, 1
    ACK  :a3, 2, 1
    IDLE :a4, 3, 7
    section Timeslot 1
    CCA :b1, 10, 1
    RX   :b2, 11, 1
    ACK  :b3, 12, 1
    IDLE :b4, 13, 7
    section Timeslot 2
    SLEEP :c1, 20, 10
    section Timeslot 3
    SLEEP :d1, 30, 10
    section Timeslot 4
    CCA :e1, 40, 1
    TX   :e2, 41, 1
    ACK  :e3, 42, 1
    IDLE :e4, 43, 7
```

> **Reading guide:** Slotframe = group of $N_{ts}$ slots. Cell is allocated only where transmission occurs; other slots are powered-down (sleep) for energy efficiency.

## 4.3 6P Cell Negotiation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant NodeA
    participant NodeB
    Note over NodeA,NodeB: 6P ADD Transaction
    NodeA->>NodeB: ADD Request (cellList, numCells)
    NodeB->>NodeB: Run Scheduling Function
    NodeB-->>NodeA: ADD Response (Success/Error, allocated cells)
    Note over NodeA,NodeB: Cell now active in schedule
```

## 4.4 6TiSCH Channel Hopping Pattern

```mermaid
graph LR
    asn1["ASN 100 offset 2 Channel 13"]
    asn2["ASN 101 offset 2 Channel 14"]
    asn3["ASN 102 offset 2 Channel 15"]
    asn4["ASN 103 offset 2 Channel 16"]
    asn5["ASN 104 offset 2 Channel 17"]
    asn1 --> asn2 --> asn3 --> asn4 --> asn5
```

> **Reading guide:** Each successive timeslot, the physical channel rotates by +1 (modulo $n_{ch}$ = 16), thereby mitigating narrowband interference and multipath fading.

## 4.5 Functional Block Architecture of a 6TiSCH Node

```mermaid
graph TB
    sensor["Physical Sensors"]
    appB["Application Logic"]
    coapB["CoAP Client"]
    udpB["UDP Socket"]
    rplB["RPL DODAG"]
    loWPAN["6LoWPAN Compression"]
    sixP["6P Engine"]
    sched["Schedule Table"]
    tschMac["TSCH MAC"]
    radio["CC2538 / CC2652 Radio"]
    batt["Battery Monitor"]

    sensor --> appB --> coapB --> udpB --> rplB
    rplB --> loWPAN --> sixP --> sched --> tschMac --> radio
    batt -.Power Gate.-> radio
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Q, June 2024 Pattern]**
*Define 6TiSCH and explain its role in the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
6TiSCH stands for *IPv6 over the Time-Slotted Channel Hopping mode of IEEE 802.15.4e*. It is an IETF-defined networking architecture (RFC 9030) that combines the deterministic, low-power MAC capabilities of IEEE 802.15.4e TSCH with IPv6 networking primitives (6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP). Its role in IIoT is to provide **deterministic latency, ultra-high reliability (5-nines), and energy efficiency** required for industrial control and monitoring applications such as smart grid, process automation, and predictive maintenance. By using a centralized or distributed schedule, every node knows exactly when and on which channel to transmit, eliminating contention.

> **Mark split:** Definition — 1.5 marks, IIoT role — 1.5 marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Q, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*List and briefly explain any THREE components of the 6TiSCH architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **TSCH MAC (IEEE 802.15.4e)** — Provides time-slotted access and channel hopping at the link layer. (1 mark)
2. **6top Sublayer (6P Protocol, RFC 8480)** — Enables distributed negotiation, addition, and deletion of schedule cells between neighboring nodes. (1 mark)
3. **6LoWPAN** — Compresses IPv6 headers to fit the small frame sizes of IEEE 802.15.4, enabling native IPv6 over low-power networks. (1 mark)

(Alternatively: RPL, CoAP, Slotframe, Channel Hopping, PCE, JRC.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Q, June 2024 Pattern]**

(a) **Explain the IEEE 802.15.4e TSCH MAC mode in detail. How does it achieve deterministic communication? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Time-Slotted Access (3 marks):**  
In TSCH, all nodes are tightly synchronized to a global time base called the **Absolute Slot Number (ASN)**. Time is divided into fixed-length **timeslots** of typically **10 ms**, and a group of timeslots forms a **slotframe** (e.g., 101 slots ≈ 1.01 s). Within each timeslot, a node may either transmit (TX), receive (RX), or sleep. Because the schedule is predetermined, **contention is eliminated**, giving bounded latency equal to one slot duration (or one slotframe for the worst case).

**2. Channel Hopping (2 marks):**  
The physical channel used in each timeslot is computed using:
$$ ch = ((ASN + ch_{offset}) \bmod n_{ch}) + f_{min} $$
This constantly rotates across the **16 available 2.4 GHz channels**, providing **frequency diversity** that defeats narrowband interference, multipath fading, and jamming.

**3. Synchronization (1 mark):**  
Nodes maintain synchronization through **Enhanced Beacons (EBs)** sent by the PAN coordinator in dedicated cells, and via **ACK-based clock drift compensation** at the receiver.

**4. Determinism (1 mark):**  
Since every node has its own pre-assigned (slot, channel) cell, the maximum one-hop latency is exactly $T_{slot}$ (e.g., 10 ms). Multi-hop latency is bounded by $\sum_{hops} T_{slot}$. This **worst-case bounded latency** is what TSCH guarantees — and hence deterministic communication.

---

(b) **Describe the 6TiSCH protocol stack. With a neat diagram, explain the role of the 6P protocol in cell management. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Protocol Stack (3 marks):**  
The 6TiSCH stack is layered as follows (bottom-up):

| Layer | Protocol | Standard Body |
|---|---|---|
| PHY | IEEE 802.15.4 (2.4 GHz) | IEEE |
| MAC | IEEE 802.15.4e TSCH | IEEE |
| 6TiSCH Sublayer | 6P (6top) | IETF 6TiSCH WG |
| Adaptation | 6LoWPAN | IETF 6lo WG |
| Routing | RPL | IETF roll WG |
| Transport | UDP | IETF |
| Application | CoAP / MQTT-SN | IETF CoRE / OASIS |

**2. Role of 6P (3 marks):**  
The **6top Protocol (6P)**, defined in **RFC 8480**, is a two-way signaling protocol that lets neighbors **negotiate cells** in the TSCH schedule. It supports three primary operations:
- **ADD** — request to allocate one or more new (slot, channel) cells
- **DELETE** — release previously allocated cells
- **RELOCATE** — shift a cell to a different slot or channel

A 6P transaction is two-message: **Request → Response**, with confirmation codes (RC_SUCCESS, RC_EOL, RC_BUSY, etc.).

**3. Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.3 in this material for the 6P ADD sequence diagram.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Q, Dec 2023 Pattern]**

(a) **A 6TiSCH network uses a slotframe of 101 timeslots, each of duration 10 ms. Calculate the slotframe duration. If 20 nodes each require one dedicated uplink cell, what fraction of the slotframe is utilized? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Slotframe duration (2 marks):**
$$ T_{SF} = N_{ts} \times T_{slot} = 101 \times 10 \text{ ms} = 1010 \text{ ms} = 1.01 \text{ s} $$

**Step 2 — Cells required (1 mark):**  
20 nodes × 1 uplink cell/node = **20 cells**

**Step 3 — Utilization fraction (2 marks):**
$$ \eta = \frac{N_{used}}{N_{ts}} = \frac{20}{101} \approx 0.1980 = 19.8\% $$

**Step 4 — Engineering inference (2 marks):**  
With ~20% utilization, the network has substantial headroom for growth (e.g., adding retransmission cells, broadcast cells, downlink cells). The **duty cycle of each node** is approximately $1/101 \approx 1\%$, which leads to excellent energy efficiency. To improve scalability, the slotframe length could be increased to 1010 slots (10 s cycle) to support up to ~1000 nodes.

---

(b) **Explain the minimal 6TiSCH configuration. Why is it required, and how does the 6P protocol extend it? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. What is the Minimal Configuration? (2 marks):**  
The minimal configuration (RFC 8180) is the **smallest set of scheduled cells** that every 6TiSCH node MUST support to bootstrap into a network:
- **1 EB (Enhanced Beacon) cell per slotframe** for the PAN coordinator
- **1 shared broadcast cell per slotframe** for general-purpose transmissions
- All other cells are dynamically negotiated.

**2. Why it is required (2 marks):**  
It guarantees that even a brand-new, unconfigured node — when it boots up — can:
- Discover the network (via EBs)
- Receive a join response (via broadcast cell)
- Perform RPL DIO/DAO exchange

Without the minimal schedule, the network would have a **chicken-and-egg problem**: nodes need cells to communicate, but they need to communicate to get cells.

**3. How 6P extends it (3 marks):**  
Once a node has joined via the minimal schedule, it uses the **6P protocol** with its preferred parent (typically a DAG root or border router) to **request additional dedicated cells** using the ADD command. The Scheduling Function (e.g., SFRMS) running in each node decides which cells to allocate. Over time, the schedule is built up — cell by cell — into a fully provisioned, deterministic, and reliable network.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TSCH (802.15.4e) with classical 802.15.4** — TSCH is a *mode* of 802.15.4e, not the original 802.15.4 (which uses CSMA/CA). Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 2. **Writing "6TiSCH is a protocol"** — It is a *protocol architecture / working group*, not a single protocol. Use the term "architecture" or "protocol stack."
> 3. **Forgetting units in numericals** — Always write ms, GHz, mAh next to the numbers. Marks are deducted for dimensional sloppiness.
> 4. **Ignoring the 16-channel limit** — When computing $ch$, students often forget that $f_{min} = 11$ shifts the result; channel 26 is the highest, not channel 15.
> 5. **Not mentioning 6P, RPL, 6LoWPAN by name** — These are *expected* in any 6TiSCH answer on KTU 2024 papers. A bare TSCH answer will only earn partial marks.
> 6. **Confusing 6P with 6LoWPAN** — 6P is *scheduling*; 6LoWPAN is *header compression*. Examiners immediately mark this as a conceptual error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

### A. Acronyms
- **6TiSCH** = IPv6 over **Ti**me-**S**ynchronized **Ch**annel **H**opping
- **TSCH** = Time-Slotted Channel Hopping (MAC mode of 802.15.4e)
- **6P** = 6top Protocol (RFC 8480) — cell negotiation
- **RPL** = Routing Protocol for LLNs (RFC 6550)
- **6LoWPAN** = IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN (RFC 4919, 6282)
- **EB** = Enhanced Beacon
- **ASN** = Absolute Slot Number
- **PCE** = Path Computation Element (centralized scheduler)
- **JRC** = Join Registrar Coordinator

### B. Numbers to Remember
- 16 channels in 2.4 GHz band (channels 11–26)
- Typical $T_{slot}$ = **10 ms** or 15 ms
- Default $N_{ts}$ = 101 → $T_{SF} = 1.01$ s
- ASN is **64-bit** counter
- Slotframe repeats periodically; multiple slotframes can coexist (e.g., 41-slot for beacons, 101-slot for data)

### C. Core Formulas
- $T_{SF} = N_{ts} \times T_{slot}$
- $ch = ((ASN + ch_{offset}) \bmod n_{ch}) + f_{min}$
- $ASN = (N_{ts} \times SFC) + slot_{idx}$
- $D = N_{active} / N_{ts}$ (duty cycle)
- $I_{avg} = D \cdot I_{active} + (1-D) \cdot I_{sleep}$
- $L_{max} = H \times T_{slot}$ (worst-case latency, H = hops)

### D. Key Distinctions (Frequently Asked)
- **Static vs Centralized vs Distributed Scheduling**
- **Minimal Schedule vs Negotiated Schedule**
- **6P (scheduling) vs 6LoWPAN (compression)**
- **TSCH vs CSMA/CA**
- **Determinism (6TiSCH) vs Best-Effort (legacy WSN)**

### E. Standards Reference
- IEEE 802.15.4e — 2012 (TSCH MAC)
- RFC 8180 — 6TiSCH Use Cases
- RFC 9030 — 6TiSCH Architecture
- RFC 8480 — 6P Protocol
- RFC 6550 — RPL
- RFC 4919 / 6282 — 6LoWPAN

> **Final Exam Tip:** When the question says "explain 6TiSCH," always structure your answer in three layers — **(1) what problem it solves, (2) how the architecture is built, (3) why each component is needed**. This is the exact structure used by KTU 2024 model answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
