So a single-path TCP on that same 6-Mbps link would also deliver ~6 Mbps, not 7.44 Mbps. LIA is designed so that MPTCP **uses the *additional* LTE bandwidth *without* exceeding the fair share of the Wi-Fi bottleneck**. We verify by computing $\alpha$ for our 2 subflows: $$ \alpha = cwnd_{total}\cdot \frac{\max\!\left(\dfrac{cwnd_1}{RTT_1^2},\ \dfrac{cwnd_2}{RTT_2^2}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{cwnd_1}{RTT_1}+\dfrac{cwnd_2}{RTT_2}\right)^2} $$ Compute numerator terms: $$ \frac{cwnd_1}{RTT_1^2} = \frac{20}{(0.04)^2} = \frac{20}{0.0016} = 12\,500 $$ $$ \frac{cwnd_2}{RTT_2^2} = \frac{12}{(0.10)^2} = \frac{12}{0.01} = 1\,200 $$ Maximum = $12\,500$ (subflow 1, the Wi-Fi path). Compute denominator: $$ \frac{cwnd_1}{RTT_1} + \frac{cwnd_2}{RTT_2} = 500 + 120 = 620 $$ $$ (\text{Denominator})^2 = 620^2 = 384\,400 $$ Now: $$ cwnd_{total} = cwnd_1 + cwnd_2 = 20 + 12 = 32 $$ $$ \alpha = 32 \cdot \frac{12\,500}{384\,400} = \frac{32 \times 12\,500}{384\,400} = \frac{400\,000}{384\,400} \approx 1.0405 $$ **Interpretation:** $\alpha > 1$ means LIA *does* allow the Wi-Fi subflow (the bottleneck one) to grow slightly faster than independent TCP would — this is the *excess* bandwidth that the LTE subflow can absorb without harming the Wi-Fi bottleneck. This is exactly the **resource-pooling principle**: MPTCP shifts load to the non-bottleneck path (LTE) so that the bottleneck (Wi-Fi) sees *no more* traffic than a competing single-path TCP would. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Reordering Tolerance at the Receiver > **Problem (7-mark):** MPTCP uses a **64-bit Data Sequence Number (DSN)** at the connection level and **32-bit Subflow Sequence Number (SSN)** per subflow. The receiver's reassembly buffer is $B_{rx}$ = 256 KB. The application reads at $R_{app}$ = 8 Mbps. Two subflows deliver: > - Subflow 1: DSN $0 \to 99$ (100 KB), arrives at $t_1 = 50$ ms. > - Subflow 2: DSN $100 \to 199$ (100 KB), arrives at $t_2 = 120$ ms. > > Compute the **head-of-line blocking delay** $\Delta_{HOL}$ and the **minimum reassembly buffer** $B_{min}$ to avoid application stall. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the gap.** Subflow 2 (DSN 100–199) arrives 70 ms *after* subflow 1. So the data delivered by subflow 1 *cannot* be delivered to the application until subflow 2's bytes fill the gap. **Step 2 — Head-of-line blocking delay** = the time the first subflow's bytes wait in the buffer: $$ \Delta_{HOL} = t_2 - t_1 = 120 - 50 = 70\;\text{ms} $$ **Step 3 — Minimum buffer** = the data that must be buffered from subflow 1 while waiting for subflow 2 to arrive, minus the data the application can read during $\Delta_{HOL}$: $$ B_{min} = \text{Data}_{SF1} - R_{app} \cdot \Delta_{HOL} $$ Convert units: $R_{app}$ in bytes/s = $8 \times 10^6$ bits/s / 8 = $10^6$ B/s = 1 MB/s. $$ R_{app} \cdot \Delta_{HOL} = 10^6 \;\text{B/s} \times 70 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s} = 70\,000\;\text{B} = 70\;\text{KB} $$ $$ B_{min} = 100\;\text{KB} - 70\;\text{KB} = 30\;\text{KB} $$ > **Conclusion:** A 30-KB receive buffer is sufficient; the 256-KB allocation provides ample safety margin. This buffer sizing is why **MPTCP schedulers** (BLEST, DAPS) try to *minimize $\Delta_{HOL}$* by sending late-arriving bytes on faster paths. --- ## 3.3 KTU-Standard Analytical Matrix (Quick-Reference Decision Table) | Parameter | Standard TCP | MPTCP (RFC 8684) | KTU Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Sequence number space | 32-bit, per-flow | 64-bit DSN (connection) + 32-bit SSN (per subflow) | Allows in-order reassembly across paths | | Endpoints required to be multihomed? | No | Yes (at least one side) | MPTCP asymmetric — works if only client is multihomed | | Header overhead (bytes) | 0 (no options) | 12–28 bytes per subflow SYN (MP_CAPABLE/ADD_ADDR) | Slight cost on tiny IoT packets | | Connection-level throughput | $B_{single}$ | $\sum_i B_i$ (upper bound) | Bandwidth aggregation | | Failover time (link break) | ~RTO timeout (seconds) | **MP_FAIL** < 1 RTT (RFC 8524) | Sub-second resilience | | Congestion fairness vs. TCP | Baseline | **Resource-pooled** (LIA/OLIA/BALIA) | No "off-path harm" | | Application API | Standard socket | **Sockets API** is preserved (transparent) | Zero code change in user app | | Mid-box compatibility | Excellent | **Poor without MPTCP-aware middleboxes** | The biggest deployment barrier | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 MPTCP Protocol Stack — Position in the IoT Reference Model The following Mermaid block shows exactly *where* MPTCP lives in the IoT/TCP/IP protocol stack, and the *sub-layer* (subflow vs. connection) split: ```mermaid flowchart TB app["APPLICATION LAYER
HTTP, MQTT, CoAP, RTP"] sock["MPTCP SOCKET
connection level 64 bit DSN
transparent to app"] mpconn["MPTCP CONNECTION LAYER
path manager, packet scheduler
receive buffer reassembly"] sf1["SUBFLOW 1
Wi Fi 192.168.1.10 5000
SSN 32 bit cwnd 1"] sf2["SUBFLOW 2
LTE 10.45.0.7 49152
SSN 32 bit cwnd 2"] sf3["SUBFLOW 3 BACKUP
5G NR 10.99.0.2 62000
MP_PRIO backup"] ip["IP LAYER ROUTING"] l1["LINK LAYER
Wi Fi radio"] l2["LINK LAYER
LTE radio"] l3["LINK LAYER
5G NR radio"] app --> sock sock --> mpconn mpconn --> sf1 mpconn --> sf2 mpconn --> sf3 sf1 --> ip sf2 --> ip sf3 --> ip ip --> l1 ip --> l2 ip --> l3 ``` ## 4.2 MPTCP Handshake — Establishing an MPTCP-Capable Connection This diagram shows the full **3-way handshake with `MP_CAPABLE`** plus the addition of a *second* subflow using `MP_JOIN`. This is a KTU classic 14-mark question ("explain MPTCP connection establishment with a diagram"). ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant C as Client multihomed participant N1 as Path 1 Wi Fi participant N2 as Path 2 LTE participant S as Server multihomed Note over C,S: STEP 1 initial handshake MP_CAPABLE C->>S: SYN seq x MP_CAPABLE csum key CK_S
addresses 2 local 1 remote S->>C: SYN ACK seq y ack x 1 MP_CAPABLE csum key CK_S
addresses 1 local 2 remote C->>S: ACK seq x 1 ack y 1 MP_CAPABLE
connection token exchanged Note over C,S: MPTCP connection established subflow 1 ready Note over C,S: STEP 2 add a second subflow MP_JOIN C->>S: SYN seq x prime MP_JOIN token B rand nonce S->>C: SYN ACK seq z ack x prime MP_JOIN
token B HMAC hash truncated C->>S: ACK ack z MP_JOIN HMAC verified Note over C,S: subflow 2 added over LTE path via N2 ``` ## 4.3 MPTCP Block-Level Functional Architecture Per the protocol specification, MPTCP has **three internal functional blocks** at the sender, mirrored at the receiver. The following diagram shows their interaction: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SENDER PM["PATH MANAGER
Fullmesh Singlepath Backup
RFC 8684 Section 3 4"] PS["PACKET SCHEDULER
RR BLEST ECF DAPS
RFC 8684 Section 3 3"] CC["COUPLED CONGESTION CTRL
LIA OLIA BALIA
RFC 6356"] SF_A["SUBFLOW A cwnd_a rwnd_a"] SF_B["SUBFLOW B cwnd_b rwnd_b"] end APP["APPLICATION
socket write buffer"] subgraph NETWORK NW1["PATH 1 e g Wi Fi"] NW2["PATH 2 e g LTE"] end subgraph RECEIVER RCV_SF_A["SUBFLOW A receiver"] RCV_SF_B["SUBFLOW B receiver"] RBUF["REASSEMBLY BUFFER
maps DSN to SSN
in order delivery"] end APP --> PS PM --> PS PS --> SF_A PS --> SF_B CC -. feedback .-> SF_A CC -. feedback .-> SF_B SF_A --> NW1 SF_B --> NW2 NW1 --> RCV_SF_A NW2 --> RCV_SF_B RCV_SF_A --> RBUF RCV_SF_B --> RBUF ``` ## 4.4 Failover State Machine — What Happens When One Subflow Breaks This is the most-examined state machine in the MPTCP specification (defined in **RFC 8524**): ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> ESTABLISHED ESTABLISHED --> ESTABLISHED : Data ACK
subflow healthy ESTABLISHED --> DETECTING_LOSS : RTO expired or
duplicate ACK threshold DETECTING_LOSS --> SENDING_MP_FAIL : MP_FAIL option sent
on healthy subflow SENDING_MP_FAIL --> PENDING_FAIL_ACK : wait for peer ack PENDING_FAIL_ACK --> SUBFLOW_REMOVED : peer acks MP_FAIL
REMOVE_ADDR sent PENDING_FAIL_ACK --> SENDING_MP_FAIL : retransmit MP_FAIL
up to 3 times SUBFLOW_REMOVED --> ESTABLISHED : other subflow
continues traffic ESTABLISHED --> [*] : connection closed FIN ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define MPTCP. List any two advantages it offers over classical TCP in IoT scenarios. **Model Answer:** Multipath TCP (MPTCP) is a transport-layer extension of TCP standardized in **RFC 8684**, which allows a single end-to-end TCP connection to simultaneously use multiple network paths between two endpoints by establishing multiple subflows over different IP address pairs. **Two advantages in IoT:** 1. **Bandwidth aggregation** — A device with both Wi-Fi and 5G interfaces can sum the throughput of both, increasing total data rate without changing the application. 2. **Resilience to link failure** — Using **MP_FAIL (RFC 8524)**, MPTCP can detect a broken subflow and switch traffic to a healthy one within a single RTT, without dropping the socket connection — critical for connected-vehicle and industrial-automation IoT. *(Any two of: handover, path diversity, resource pooling, multihoming transparency — accepted.)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] What is the role of the `MP_CAPABLE` TCP option in MPTCP? **Model Answer:** The `MP_CAPABLE` option (kind **30**) is sent in the SYN, SYN-ACK, and final ACK of the initial 3-way handshake. Its role is to: 1. **Advertise MPTCP capability** — the endpoints negotiate whether both support MPTCP. 2. **Exchange cryptographic keys** (`sender_key` / `receiver_key`) used to compute the **HMAC-SHA256** for verifying future subflow additions via `MP_JOIN`. 3. **Generate the connection token** — a random 32-bit identifier that uniquely labels this MPTCP connection (since the 4-tuple is no longer unique across subflows). Without successful `MP_CAPABLE` negotiation, the connection falls back to standard TCP — ensuring backward compatibility with legacy middleboxes. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model Paper, Module 4)]* > **(a)** With a neat sequence diagram, explain the MPTCP connection establishment procedure, including the addition of a second subflow using `MP_JOIN`. Mention the relevant TCP option numbers. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **Linked Increases Algorithm (LIA)** for coupled congestion control. Why is plain independent (uncoupled) TCP congestion control on each subflow considered harmful? Derive the steady-state throughput of a 2-subflow MPTCP flow. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) The MPTCP connection is established in **two phases**: **Phase 1 — Initial 3-way handshake with `MP_CAPABLE` (option kind 30).** 1. The **client** sends a `SYN` to the server. The SYN segment carries the `MP_CAPABLE` option, which contains the client's `sender_key`, a checksum algorithm flag, and a list of the client's local IP addresses the server may use to reach it (in `ADD_ADDR` sub-options). 2. The **server** replies with `SYN-ACK`, including its own `MP_CAPABLE` with `receiver_key` and *its* local addresses. 3. The **client** sends a final `ACK` confirming the keys. At this point, both endpoints derive the **connection token** by hashing the keys via HMAC-SHA256 and truncating to 32 bits. Subflow 1 is now ready to carry data. **Phase 2 — Adding a new subflow with `MP_JOIN` (option kind 31).** 4. The **client** opens a *new* TCP subflow (different 4-tuple — e.g., now using the LTE interface) by sending a `SYN` with `MP_JOIN` option. The option contains the *connection token* (so the server can identify the parent MPTCP connection) and a random `nonce`. 5. The **server** replies with `SYN-ACK` carrying `MP_JOIN` plus an HMAC computed from the nonce, the shared keys, and the subflow's random `rand`. 6. The **client** verifies the HMAC and sends a final `ACK` with `MP_JOIN`. If the HMACs match, the new subflow is attached to the existing MPTCP connection. **TCP option numbers:** - `MP_CAPABLE` = **30** (0x1E) - `MP_JOIN` = **31** (0x1F) - `ADD_ADDR` = **29** (0x1D) - `MP_FAIL` = **26** (0x1A) — used in failover > **Reference diagram:** see **Section 4.2** above — the same MP_CAPABLE → MP_JOIN sequence is shown as a Mermaid sequence diagram. Students should reproduce a *hand-drawn* version in the exam. #### Model Solution for (b) **Why uncoupled congestion control is harmful:** If MPTCP ran *vanilla* TCP Reno/Cubic on each subflow independently, then a 2-path MPTCP flow would send as aggressively as **two competing single-path TCPs**. On the *shared* bottleneck link (the link common to both paths — e.g., the server's ISP uplink), MPTCP would thus occupy **2× the fair share** that a single TCP would, *starving* competing single-path flows. This is called the **"off-path harm" problem** — MPTCP would unfairly seize the bottleneck's bandwidth. **LIA (Linked Increases Algorithm) — RFC 6356:** LIA imposes the **resource-pooling principle**: an MPTCP flow, on each ACK, increases its window on subflow $i$ by an amount that is *coupled* to the total window and to the relative RTTs. Specifically: $$ cwnd_i \leftarrow cwnd_i + \min\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{cwnd_{total}},\ \frac{1}{cwnd_i}\right) $$ where $$ \alpha = cwnd_{total}\cdot\frac{\max_k\!\left(\dfrac{cwnd_k}{RTT_k^2}\right)}{\left(\sum_k \dfrac{cwnd_k}{RTT_k}\right)^2} $$ On a single loss on subflow $i$: $$ cwnd_i \leftarrow \frac{cwnd_i}{2} $$ **Steady-state throughput derivation (2 subflows):** In steady state, the *increase* and *decrease* of $cwnd_i$ balance: $$ \frac{\alpha}{cwnd_{total}} \approx \frac{cwnd_i}{2}\cdot p_i $$ Summing over both subflows $i = 1, 2$ and applying the definition of $\alpha$, one obtains: $$ T_{MPTCP} = \sum_i \frac{cwnd_i}{RTT_i} \approx \sum_i \frac{1}{RTT_i\sqrt{p_i}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \cdot \text{constant}}} $$ For a simplified form (assuming both subflows have equal loss probability $p$): $$ T_{MPTCP} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left(\frac{1}{RTT_1 \sqrt{p}} + \frac{1}{RTT_2 \sqrt{p}}\right) $$ The $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ prefactor is what ensures MPTCP is **not more aggressive** than a single TCP at the bottleneck — it gets *aggregate* throughput by using multiple paths, but on *each* bottleneck, it behaves like a *single* TCP. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model Paper, Module 4)]* > **(a)** List and explain any **four** packet scheduling algorithms used in MPTCP. Compare their behavior in the presence of heterogeneous RTTs. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A smartphone opens an MPTCP connection over Wi-Fi and LTE. The Wi-Fi path has $RTT_1 = 30$ ms and the LTE path has $RTT_2 = 90$ ms. Both subflows have $cwnd = 10$ segments and segment size = 1460 bytes. Compute (i) the per-subflow throughput, (ii) the aggregate throughput, and (iii) the value of the LIA aggressiveness factor $\alpha$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) | Algorithm | Strategy | Best for | Worst-case behavior | |---|---|---|---| | **Round-Robin (RR)** | Alternate segments one-by-one across subflows | Low-RTT-difference, loss-free links | Heavy HOL blocking when RTTs diverge; bursts of retransmissions on the slower path | | **Lowest-RTT-First (Default in Linux kernel)** | Always send on the subflow with the smallest smoothed RTT (until its cwnd fills) | Homogeneous-loss networks | Buffering on slow path inflates RTT; risk of starvation for backup subflow | | **BLEST (Blocking Estimation-based Scheduler)** | Estimates *blocking probability* on each path using cwnd/RTT ratio; sends on a faster path *if* it would not cause extra HOL blocking | IoT, Wi-Fi+LTE combos | Needs accurate cwnd tracking; still has Owd variance issues | | **ECF (Earliest Completion First)** | Compute *expected* finish time $EFT_i = t + \frac{remaining\_bytes}{cwnd_i/RTT_i}$ per subflow; pick the smallest | Maximizing *throughput* in bulk transfers | Stale RTT estimates → wrong prediction; high computational cost | | **DAPS (Delay-Aware Packet Scheduler, RFC 8714-inspired)** | Uses a *virtual* estimate of when each segment would arrive at the receiver and pushes late ones *earlier* | Bursty / interactive traffic with strict deadlines | Requires fine-grained timestamp exchange | In **heterogeneous-RTT** scenarios (e.g., 5G at 20 ms + LEO satellite at 600 ms), the default Lowest-RTT-First scheduler will *monopolize* the low-RTT path, while the high-RTT path sits idle. **ECF** would re-balance correctly, while **BLEST** would only occasionally use the slow path to fill the receive buffer. #### Model Solution for (b) **Given:** $RTT_1 = 30$ ms, $RTT_2 = 90$ ms, $cwnd_1 = cwnd_2 = 10$ segments, MSS = 1460 bytes. **(i) Per-subflow throughput (bytes/s):** $$ T_1 = \frac{cwnd_1 \cdot MSS}{RTT_1} = \frac{10 \times 1460}{30 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{14\,600}{0.03} = 486\,666.67\;\text{B/s} \approx 3.89\;\text{Mbps} $$ $$ T_2 = \frac{cwnd_2 \cdot MSS}{RTT_2} = \frac{10 \times 1460}{90 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{14\,600}{0.09} = 162\,222.22\;\text{B/s} \approx 1.30\;\text{Mbps} $$ **(ii) Aggregate throughput:** $$ T_{MP} = T_1 + T_2 = 486\,666.67 + 162\,222.22 = 648\,888.89\;\text{B/s} \approx 5.19\;\text{Mbps} $$ **(iii) Compute $\alpha$:** $$ cwnd_{total} = cwnd_1 + cwnd_2 = 10 + 10 = 20 $$ Compute the per-subflow $cwnd_i / RTT_i^2$ terms: $$ \frac{cwnd_1}{RTT_1^2} = \frac{10}{(0.03)^2} = \frac{10}{0.0009} \approx 11\,111.11 $$ $$ \frac{cwnd_2}{RTT_2^2} = \frac{10}{(0.09)^2} = \frac{10}{0.0081} \approx 1\,234.57 $$ Maximum = $11\,111.11$ (Wi-Fi path). Compute the sum $\sum \frac{cwnd_i}{RTT_i}$: $$ \frac{cwnd_1}{RTT_1} = \frac{10}{0.03} \approx 333.33 $$ $$ \frac{cwnd_2}{RTT_2} = \frac{10}{0.09} \approx 111.11 $$ $$ \sum \frac{cwnd_i}{RTT_i} = 333.33 + 111.11 = 444.44 $$ $$ \left(\sum \frac{cwnd_i}{RTT_i}\right)^2 = (444.44)^2 \approx 197\,528 $$ Now: $$ \alpha = 20 \times \frac{11\,111.11}{197\,528} = \frac{222\,222.2}{197\,528} \approx 1.125 $$ Since $\alpha > 1$, LIA is correctly **resource-pooled**: the Wi-Fi subflow (the bottleneck, with smaller RTT) is allowed to grow slightly faster than independent TCP would — this is the bandwidth made available by the *additional* LTE subflow. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!** > 1. **Do not confuse `MP_CAPABLE` (option 30) and `MP_JOIN` (option 31).** Examiners *deliberately* swap these in Part A questions to test your reading. Memo: **30 is for the FIRST** subflow, **31 is for additional** subflows. > 2. **Forgetting to include the `ADD_ADDR` option number (29).** Many students list only 30 and 31; full credit requires *all four* of 26/27/29/30/31 from RFC 8684. > 3. **Writing "MPTCP = Multipath UDP".** It is **TCP-based**, not UDP. Half a mark is deducted for this common slip. > 4. **Treating LIA as the *only* coupled algorithm.** RFC 6356 specifies *three* algorithms: **LIA, OLIA (RFC 6356, an opportunistic variant)**, and **BALIA (RFC 6346-style)**. Mentioning at least LIA and OLIA is required for full marks. > 5. **Skipping the units in the throughput computation.** Always write "in Mbps" or "in KB/s" — an answer with bare numbers loses one mark. > 6. **Failing to cite RFC numbers.** KTU 2024 examiners are trained to look for **RFC 8684 (MPTCPv1)** and **RFC 8524 (MP_FAIL)**. Memorize these two. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **MPTCP** = Multipath TCP, an extension defined in **RFC 8684** (v1) / **RFC 6824** (v0, historical). Always cite v1. - It splits one TCP connection into **multiple subflows** over different 4-tuples. The application sees a normal socket. - The three internal modules: **Path Manager** (RFC 8684 §3.4), **Packet Scheduler** (§3.3), **Coupled Congestion Control** (RFC 6356). - The five TCP options you must memorize: - `MP_CAPABLE` = **30**, `MP_JOIN` = **31**, `ADD_ADDR` = **29**, `REMOVE_ADDR` = **28**, `MP_PRIO` = **27**, `MP_FAIL` = **26**. - **Sequence numbers:** 64-bit DSN (connection-level) + 32-bit SSN (per-subflow). - **Throughput per subflow:** $\displaystyle T_i = \frac{cwnd_i}{RTT_i}$. Aggregate: sum across subflows. - **LIA aggressiveness factor:** $$ \alpha = cwnd_{total}\cdot\frac{\max_k\!\left(\dfrac{cwnd_k}{RTT_k^2}\right)}{\left(\sum_k \dfrac{cwnd_k}{RTT_k}\right)^2} $$ - **LIA increase:** $cwnd_i \leftarrow cwnd_i + \min\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{cwnd_{total}},\ \dfrac{1}{cwnd_i}\right)$. - **LIA decrease:** $cwnd_i \leftarrow cwnd_i / 2$ on loss. - **Resource pooling principle:** MPTCP must *not* be more aggressive than a single-path TCP on any shared bottleneck. - **Fast failover:** `MP_FAIL` option (RFC 8524) detects subflow failure within **one RTT** — vs. classical TCP's RTO timeout of seconds. - **Schedulers:** Round-Robin, Lowest-RTT-First (Linux default), **BLEST**, **ECF**, **DAPS**. - **IoT benefits:** bandwidth aggregation, seamless handover, path diversity, no application change. - **Biggest deployment barrier:** middleboxes (firewalls, NATs, load balancers) that drop unknown TCP options — solvable with **MPTCP-aware proxy / OSS-waypoint** (RFC 8803). - **MPTCP deployment examples:** Apple iOS (since iOS 7, 2013), Korean carriers (KT, SKT), Tessares/CableLabs in DOCSIS, Samsung Galaxy S-series with select carriers. - **Standard citation bundle for KTU answers:** RFC 8684 (main), RFC 8524 (MP_FAIL), RFC 6356 (congestion control), RFC 8803 (ossification / proxy). ---

topics.title: DCCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIT601 - IOT – ARCHITECTURE, PROTOCOLS AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** DCCP ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Datagram Congestion Control Protocol (DCCP)** is a **transport-layer protocol** standardized by the **IETF in RFC 4340** that provides **unreliable, connection-oriented, congestion-controlled datagram delivery** for applications that require **timely delivery** rather than perfect reliability. It is formally positioned **between TCP (reliable, congestion-controlled) and UDP (unreliable, uncontrolled)** on the transport-layer spectrum. DCCP is engineered to carry traffic such as **streaming media, VoIP, online gaming, and IPTV**, where retransmitting an old lost packet is **worse than dropping it** (because the playback time has already passed). Hence, DCCP drops packets instead of resending them, but still uses **feedback acknowledgments** to compute the available bandwidth and avoid flooding the network. > [!NOTE] > **Key Identification Metrics** > - **Protocol Number (IP):** **33** > - **Standardizing Body:** **IETF** > - **Primary RFC:** **RFC 4340** (and RFCs 4341, 4342 for CCIDs) > - **Default Port Range:** **33 (unassigned)**, application-specific > - **Reliability Class:** **Unreliable, but congestion-controlled** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Live Sports Radio Announcer"** > Imagine you are listening to a live cricket commentary on the radio. The commentator cannot pause the match to "re-say" the ball that was just bowled 3 seconds ago. If a word gets garbled, you simply **miss it and move on**, but the radio station **constantly monitors how many listeners are tuned in** and adjusts the broadcast power to avoid jamming the airwaves. > > - **UDP** would be the analogy of a commentator who speaks as loudly as possible, regardless of the airwave congestion (no congestion control). > - **TCP** would be the analogy of a letter-writer who re-sends every page that gets lost (perfect reliability, but delayed). > - **DCCP** is the **radio announcer** – it drops garbled syllables (no retransmission) but cooperatively lowers its volume when the airwaves get busy (congestion control). ### 1.3 Why DCCP Exists — The Design Gap The original Internet transport layer offered only two extremes: | Protocol | Reliability | Congestion Control | Delivery Semantics | |----------|------------|--------------------|---------------------| | **TCP** | Reliable (retransmits) | Yes | Byte-stream, in-order | | **UDP** | Unreliable (no retransmits) | **No** | Message-based, best-effort | DCCP fills the missing third quadrant: **unreliable + congestion-controlled**, which is exactly what real-time media needs. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Architectural Features of DCCP DCCP is structured around **five core design features** that the KTU examiner typically tests: 1. **Unreliable Datagram Service** — Lost packets are NOT retransmitted. The application receives "congestion events" but no guarantee of arrival. 2. **Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN) Support** — Receivers can mark packets as experiencing congestion without dropping them, providing **two feedback levels** (CE = Congestion Experienced, ECN-Echo). 3. **Pluggable Congestion Control IDs (CCIDs)** — DCCP is **modular**; the congestion control algorithm is decoupled from the protocol itself. 4. **Connection-Oriented Handshake** — Unlike UDP, DCCP establishes a stateful connection using **DCCP-Request, DCCP-Response, and ACK packets** (a 3-way style handshake). 5. **Built-in Path MTU Discovery and Small Header** — Includes a checksum coverage field and a fixed-length short header option for low-bandwidth links (very relevant to **IoT**). ### 2.2 Congestion Control IDs (CCIDs) — The Heart of DCCP > [!IMPORTANT] > **The two standardized CCIDs tested in KTU 2024 are CCID 2 and CCID 3.** | Feature | **CCID 2 (RFC 4341) — "TCP-Like"** | **CCID 3 (RFC 4342) — "TCP-Friendly Rate Control"** | |---------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------| | **Algorithm** | AIMD (Additive-Increase, Multiplicative-Decrease) | TFRC (equation-based) | | **Congestion Window** | Maintains `cwnd` like TCP | Maintains a **sending rate** `X` | | **Reaction to Loss** | Halves the window (multiplicative decrease) | Smoothly reduces the sending rate | | **Rate Oscillation** | **Saw-tooth** (oscillates aggressively) | **Smooth** (stable, predictable) | | **Best Suited For** | Elastic, bulk transfer-style traffic | **Streaming media, VoIP, IPTV** | | **Fairness with TCP** | TCP-competitive | **TCP-friendly** | | **Feedback Frequency** | Ack for every packet (or partial) | Periodic feedback packets | ### 2.3 DCCP Connection State Machine (High-Yield) DCCP transitions through these states, frequently asked in KTU short-answer questions: `CLOSED → LISTEN → REQUEST → RESPOND → PARTOPEN → OPEN → CLOSING → CLOSED` (graceful) `OPEN → TIMEWAIT → CLOSED` (post-graceful) `Any State → CLOSED` (via DCCP-Reset, abrupt close) ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following equations are **mathematically verifiable** from RFC 4342 and are the ones a KTU student is expected to memorize for numerical/application problems in transport-layer congestion control. | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | |---------|----------------------|-------------------| | **TCP Throughput (Padhye Model)** | $$B \;=\; \dfrac{MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2p}{3}} + T_0 \cdot \min\!\left(1, 3\sqrt{\dfrac{3p}{8}}\right) p \cdot (1 + 32p^2)}$$ | $B$ = throughput (Bps), $MSS$ = max segment size (bytes), $RTT$ = round-trip time (s), $p$ = loss probability, $T_0$ = retransmission timeout (s) | | **Simplified TFRC Throughput (CCID 3)** | $$X \;=\; \dfrac{8 \cdot MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2p}{3}}}$$ | $X$ = allowed sending rate (Bps) | | **AIMD Increase (CCID 2)** | $$cwnd_{t+1} \;=\; cwnd_t + \dfrac{MSS}{cwnd_t}$$ | Slow-start equivalent in bytes | | **AIMD Decrease (CCID 2)** | $$cwnd_{new} \;=\; \dfrac{cwnd_{old}}{2}$$ | On detection of a single loss event | | **DCCP Header Size (Fixed)** | $$H_{min} \;=\; 12 \text{ bytes} \; (96 \text{ bits})$$ | Without options | | **DCCP Header (with Short Seq)** | $$H_{short} \;=\; 16 \text{ bytes}$$ | When using 24-bit DataAck Seq numbers | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility in IoT & Engineering - **Smart IP Cameras & CCTV** — DCCP streams live video over a wireless network without retransmission-induced "buffer bloat". - **Voice over IP (VoIP) in Industrial Telephony** — Avoids the "TCP meltdown" problem of VoIP-over-TCP. - **Online Multiplayer Gaming** — CCID 3 provides smooth bandwidth instead of TCP's saw-tooth pattern. - **IoT Video Doorbells, Drones** — Latency-sensitive telemetry where stale data is useless. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: DCCP Short Header Format (RFC 4340) The DCCP header is **variable-length** depending on whether it carries a 48-bit or 24-bit sequence number. The following derivation walks through the bit-allocation of the **Generic Header (12 bytes minimum)**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Generic Header} &= \underbrace{16}_{Src\ Port} + \underbrace{16}_{Dst\ Port} + \underbrace{8}_{Data\ Offset} + \underbrace{8}_{CCVal \cup CsCov} + \underbrace{16}_{Checksum} + \underbrace{8}_{Res \cup Type} \\[6pt] &= 16 + 16 + 8 + 8 + 16 + 8 \\[6pt] &= 72\ \text{bits} = 9\ \text{bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic (Step-by-Step):** - **Step 1:** Add the `Source Port` (16 bits) — the sending application's port. - **Step 2:** Add the `Destination Port` (16 bits) — the receiving application's port. - **Step 3:** Add the `Data Offset` (8 bits) — the length of the DCCP header in 32-bit words. - **Step 4:** Add the `CCVal` (4 bits) + `CsCov` (4 bits) packed into one byte — congestion control validation flag and checksum-coverage length. - **Step 5:** Add the `Checksum` (16 bits) — Internet checksum covering the header and a selectable portion of the payload. - **Step 6:** Add the `Reserved` (3 bits) + `Type` (4 bits) + `X` (1 bit) packed into one byte — packet-type identifier (Request, Response, Data, DataAck, etc.). - **Step 7:** Sum the bit-widths: 16+16+8+8+16+8 = **72 bits = 9 bytes**. Now we add the **Sequence Number / Acknowledgment Number Subheader** depending on packet type: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SeqNum-48 (Data)} &= 48\ \text{bits} \Rightarrow H_{data} = 9 + 6 = 15\ \text{bytes (rounded to 16)} \\[4pt] \text{SeqNum-24 / AckNum-48 (DataAck)} &= 24 + 48 = 72\ \text{bits} \Rightarrow H_{dataAck} = 9 + 9 = 18\ \text{bytes} \\[4pt] \text{SeqNum-24 / AckNum-24 (Request, Response, etc.)} &= 24 + 24 = 48\ \text{bits} \Rightarrow H_{req} = 9 + 6 = 15\ \text{bytes (rounded to 16)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: CCID 3 Sending Rate Update The TFRC equation updates the **allowed sending rate $X$** in Bytes/second as a function of loss event rate $p$: $$ \begin{aligned} X(p) &= \dfrac{8 \cdot MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2p}{3}} + \bigl(RTO \cdot 4p(1+32p^2)\bigr)} \\[10pt] \text{Simplified form (used in KTU problems):} \quad X &\approx \dfrac{8 \cdot MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2p}{3}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic (Step-by-Step):** - **Step 1 — Identify the inputs.** Given an MSS of 1500 bytes, an RTT of 100 ms = 0.1 s, and an observed loss event rate $p$ = 0.01 (i.e., 1% of packets lost). - **Step 2 — Compute the denominator's first term.** $\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.01}{3}} = \sqrt{0.00666...} \approx 0.0816$. - **Step 3 — Multiply by RTT.** $0.1 \times 0.0816 = 0.00816$ seconds. - **Step 4 — Compute the numerator.** $8 \times 1500 = 12000$ bits equivalent per packet (DCCP uses 8 bits = 1 byte; the *8 factor converts to bits). - **Step 5 — Divide.** $X = \dfrac{12000}{0.00816} \approx 1{,}470{,}588\ \text{bits/sec} \approx \mathbf{1.47\ Mbps}$. - **Step 6 — Interpretation.** With only 1% packet loss, TFRC authorizes a CCID 3 sender to push ~1.47 Mbps of media — a **smooth**, **TCP-friendly** rate, rather than TCP's halved window. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favorite) | Parameter | **TCP (RFC 9293)** | **UDP (RFC 768)** | **DCCP (RFC 4340)** | |-----------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------------| | Connection Setup | 3-way handshake | None | 3-way style (Req/Resp/Ack) | | Reliability | Full retransmission | None | None (but reports loss) | | Congestion Control | Mandatory (AIMD/Reno/Cubic) | None | Pluggable (CCID 2/3) | | ECN Support | Yes | No | **Yes (4 congestion levels)** | | Header Size | 20 bytes min | 8 bytes | **12–18 bytes** | | In-order Delivery | Yes | No | **No** | | Best For IoT Use | File transfer, MQTT-over-TCP | DNS, SNMP traps | **Live video, VoIP, gaming** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Insight:** The reason DCCP is often called **"TCP without retransmission"** is because it inherits **TCP's congestion-handling discipline** but strips away the **reliability and ordering guarantees**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DCCP Generic Header Layout (Bit-Level Block Diagram) ```mermaid block-beta columns 12 SrcPort["Src Port
16b"]:2 DstPort["Dst Port
16b"]:2 DataOff["Data Offset
8b"]:1 CCC["CCVal 4b | CsCov 4b"]:1 ChkSum["Checksum
16b"]:2 RXT["Res 3b | Type 4b | X 1b"]:1 SeqNum["Seq Num
48b OR 24b"]:3 AckNum["Ack Num
0 OR 48b"]:3 Options["Options
Variable"]:3 Payload["Payload
Variable"]:2 ``` **Reading Guide:** The first six blocks form the **Generic Header (72 bits = 9 bytes)**. The `SeqNum` and `AckNum` blocks are present only in specific packet types. `Options` carries DCCP-specific options like `Timestamp`, `Elapsed Time`, `CCID-Preferences`, etc. ### 4.2 DCCP Connection Lifecycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["State CLOSED"] --> B["State LISTEN
Server binds port"] B --> C["Client sends
DCCP-Request"] C --> D["Server enters
RESPOND state"] D --> E["Server sends
DCCP-Response"] E --> F["Client sends
DCCP-Ack
enters OPEN"] D --> G["Server enters
PARTOPEN on first data"] F --> H["State OPEN
Bidirectional data"] G --> H H --> I["DCCP-Data +
DCCP-DataAck
feedback"] I --> J{"Loss detected
or ECN-CE?"} J -->|"Yes"| K["Apply CCID 2 or 3
update cwnd or rate X"] K --> I J -->|"No"| L["Steady streaming"] L --> I I --> M["DCCP-CloseReq"] M --> N["DCCP-Close"] N --> O["State TIMEWAIT"] O --> P["State CLOSED"] H -->|"Abrupt close"| Q["DCCP-Reset
forces CLOSED"] Q --> P ``` ### 4.3 CCID Architecture — Pluggable Congestion Control ```mermaid flowchart LR App["Application Layer
VoIP / Video / Game"] --> DCCP["DCCP Core
RFC 4340"] DCCP --> CCID2{"CCID 2
TCP-Like AIMD"} DCCP --> CCID3{"CCID 3
TFRC Equation"} DCCP --> Custom["Future CCID
Pluggable"] CCID2 --> Net["Network Layer
IP / ECN"] CCID3 --> Net Custom --> Net Net -.Feedback.-> DCCP ``` > [!NOTE] > The dotted **Feedback** arrow represents **DCCP-DataAck** packets (or **DCCP-Ack** for control packets) flowing back from the receiver to the sender, carrying loss indications and ECN-Echoed bits. This feedback loop is what enables DCCP to do congestion control **without** doing retransmission. ### 4.4 DCCP in the IoT Protocol Stack ```mermaid block-beta columns 1 block:App["Application Layer
RTSP, RTP, SIP, Gaming"] columns 1 App1["Live Media Payload"] end block:Trans["Transport Layer"] columns 3 TransA["DCCP"] TransB["TCP"] TransC["UDP"] end block:Net["Network Layer
IPv4 / IPv6 + ECN"] end block:Link["Link Layer
Wi-Fi / Ethernet / LoRa-WAN"] end App --> Trans Trans --> Net Net --> Link ``` **Reading Guide:** Notice DCCP occupies the **same layer as TCP and UDP**, positioned specifically for **real-time, time-bounded IoT media** (camera streams, drone telemetry, VoIP assistants). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Q1.** Define the **Datagram Congestion Control Protocol (DCCP)**. Mention **two key features** that distinguish it from UDP. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **DCCP** (RFC 4340) is a **transport-layer protocol** that provides **unreliable, connection-oriented, congestion-controlled** datagram delivery. It is designed for **time-sensitive applications** such as streaming media, VoIP, and online gaming. > > **Two features distinguishing DCCP from UDP:** > 1. **Congestion Control:** DCCP actively regulates its sending rate using pluggable CCID 2 or CCID 3 algorithms, while UDP sends at any rate the application chooses. > 2. **Connection Establishment:** DCCP performs a **3-way-style handshake** (Request → Response → Ack) to set up state, while UDP is fully connectionless. > 3. (Bonus) **ECN support and built-in feedback** via DCCP-DataAck packets. --- **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]** **Q2.** What is the role of **CCVal** and **CsCov** fields in the DCCP generic header? **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **CCVal (4 bits):** Validates the **Congestion Control ID** negotiation. The receiver echoes back the CCVal from the request to confirm which CCID is in use. If CCVal is set to zero, the field is **invalid/ignored** in that packet. > - **CsCov (4 bits):** Indicates the **length of the header portion covered by the checksum**, in 32-bit words. This lets the receiver decide **how much of the payload** is checksummed — a useful optimization for lossy real-time flows where re-checking the entire payload is wasteful. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Question]** **Question A (14 Marks):** > **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **DCCP packet types** in detail with a neat diagram of the **Generic Header Format**. > > **(b)** [7 marks] Compare and contrast **CCID 2 (TCP-Like)** and **CCID 3 (TFRC)** congestion control mechanisms used in DCCP. Which one is **preferred for VoIP** and why? **Model Answer (Part A-a — 7 marks):** The DCCP protocol uses **10 standardized packet types** (Type field values 0–15, some reserved): | Type | Packet Name | Purpose | |------|-------------|---------| | **0** | DCCP-Request | Initiates connection (client → server) | | **1** | DCCP-Response | Server's reply to a Request | | **2** | DCCP-Data | Pure data packet with 48-bit SeqNo | | **3** | DCCP-DataAck | Data with feedback (24-bit Seq + 48-bit Ack) | | **4** | DCCP-Ack | Pure acknowledgment (no payload) | | **5** | DCCP-CloseReq | Polite close request from one side | | **6** | DCCP-Close | Full graceful close | | **7** | DCCP-Reset | Abrupt connection termination | | **8** | DCCP-Sync | Resynchronize after loss of state | | **9** | DCCP-SyncAck | Acknowledgment of Sync | The **Generic Header (12 bytes minimum, padded to 16)** contains: `Source Port (16) | Destination Port (16) | Data Offset (8) | CCVal (4) + CsCov (4) | Checksum (16) | Reserved (3) + Type (4) + X (1) | [Seq Number 24/48] | [Ack Number 0/48]` **Model Answer (Part A-b — 7 marks):** | Property | **CCID 2** | **CCID 3** | |----------|------------|------------| | Algorithm | AIMD | TFRC (equation-based) | | State Variable | `cwnd` (window) | `X` (rate in Bps) | | On Loss | `cwnd = cwnd / 2` (sharp drop) | Smooth rate reduction via $X(p)$ equation | | On No Loss | `cwnd += MSS / cwnd` (slow ramp) | Increase $X$ slowly | | Rate Profile | **Saw-tooth / oscillating** | **Smooth / stable** | | TCP Friendliness | **TCP-competitive** | **TCP-friendly** | | Use Case | Bulk, elastic apps | **VoIP, IPTV, gaming** | | Feedback Needed | Every packet or partial | Periodic feedback packets | | Standard | RFC 4341 | RFC 4342 | **Preferred for VoIP: CCID 3**, because VoIP codecs produce a **constant bitrate (CBR)** stream (e.g., 64 kbps G.711). A saw-tooth CCID 2 rate would cause **audible jitter and buffer underruns** at the receiver, whereas CCID 3's smooth, predictable rate keeps the playback buffer stable. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** > **(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a **state diagram**, describe the **DCCP connection establishment and termination** procedure. > > **(b)** [7 marks] A DCCP sender using **CCID 3 (TFRC)** measures an **RTT of 200 ms**, an **MSS of 1000 bytes**, and a **packet loss event rate $p = 0.02$**. Compute the **allowed sending rate $X$** using the simplified TFRC equation. What happens to the rate if the loss probability **quadruples** to $p = 0.08$? **Model Answer (Part B-a — 7 marks):** **DCCP Connection Establishment (3-way style):** 1. **Client (CLOSED → REQUEST):** Sends a `DCCP-Request` packet with a 24-bit sequence number and a `CCID-Preferences` option listing the supported CCIDs. 2. **Server (LISTEN → RESPOND):** Replies with a `DCCP-Response` echoing the chosen CCID and setting its own sequence number. 3. **Client (REQUEST → OPEN):** Sends a `DCCP-Ack` confirming the response, transitioning to OPEN. 4. **Server (RESPOND → PARTOPEN):** Enters PARTOPEN upon receiving the first data packet. It transitions to OPEN when its Ack is acknowledged. **DCCP Connection Termination (two variants):** - **Graceful Close:** Either side sends `DCCP-CloseReq`; the other side replies with `DCCP-Close`; both enter `TIMEWAIT` and finally `CLOSED`. - **Abrupt Close:** Either side sends `DCCP-Reset` to forcibly tear down the connection — used on unrecoverable error or CCID failure. **State Diagram (mermaid equivalent):** `CLOSED → LISTEN → RESPOND → PARTOPEN → OPEN → CLOSING/TIMEWAIT → CLOSED` With an out-of-band arrow from `OPEN → CLOSED` triggered by `DCCP-Reset`. **Model Answer (Part B-b — 7 marks):** **Given:** - $MSS = 1000$ bytes - $RTT = 200\ ms = 0.2$ seconds - $p = 0.02$ **Step 1 — Apply the simplified TFRC formula:** $$X = \dfrac{8 \cdot MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2p}{3}}}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the inner term:** $$\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.02}{3}} = \sqrt{0.01333...} \approx 0.11547$$ **Step 3 — Multiply by RTT:** $$0.2 \times 0.11547 = 0.023094\ \text{seconds}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the numerator:** $$8 \times 1000 = 8000\ \text{bits}$$ **Step 5 — Divide:** $$X = \dfrac{8000}{0.023094} \approx 346{,}410\ \text{bps} \approx \mathbf{346.4\ kbps}$$ **Step 6 — Repeat for $p = 0.08$ (4× loss):** $$\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.08}{3}} = \sqrt{0.05333...} \approx 0.23094$$ $$X = \dfrac{8000}{0.2 \times 0.23094} = \dfrac{8000}{0.04619} \approx 173{,}205\ \text{bps} \approx \mathbf{173.2\ kbps}$$ **Step 7 — Inference:** When the loss rate **quadruples**, the allowed sending rate **roughly halves** (346.4 → 173.2 kbps). This is the defining property of TFRC: rate decreases **proportional to $\sqrt{1/p}$**, giving a smooth, gradual slowdown rather than TCP's abrupt halving of the window. This is why CCID 3 is preferred for **steady, predictable media streams** in IoT camera and VoIP systems. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks)** > 1. **Confusing CCID numbers:** CCID **2 = TCP-Like** (AIMD), CCID **3 = TFRC**. Reversing them is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. > 2. **Forgetting the "8" multiplier** in the TFRC throughput formula — it converts bytes to bits. > 3. **Mis-stating the header size** as 8 bytes (UDP) or 20 bytes (TCP); the DCCP generic header is **9 bytes rounded to 12 or 16**. > 4. **Claiming DCCP is reliable** — it is intentionally **unreliable**; it only does congestion control, not retransmission. > 5. **Omitting the connection handshake** — DCCP is **NOT** connectionless like UDP; it has a 3-way-style setup. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam)** - [x] **DCCP = RFC 4340**, **IP Protocol Number = 33**. - [x] DCCP provides **unreliable, congestion-controlled, connection-oriented** datagram service. - [x] It sits **between TCP (reliable) and UDP (uncontrolled)** in the transport layer. - [x] DCCP supports **ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification)** for early congestion signals. - [x] **CCID 2** (RFC 4341) uses **AIMD** like TCP — window-based, aggressive, saw-tooth rate. - [x] **CCID 3** (RFC 4342) uses **TFRC** — rate-based, smooth, TCP-friendly; ideal for **VoIP & streaming media**. - [x] TFRC throughput equation: $X = \dfrac{8 \cdot MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{2p/3}}$ — memorize this verbatim. - [x] Generic header fields: **Src Port, Dst Port, Data Offset, CCVal, CsCov, Checksum, Reserved, Type, X**. - [x] Packet types: **Request, Response, Data, DataAck, Ack, CloseReq, Close, Reset, Sync, SyncAck**. - [x] Connection states: **CLOSED → LISTEN → REQUEST → RESPOND → PARTOPEN → OPEN → CLOSING → TIMEWAIT → CLOSED**. - [x] DCCP is **ideal for IoT video/voice/real-time telemetry** but **NOT** for file transfer or reliable command/control. - [x] It uses **pluggable CCIDs**, allowing future algorithms to be added without changing the base protocol. - [x] DCCP **does NOT retransmit** lost packets — it merely **reports** them via Ack packets. - [x] Header minimum size = **9 bytes** (padded to **12** or **16** depending on Seq/Ack fields). > **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question says *"Discuss a transport protocol for real-time IoT media"*, the expected one-paragraph answer is **DCCP** — mention RFC 4340, CCID 3 (TFRC), ECN support, and the unreliable-but-congestion-controlled nature. You will secure full marks.

topics.title: SCTP) - (TLS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIT601 - IOT – ARCHITECTURE, PROTOCOLS AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** SCTP) - (TLS) # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to the Transport & Security Layer Protocols in IoT In the **IoT protocol stack**, after the application and network layers, we depend heavily on two critical transport and security layer mechanisms: **SCTP (Stream Control Transmission Protocol)** as a robust transport-layer alternative to TCP/UDP, and **TLS (Transport Layer Security)** as the cryptographic backbone that secures IoT communications against eavesdropping, tampering, and impersonation. The 2024 KTU syllabus groups these two together because they jointly address **reliable message delivery + end-to-end security**, both of which are non-negotiable in mission-critical IoT deployments (smart grids, industrial control, vehicular networks, healthcare telemetry). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** Students must be able to explain the **multi-streaming and multi-homing features of SCTP**, the **4-way association handshake**, the **TLS record and handshake protocols**, and the relevance of **DTLS (Datagram TLS)** for UDP-based IoT protocols such as CoAP and MQTT-SN. --- ## 1.2 What is SCTP? — Formal Definition **SCTP (Stream Control Transmission Protocol)** is a **connection-oriented, message-oriented, reliable, full-duplex transport-layer protocol** defined by the IETF in **RFC 4960**. It is standardized as a transport layer protocol (same layer as TCP and UDP) in the **OSI / TCP-IP model**, originally designed to carry **SS7 (Signalling System No. 7)** telephony signalling over IP networks, but later adopted widely in **IoT, LTE/4G, and 5G core networks (S1-AP, X2-AP, NGAP interfaces)**. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (RFC 4960):** "SCTP is a reliable transport protocol operating on top of a connectionless packet network such as IP. It offers the reliable, ordered, and error-free transmission of messages (datagrams) between two endpoints, while providing a means for each endpoint to declare multiple IP addresses through which it can be reached." --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: SCTP as a "Multi-Track Bullet Train" Imagine a **single bullet train reservation** between two cities: - **TCP analogy** = A train track with **one single track**. If a single packet (a wagon) derails, the whole train is delayed (head-of-line blocking). - **SCTP analogy** = A railway station with **multiple parallel tracks (multi-streaming)** running between the same two cities, AND each city has **two railway stations (multi-homing)** so if one station floods/closes, trains automatically divert to the backup station. In this analogy: - **Streams** = independent parallel tracks. Loss on one track does NOT block the others. - **Multi-homing** = each endpoint having redundant network interfaces. If primary Wi-Fi fails, the link automatically fails over to cellular. - **Chunks (messages)** = the actual train wagons. SCTP transmits **discrete messages**, not a continuous byte-stream. --- ## 1.4 What is TLS? — Formal Definition **TLS (Transport Layer Security)** is a **cryptographic protocol** that provides **confidentiality, integrity, and authentication** for communications over an untrusted network (typically the Internet). It operates as a **layer between the transport layer (TCP) and the application layer**. The current standardized version is **TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018)**, which is mandatory for all IoT devices shipping under KTU 2024-aligned curricula. > [!IMPORTANT] > **DTLS (Datagram TLS — RFC 6347/9147)** is the **UDP-based variant of TLS**, specifically engineered for IoT protocols like **CoAP, MQTT-SN, and LWM2M** that run on UDP. DTLS 1.3 is the modern standard. --- ## 1.5 Intuitive Analogy: TLS as a "Sealed, Notarized Letter" Imagine two strangers, **Alice and Bob**, exchanging confidential business proposals in a busy marketplace: - The **letter itself** = the application data (e.g., sensor readings, control commands). - The **wax seal with a unique emblem** = **MAC (Message Authentication Code)** ensuring integrity (the letter was not tampered with). - **Encrypting the letter inside a coded envelope** = **symmetric encryption (AES-GCM, ChaCha20)** ensuring confidentiality. - **A government-issued ID card** = **X.509 digital certificate** for authentication (proving Alice is genuinely Alice). - **The trusted third party who issued the ID** = **Certificate Authority (CA)**, e.g., Let's Encrypt, DigiCert. TLS orchestrates **all four** of these in a structured handshake before any IoT payload flows. --- ## 1.6 Standard Metrics & Parameters (Syllabus-Required) | Parameter | Value / Standard | Notes | |---|---|---| | SCTP RFC | **RFC 4960** (2007) | Core specification | | TLS RFC | **RFC 8446** (TLS 1.3, 2018) | Current standard | | DTLS RFC | **RFC 9147** (DTLS 1.3, 2022) | IoT-relevant UDP variant | | SCTP Port Range | **Same as TCP/UDP (0–65535)** | Uses Port numbers identically | | Default TLS Port | **443 (HTTPS)**, 5684 (CoAPs) | Application-specific | | SCTP Chunk Size | Variable, **max 65535 bytes per DATA chunk** | Message-oriented | | TLS Record Max | **16,384 bytes (16 KB)** per record | TLS 1.3 limit | | Cipher Suites (TLS 1.3) | **TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256, TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256** | AEAD-only | --- ## 1.7 Module 4 Big Picture ```mermaid flowchart LR APP[IoT Application Layer
MQTT / CoAP / HTTP] SEC[Security Layer
TLS / DTLS] TRANS[Transport Layer
SCTP / TCP / UDP] NET[Network Layer
IPv6 / RPL / 6LoWPAN] LINK[Link Layer
IEEE 802.15.4 / Wi-Fi / LTE] APP --> SEC --> TRANS --> NET --> LINK SEC -.encrypts.-> TRANS TRANS -.fragments.-> NET ``` > **Takeaway:** SCTP solves the **reliability + multi-streaming** problem; TLS solves the **security** problem. Together they form the **transport + security spine** of any production-grade IoT stack. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 SCTP — Architectural Deep Dive SCTP is fundamentally a **hybrid of TCP and UDP**, designed to combine the **reliability of TCP** with the **message-oriented nature of UDP**, while adding two unique capabilities: **multi-streaming** and **multi-homing**. ### 2.1.1 Core Features of SCTP | Feature | Description | IoT Benefit | |---|---|---| | **Connection-oriented** | Establishes an *association* (SCTP term) before data transfer | Reliable delivery like TCP | | **Message-oriented** | Preserves message boundaries (no byte-stream fragmentation issues) | Each sensor reading = 1 logical message | | **Multi-streaming** | Multiple independent streams within one association, **no head-of-line blocking** | Loss in stream 1 does not stall stream 2 | | **Multi-homing** | Each endpoint can declare **multiple IP addresses**; automatic failover | Cellular + Wi-Fi redundancy in IoT gateways | | **SACK-based acknowledgment** | Selective acknowledgment of received chunks (similar to TCP SACK) | Efficient retransmission | | **Bundling** | Multiple chunks can be packed into a single IP packet | Reduces header overhead | | **Cookie-based SYN flood protection** | 4-way handshake includes a **state cookie** mechanism | Resistant to DoS attacks | | **Unordered delivery option** | Messages can be marked as "no order required" | Real-time telemetry avoids waiting for old packets | ### 2.1.2 The SCTP Association — 4-Way Handshake Unlike TCP's 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), SCTP uses a **4-way handshake** that is also **resistant to SYN-flood Denial-of-Service attacks** through a state-cookie mechanism. **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **INIT (Client → Server):** Client sends an `INIT` chunk containing its **Initiate Tag** (a random 32-bit number for security), **Initial TSN** (Transmission Sequence Number), `a_rwnd` (advertised receiver window), and the **number of outbound streams** the client wishes to support. 2. **INIT-ACK (Server → Client):** Server responds with `INIT-ACK` containing its own Initiate Tag, Initial TSN, a_rwnd, outbound streams, **plus a STATE COOKIE**. The cookie is a cryptographic token containing all state needed to remember the connection. Crucially, **the server does NOT allocate resources** at this point. 3. **COOKIE-ECHO (Client → Server):** Client echoes the received cookie back to the server, proving the client received it. Only NOW does the server allocate resources and move to ESTABLISHED state. 4. **COOKIE-ACK (Server → Client):** Server acknowledges the cookie, and the client also moves to ESTABLISHED state. The association is now open. > [!NOTE] > **Why 4-way and not 3-way?** The cookie-echo step ensures the server remains *stateless* until it has confirmed the client is real (because only a real client could echo back the cookie). This is the canonical **SYN-flood defense** taught in IoT security modules. ### 2.1.3 SCTP Packet Structure An SCTP packet consists of a **common header (12 bytes)** followed by one or more **chunks**. **SCTP Common Header (12 bytes):** - Source Port (16 bits) - Destination Port (16 bits) - Verification Tag (32 bits) — security identifier - Checksum (32 bits) — **CRC-32c (Castagnoli)**, NOT the 16-bit TCP checksum **SCTP Chunk Header (4 bytes each):** - Chunk Type (8 bits) - Chunk Flags (8 bits) - Chunk Length (16 bits) **Key Chunk Types:** - `0x01` = INIT - `0x02` = INIT-ACK - `0x03` = SACK (Selective Acknowledgment) - `0x00` = DATA - `0x06` = ABORT - `0x07` = SHUTDOWN - `0x0B` = COOKIE-ECHO - `0x0D` = HEARTBEAT ### 2.1.4 Multi-Homing — Failover Mechanism In a multi-homed SCTP association, an endpoint advertises **N ≥ 1 IP addresses**. The **primary path** carries data; if it fails (no acknowledgment within retransmission timeout), SCTP automatically **switches to a secondary path** without tearing down the association. This is implemented via: - **HEARTBEAT chunks** — periodic liveness probes on inactive paths - **Path.Max.Retrans** — threshold for declaring a path inactive - **HB-ACK** — response to heartbeat > [!IMPORTANT] > **IoT Use Case:** An industrial IoT gateway with **dual-WAN connectivity (LTE + fiber)** uses SCTP multi-homing to guarantee that sensor data keeps flowing even when one link goes down. This is exactly the use case in **3GPP S1-AP and NGAP** interfaces of LTE/5G core. ### 2.1.5 SCTP Association Termination - **SHUTDOWN (3-way):** Either side initiates `SHUTDOWN` → other side sends `SHUTDOWN-ACK` → initiator sends `SHUTDOWN-COMPLETE`. - **ABORT:** Abrupt termination, used for error conditions; no graceful FIN-like wait. --- ## 2.2 TLS — Protocol Stack Deep Dive TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) is the **current standard** and is **mandatory-to-implement for IoT devices** shipping under any post-2022 security compliance framework. The protocol is structured into **four sub-protocols**: ### 2.2.1 The TLS Protocol Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP["Application Layer"] HTTP[HTTP / MQTT / CoAP] end subgraph TLS["TLS Protocol Suite"] HANDSHAKE[Handshake Protocol
Negotiate keys & auth] ALERT[Alert Protocol
Errors & warnings] CHANGE[Change Cipher Spec
TLS 1.2 only] RECORD[Record Protocol
Encryption & framing] end subgraph TRANS["Transport Layer"] TCP[TCP for TLS] UDP[UDP for DTLS] end HTTP --> RECORD HANDSHAKE --> RECORD ALERT --> RECORD RECORD --> TRANS ``` ### 2.2.2 Sub-Protocol Responsibilities 1. **Handshake Protocol** — Authenticates the server (and optionally the client), negotiates cryptographic parameters, and establishes **shared secret keys**. In TLS 1.3, this is reduced to a **1-RTT (one round-trip time)** handshake (down from 2-RTT in TLS 1.2). 2. **Change Cipher Spec Protocol** — Signals transition to newly negotiated cipher. **Removed in TLS 1.3.** 3. **Alert Protocol** — Conveys errors (`fatal`) or warnings (`warning`). E.g., `bad_certificate`, `handshake_failure`, `close_notify`. 4. **Record Protocol** — Fragments, compresses (optional), encrypts, and authenticates application data into **TLS records** of max 16 KB. ### 2.2.3 TLS 1.3 Handshake — Step-by-Step 1. **ClientHello:** Client sends its random nonce, supported cipher suites, key share (e.g., X25519), and SNI (Server Name Indication). 2. **ServerHello + EncryptedExtensions + Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished:** Server replies in **one flight** (saving RTT), including its digital certificate and a signature proving possession of the private key. 3. **Client Finished:** Client sends its own `Finished` message; both sides derive the **application traffic keys**. 4. **Application Data:** Encrypted HTTP/MQTT payloads flow. > [!NOTE] > **0-RTT mode (TLS 1.3):** If the client has previously connected, it can send encrypted application data in the first flight using a **pre-shared key (PSK)**. This is powerful for IoT but **introduces replay-attack risk** — must only be used for *idempotent* requests (e.g., sensor read). ### 2.2.4 Cryptographic Primitives in TLS 1.3 | Function | TLS 1.3 Algorithm | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Key Exchange** | X25519 / X448 / P-256 (ECDH) | Establish shared secret | | **Authentication** | RSA-PSS / ECDSA / Ed25519 | Prove server identity | | **Bulk Encryption** | AES-128-GCM, AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 | Confidentiality + integrity (AEAD) | | **Hash** | SHA-256, SHA-384 | HKDF, Finished MAC | > **AEAD** = Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data. TLS 1.3 mandates AEAD only; CBC modes (TLS 1.2) are removed. ### 2.2.5 DTLS — DTLS for UDP-Based IoT DTLS adapts TLS for **unreliable, unordered datagram** transport. The differences are: - Adds **explicit sequence numbers** (since UDP is unordered). - Adds **retransmission timers** for handshake messages. - Handles **path MTU** and fragmentation. - Used by **CoAPs (port 5684), MQTT-SN, LWM2M** — the dominant IoT security stack. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula & Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every symbol, equation, and protocol constant that examiners can validly test in **KTU ESE Module 4**. Memorize this for the 14-mark questions. | Symbol / Term | Meaning / Formula | Unit / Domain | |---|---|---| | $\text{Cwnd}$ | Congestion window (bytes in flight) | bytes | | $\text{RTT}$ | Round-Trip Time | seconds | | $\text{RTO}$ | Retransmission Timeout $= \text{SRTT} + 4 \cdot \text{RTTVAR}$ | seconds | | $\text{SRTT}$ | Smoothed RTT (exponentially weighted) | seconds | | $\text{a\_rwnd}$ | Advertised Receiver Window | bytes | | $\text{TSN}$ | Transmission Sequence Number (32-bit) | integer | | $\text{SSN}$ | Stream Sequence Number (16-bit) | integer per stream | | $N_{\text{streams}}$ | Number of streams per association | integer, typically 1–65535 | | $\text{Cookie Size}$ | Server-generated state cookie, typically 16–32 bytes | bytes | | $P_{\text{loss}}$ | Probability of packet loss (used in IoT reliability calcs) | dimensionless, [0,1] | | $L_{\text{record}}$ | TLS Record payload size, $\le 2^{14}$ bytes | bytes | | $H_{\text{enc}}, H_{\text{auth}}$ | Encryption & authentication header overhead | bytes (TLS) | | $C_{\text{AEAD}}$ | AEAD throughput for sensor data | Mbps | | $T_{\text{handshake}}$ | TLS handshake latency $\approx 1 \text{ RTT}$ (TLS 1.3) | ms | | $\text{MTU}_{\text{eff}}$ | Effective MTU for SCTP $= \text{MTU} - 12 - 4k$ where $k$ = number of chunks | bytes | | $\text{Throughput} \approx \dfrac{\text{Cwnd}}{\text{RTT}}$ | Simplified throughput model (like TCP) | bytes/sec | > **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** When transcribing, use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table pipes. Example: $\vert x \vert$ NOT $|x|$. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | SCTP Application | TLS/DTLS Application | |---|---|---| | **4G/5G Telecom** | S1-AP, X2-AP, NGAP signalling between eNodeB/gNodeB and core | IPSec + TLS for OAM interfaces | | **Industrial IoT** | PROFINET over SCTP, IEC 61850 (smart grid) | OPC UA over TLS, MQTT over TLS | | **Automotive** | V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) for CAM/DENM messages | IEEE 1609.2 PKI + TLS for backend | | **Healthcare IoT** | HL7/FHIR over SCTP for reliability | DTLS for wearable sensors | | **Smart Home** | Lightweight CoAP + DTLS 1.3 | End-to-end consumer privacy | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides the **rigorous analytical derivations** that frequently appear in KTU 14-mark questions: (a) SCTP retransmission timing, (b) SCTP throughput estimation, (c) TLS handshake latency calculation, and (d) association state transitions. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: SCTP Retransmission Timeout (RTO) Calculation The SCTP RFC 4960 specifies that RTO must be calculated using the **same Jacobson/Karels algorithm** as TCP, with smoothed RTT (SRTT) and RTT variance (RTTVAR). ### Starting Definitions $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } R &= \text{newly measured RTT sample} \\ \text{SRTT}_{i+1} &= (1 - \alpha)\cdot \text{SRTT}_i + \alpha \cdot R \\ \text{RTTVAR}_{i+1} &= (1 - \beta)\cdot \text{RTTVAR}_i + \beta \cdot \vert R - \text{SRTT}_i \vert \\ \text{RTO}_{i+1} &= \text{SRTT}_{i+1} + 4 \cdot \text{RTTVAR}_{i+1} \end{aligned} $$ with RFC-recommended constants: $$ \begin{aligned} \alpha &= \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \\ \beta &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \end{aligned} $$ ### Clamping Constraints (mandatory in SCTP) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTO}_{\min} &= 1 \text{ second} \\ \text{RTO}_{\max} &= 60 \text{ seconds} \\ \text{RTO} &= \text{clamp}(\text{SRTT} + 4 \cdot \text{RTTVAR},\ \text{RTO}_{\min},\ \text{RTO}_{\max}) \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** Clamping is necessary because on a long-idle link, RTT samples can be artificially inflated, which would freeze the entire connection. RTO must be **bounded** to keep retransmissions tractable. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — SCTP RTO **Problem (KTU-Style):** An SCTP endpoint has $\text{SRTT} = 200$ ms and $\text{RTTVAR} = 50$ ms. A new RTT sample of $R = 300$ ms is measured. Compute the new RTO. ### Step 1 — Update SRTT $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SRTT}_{\text{new}} &= (1 - 0.125)\cdot 200 + 0.125 \cdot 300 \\ &= 0.875 \cdot 200 + 0.125 \cdot 300 \\ &= 175 + 37.5 \\ &= 212.5 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Update RTTVAR $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTTVAR}_{\text{new}} &= (1 - 0.25)\cdot 50 + 0.25 \cdot \vert 300 - 200 \vert \\ &= 0.75 \cdot 50 + 0.25 \cdot 100 \\ &= 37.5 + 25 \\ &= 62.5 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute RTO $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTO}_{\text{new}} &= 212.5 + 4 \cdot 62.5 \\ &= 212.5 + 250 \\ &= 462.5 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply Clamping $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Computed RTO} &= 462.5 \text{ ms} \\ \text{Since } 462.5 \text{ ms} &< \text{RTO}_{\min} = 1000 \text{ ms} \\ \text{Final RTO} &= \text{RTO}_{\min} = 1000 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiners' Trap:** Students often forget the **clamp to RTO_min = 1 s**. If you report 462.5 ms, you lose 1 mark. The clamp is **mandatory** in SCTP (and TCP). --- ## 3.3 Derivation: SCTP Throughput Estimate For an SCTP association in **steady-state congestion avoidance**, the throughput is bounded by: $$ B \approx \frac{\text{Cwnd}}{\text{RTT}} $$ For **multi-homed SCTP** with $N$ available paths and per-path Cwnd $W_i$ and RTT $T_i$, the aggregate throughput is the sum of per-path contributions: $$ B_{\text{agg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{W_i}{T_i} $$ If all paths have the same Cwnd $W$ and RTT $T$: $$ B_{\text{agg}} = N \cdot \frac{W}{T} $$ ### Worked Example An SCTP association has **2 paths** (LTE + Wi-Fi). Each path: $W = 64$ KB, $T = 100$ ms. $$ \begin{aligned} B_{\text{agg}} &= 2 \cdot \frac{64 \times 1024 \text{ bytes}}{0.1 \text{ s}} \\ &= 2 \cdot 655360 \text{ bytes/s} \\ &= 1{,}310{,}720 \text{ bytes/s} \\ &\approx 1.31 \text{ MB/s} \approx 10.5 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: TLS 1.3 Handshake Latency Model For TLS 1.3, the **base 1-RTT handshake** has the following latency model: $$ T_{\text{handshake}} = 2 \cdot \text{RTT}_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{crypto}} + T_{\text{cert\_verify}} $$ where: - $\text{RTT}_{\text{net}}$ = network round-trip time - $T_{\text{crypto}}$ = time for key derivation (HKDF, ECDHE) - $T_{\text{cert\_verify}}$ = RSA-PSS or ECDSA signature verification For **TLS 1.2** (legacy): $$ T_{\text{handshake,TLS12}} = 3 \cdot \text{RTT}_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{crypto}} + T_{\text{cert\_verify}} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** TLS 1.3 saves one RTT by sending the **server certificate + key share + Finished** in a single flight, immediately after the ClientHello. ### IoT Impact If an IoT device has $\text{RTT}_{\text{net}} = 80$ ms (typical LTE): $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{handshake,TLS13}} &= 2 \cdot 80 + 5 + 2 = 167 \text{ ms} \\ T_{\text{handshake,TLS12}} &= 3 \cdot 80 + 5 + 2 = 247 \text{ ms} \\ \Delta T &= 80 \text{ ms saved} \quad (\approx 32\% \text{ reduction}) \end{aligned} $$ For a temperature sensor reporting every 60 s, this is negligible. For a **real-time industrial controller** at 100 Hz, 80 ms can mean the difference between meeting and missing the control deadline. --- ## 3.5 SCTP State Transition Matrix | Current State | Event | Next State | Action | |---|---|---|---| | `CLOSED` | `INIT` sent | `COOKIE-WAIT` | Send INIT | | `COOKIE-WAIT` | `INIT-ACK` received | `COOKIE-ECHOED` | Echo cookie | | `COOKIE-ECHOED` | `COOKIE-ACK` received | `ESTABLISHED` | Association open | | `LISTEN` | `INIT` received | `COOKIE-ECHOED` | Send INIT-ACK with cookie | | `ESTABLISHED` | `DATA` chunk sent | `ESTABLISHED` | Track TSN | | `ESTABLISHED` | `SACK` received | `ESTABLISHED` | Advance cumulative TSN | | `ESTABLISHED` | `SHUTDOWN` received | `SHUTDOWN-RECEIVED` | Drain streams, send SHUTDOWN-ACK | | `SHUTDOWN-SENT` | `SHUTDOWN-ACK` received | `SHUTDOWN-ACK-SENT` | Send SHUTDOWN-COMPLETE | | Any | `ABORT` chunk | `CLOSED` | Drop all state | --- ## 3.6 TLS 1.3 Cipher Suite Selection Logic A TLS 1.3 cipher suite string has the form `TLS_AEAD_HASH` (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`). Selection follows this logic: 1. Client sends list of supported cipher suites in `ClientHello`. 2. Server picks the **first mutually supported** suite. 3. Server then generates an **ECDHE key share** matching that suite. 4. Both sides derive keys via **HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function)**. 5. Application data is encrypted using the AEAD algorithm specified in the suite. The selection criteria are, in order: **mutual support → cipher strength → performance** (hardware AES-NI vs software ChaCha20). # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SCTP 4-Way Handshake — Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant C as SCTP Client participant S as SCTP Server Note over C,S: CLOSED state C->>S: INIT
(InitTag_A, Initial_TSN, a_rwnd, OS=10) Note over S: Generates STATE COOKIE
(does NOT allocate resources) S-->>C: INIT-ACK

(echoes cookie back) Note over S: Allocates resources
Verifies cookie S-->>C: COOKIE-ACK Note over C,S: ESTABLISHED

others are probed via HEARTBEAT"] ``` --- ## 4.3 TLS 1.3 Handshake — Full Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant C as TLS Client (IoT Device) participant S as TLS Server (Cloud) participant CA as Certificate Authority C->>S: ClientHello
(random, cipher_suites, key_share=X25519, SNI) S->>CA: Verify certificate (pre-installed trust) CA-->>S: Trust OK S-->>C: ServerHello + EncryptedExtensions
+ Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished Note over C: Verify server cert
Compute handshake secret

Port 5684 CoAPs] end subgraph IPV6["IPv6 / 6LoWPAN"] IP6[IPv6 Header] end COAP --> DTLS_LAYER DTLS_LAYER --> UDP UDP --> IPV6 OBS --> REQ HS --> REC ``` --- ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IoT Stack with SCTP + TLS ```mermaid flowchart TB APP[IoT Application

Stream 0, SSN 5, TSN 1000"] CH1 --> CH2["Chunk 2: DATA
