Cumulative TSN ack 999"] CH3 --> CH4["Chunk 4: HEARTBEAT
Path 10.0.0.6 probe"] Note["SCTP bundles multiple chunks
in ONE IP datagram — saves
header overhead vs TCP"] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Explain the multi-streaming feature of SCTP. How does it eliminate head-of-line blocking?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Multi-streaming allows a **single SCTP association** to carry multiple **independent streams** of data between the same two endpoints. Each stream has its own **Stream Sequence Number (SSN, 16-bit)**, and loss of a message in one stream does not block delivery of messages in other streams. This eliminates the **head-of-line blocking** problem of TCP, where a single lost segment stalls the entire byte stream until retransmission completes. In IoT, this is critical because a lost control command on one stream should never delay telemetry flowing on another. > **Marking:** 1 mark definition, 1 mark mechanism (SSN per stream), 1 mark HoL-blocking elimination + IoT benefit. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the role of the State Cookie in SCTP's 4-way handshake?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **State Cookie** is a cryptographic token generated by the server and sent in the `INIT-ACK` chunk. The client must echo it back via `COOKIE-ECHO`. The server **does not allocate any state** until the cookie is verified. This makes SCTP **resistant to SYN-flood Denial-of-Service attacks** because an attacker cannot exhaust server memory by sending fake `INIT` packets — the server remains stateless until the third handshake step confirms the client is real. > **Marking:** 1 mark cookie definition, 1 mark stateless-until-verified, 1 mark DoS resistance. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Within a module, you must attempt **ONE 14-mark question** with choice between Q-A and Q-B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted] **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **SCTP 4-way handshake** with a neat diagram. Explain how it differs from TCP's 3-way handshake and why the additional step is necessary. **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **multi-homing feature of SCTP**. Illustrate with an IoT gateway having dual connectivity (Wi-Fi + LTE) and explain the failover mechanism using **HEARTBEAT chunks**. --- #### Model Solution to Q-A (a) **Step 1 — TCP vs SCTP handshake comparison (2 marks):** | Aspect | TCP | SCTP | |---|---|---| | Phases | 3 | 4 | | Header | SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK | INIT, INIT-ACK, COOKIE-ECHO, COOKIE-ACK | | DoS Protection | None (server allocates on SYN) | Cookie-based, stateless server | | Carries | Byte stream | Discrete messages | **Step 2 — SCTP 4-Way Handshake Steps (4 marks):** 1. **Client → Server (INIT):** Sends Initiate Tag, Initial TSN, advertised receiver window, and number of outbound streams. 2. **Server → Client (INIT-ACK):** Sends its own Initiate Tag, Initial TSN, advertised receiver window, and a **State Cookie**. Server does NOT allocate state. 3. **Client → Server (COOKIE-ECHO):** Client echoes the cookie back to prove it received it. 4. **Server → Client (COOKIE-ACK):** Server allocates state and confirms; both reach ESTABLISHED. **Step 3 — Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above) and Why-the-extra-step (1 mark):** The extra step is the **stateless cookie exchange** to prevent SYN-flood attacks. The server can remain free of half-open connections. --- #### Model Solution to Q-A (b) **Step 1 — Multi-homing definition (2 marks):** Multi-homing allows each SCTP endpoint to bind to **multiple IP addresses**. If the primary path fails, SCTP automatically reroutes traffic to a secondary path **without tearing down the association**. **Step 2 — IoT gateway illustration (3 marks):** The gateway has two interfaces: - `eth0` → Wi-Fi, IP `192.168.1.10` - `ppp0` → LTE, IP `10.45.12.8` It advertises both addresses during the `INIT` chunk. The cloud server binds two IPs similarly. The association has **4 possible paths** (cartesian product), with one designated **primary**. **Step 3 — Heartbeat-based failover (2 marks):** - SCTP periodically sends `HEARTBEAT` chunks on all non-primary paths. - If a configurable number of heartbeats are lost (e.g., `Path.Max.Retrans = 5`), the path is marked **INACTIVE**. - The primary path is **automatically switched** to the next available active path. - This happens **transparently** to the application. > **Exam Tip:** Mention HEARTBEAT, HEARTBEAT-ACK, Path.Max.Retrans, and "transparent failover" — full 7 marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **TLS 1.3 handshake protocol** with a message sequence diagram. Compare it with TLS 1.2 in terms of round trips and security improvements. **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **DTLS protocol** and explain how it adapts TLS for **UDP-based IoT applications** such as CoAP. List two real-world IoT scenarios where DTLS is preferred over TLS. --- #### Model Solution to Q-B (a) **Step 1 — TLS 1.3 Handshake Steps (3 marks):** 1. **ClientHello** — Client sends random, supported cipher suites (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`), key share (e.g., X25519 public key), and SNI. 2. **ServerHello** — Server picks a cipher suite and shares its ECDHE key. 3. **EncryptedExtensions + Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished** — Server authenticates itself with its X.509 certificate and signs the handshake transcript. 4. **Client Finished** — Client confirms handshake, derives application traffic keys. 5. **Application Data** — Encrypted payload flows. **Step 2 — Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Property | TLS 1.2 | TLS 1.3 | |---|---|---| | Handshake RTT | 2-RTT | **1-RTT** (or 0-RTT with PSK) | | Cipher suites | Many (RSA, DH, ECDH, CBC) | Only **AEAD** (AES-GCM, ChaCha20) | | Key Exchange | Static RSA / (EC)DH | **Ephemeral (EC)DHE only** | | 0-RTT Resumption | None | Yes (with replay caveat) | | Legacy crypto | CBC, MD5, SHA-1, RC4 | **All removed** | **Step 3 — Diagram (refer Section 4.3) and Security Improvements (2 marks):** - **Forward secrecy mandatory** — even if server's long-term key is compromised, past sessions remain secure because ephemeral (EC)DHE keys are used. - **AEAD-only** eliminates padding-oracle attacks. - **Encrypted handshake extensions** (EncryptedExtensions) hide SNI from eavesdroppers. --- #### Model Solution to Q-B (b) **Step 1 — DTLS Definition (2 marks):** **DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security — RFC 9147)** is the **UDP-compatible version of TLS**. It is essentially TLS 1.3 with modifications to handle **unreliable, out-of-order, datagram-based** transport. **Step 2 — Adaptations from TLS (3 marks):** - **Explicit sequence numbers** in the record layer (since UDP is unreliable and unordered). - **Retransmission timers** for handshake messages (because UDP may drop them). - **Path MTU handling** and fragmentation. - **No stream cipher usage** — only AEAD. - **Cookie-based DoS protection** for handshake (similar to SCTP's cookie mechanism). **Step 3 — CoAP + DTLS (1 mark):** CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol, RFC 7252) is the **de facto IoT protocol** for constrained devices. It runs over UDP. **CoAPs (port 5684)** is the secured variant using **DTLS 1.2/1.3**. Devices use **pre-shared keys (PSK), raw public keys (RPK), or X.509 certificates** for authentication. **Step 4 — Two Real-World IoT Scenarios (1 mark):** 1. **Smart energy metering** — A wireless gas/electricity meter sends periodic consumption readings over LoRaWAN / NB-IoT to a head-end server using **CoAP + DTLS** with PSK. UDP transport is essential because cellular networks charge per packet and TCP overhead is unacceptable. 2. **Wearable health monitoring** — A heart-rate sensor streams data every second to a hospital gateway using **CoAP over UDP + DTLS** to avoid TCP's handshake overhead and to allow **multicast group announcements** (DTLS supports them). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in SCTP & TLS Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the RTO clamp:** RTO must be clamped to [1 s, 60 s]. Reporting 462.5 ms in the RTO worked example = **−1 mark**. > 2. **SCTP checksum is CRC-32c, NOT 16-bit TCP checksum.** Examiners check this. Writing "16-bit checksum" = **−1 mark**. > 3. **TLS 1.3 is 1-RTT, NOT 2-RTT.** Writing "2 round-trip handshake" = **−1 mark**. > 4. **DTLS is for UDP, not TCP.** Saying "DTLS over TCP" is a serious error = **−2 marks**. > 5. **No cookies = no DoS resistance.** In SCTP, the cookie is what makes it DoS-resistant. Skipping this in the 4-way handshake = **−2 marks**. > 6. **AEAD is mandatory in TLS 1.3.** Listing RC4, 3DES, or CBC modes = **−2 marks** (they are forbidden). > 7. **Forgetting the diagram label.** The KTU rubric allocates **2 marks** strictly for a labeled, accurate diagram. A hand-drawn ASCII-only "diagram" loses these. > 8. **SCTP is message-oriented, TCP is byte-stream-oriented.** Conflating the two = **−1 to −2 marks** depending on question weight. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: SCTP & TLS** ### SCTP Essentials - [ ] SCTP = **RFC 4960**, transport layer, **connection-oriented + message-oriented**. - [ ] **4-way handshake**: INIT → INIT-ACK → COOKIE-ECHO → COOKIE-ACK. - [ ] **Cookie-based** stateless design = **SYN-flood resistant**. - [ ] **Multi-streaming** = multiple SSNs per association, **no head-of-line blocking**. - [ ] **Multi-homing** = multiple IP addresses per endpoint, **HEARTBEAT-based failover**. - [ ] **Verification Tag** + **CRC-32c checksum** in 12-byte common header. - [ ] **Chunk types**: INIT (0x01), INIT-ACK (0x02), SACK (0x03), DATA (0x00), COOKIE-ECHO (0x0B), HEARTBEAT (0x04), ABORT (0x06). - [ ] **RTO** = SRTT + 4·RTTVAR, clamped to **[1 s, 60 s]**. - [ ] Used in **3GPP S1-AP, X2-AP, NGAP** for LTE/5G core signalling. - [ ] SCTP > TCP for **reliability + redundancy**; SCTP < SCTP+DTLS for **security**. ### TLS 1.3 Essentials - [ ] TLS 1.3 = **RFC 8446** (2018), **DTLS 1.3 = RFC 9147** (2022). - [ ] **1-RTT handshake** (down from 2-RTT in TLS 1.2); **0-RTT with PSK** possible. - [ ] **Cipher suites**: `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`, `TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`, `TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256`. - [ ] **AEAD-only** (no CBC, no RC4, no MD5, no SHA-1). - [ ] **Forward secrecy mandatory** via (EC)DHE. - [ ] **4 sub-protocols**: Handshake, Alert, Change Cipher Spec (1.2 only), Record. - [ ] **Record max** = **16,384 bytes (2¹⁴)**. - [ ] **Default port 443** (HTTPS), **5684** (CoAPs). - [ ] Used for **HTTPS, MQTT over TLS, OPC UA, CoAPs, IoT cloud backends**. ### DTLS Essentials - [ ] DTLS = **TLS over UDP** (datagram mode). - [ ] Adds **explicit sequence numbers** + **handshake retransmission timers**. - [ ] Used by **CoAPs, MQTT-SN, LWM2M** — the **IoT security stack**. - [ ] Authentication: **X.509 certs, Raw Public Keys, or PSK**. ### Quick Comparison: TCP vs UDP vs SCTP - [ ] **TCP**: reliable byte-stream, no multi-stream, no multi-home, no DoS protection. - [ ] **UDP**: unreliable datagram, no ordering, no retransmission, no security. - [ ] **SCTP**: reliable message, multi-stream, multi-home, cookie-based DoS resistance. > **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024):** *“SCTP for reliability, TLS for security, DTLS for IoT-UDP — know the chunk types, know the cipher suites, draw the diagrams, and ALWAYS clamp the RTO.”* ✅

topics.title: DTLS) - Session Layer - HTTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT601 - IOT – ARCHITECTURE, PROTOCOLS AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DTLS) - Session Layer - HTTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 DTLS — Datagram Transport Layer Security

### Formal Definition
**DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security)** is a communications protocol that provides **security guarantees** (confidentiality, integrity, and authentication) for applications using **datagram-based transport protocols**, primarily **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**. It is defined in **RFC 6347** (DTLS 1.2) and **RFC 9147** (DTLS 1.3), and is essentially a **derivative of TLS (Transport Layer Security)** adapted to handle the inherent unreliability, packet loss, and reordering of datagram networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DTLS operates at the **Session Layer (Layer 5)** of the OSI Model in the IoT protocol stack. It is the de-facto security protocol for **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** in IoT, where UDP is used instead of TCP to minimize overhead.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Sealed Envelopes vs. Sealed Letters"
Imagine you want to send a private message to a friend:
- **TLS** is like sending a **sealed letter** through registered post. The post office guarantees that the letters arrive **in order**, and if one is lost, they will **resend** it. This is the world of **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)**.
- **DTLS** is like shouting the message across a noisy room using **sealed envelopes**. The envelopes are still sealed (encrypted), but pieces of the message might get lost, arrive out of order, or be duplicated. DTLS cleverly handles this **chaos** while keeping the messages secure.

### Why DTLS is Needed in IoT
| Challenge | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **UDP is unreliable** | IoT devices often use UDP (in CoAP, LWM2M) to save bandwidth and power. UDP provides no guarantees on delivery or order. |
| **TLS needs TCP** | Standard TLS assumes a reliable, ordered byte stream (TCP). Direct use over UDP would break handshake. |
| **Constrained devices** | Many IoT sensors cannot afford the overhead of TCP's handshakes and acknowledgments. |
| **Latency-sensitive** | Real-time IoT applications (industrial control, smart grids) cannot tolerate TCP's retransmission delays. |

### Core Security Services Provided by DTLS
- **Confidentiality** — data is encrypted using symmetric ciphers (e.g., **AES-128-GCM**, **ChaCha20-Poly1305**).
- **Integrity** — every record is protected by a **MAC (Message Authentication Code)**.
- **Authentication** — peers authenticate using **X.509 certificates**, **pre-shared keys (PSK)**, or **raw public keys (RPK)**.
- **Anti-replay** — sequence numbers prevent attackers from replaying old packets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** DTLS = TLS + three small but critical modifications to handle **packet loss**, **reordering**, and **datagram length** limitations. It does **not** change the cryptographic core of TLS.

---

## 1.2 HTTP — HyperText Transfer Protocol (in IoT Context)

### Formal Definition
**HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** is an **application-layer request-response protocol** originally designed for the World Wide Web, defined in **RFC 9110 / RFC 9112**. In IoT, HTTP is used as a **session/application layer protocol** for RESTful Web Services, where constrained and unconstrained devices exchange resources (sensor data, actuator commands) over **TCP** using methods like **GET, POST, PUT, DELETE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** HTTP is classified under the **Session / Application Layer** in the IoT protocol stack. It is considered a **"heavyweight" IoT protocol** suitable for **gateways, smartphones, and cloud servers**, but rarely for deeply constrained sensor motes (where CoAP is preferred).

### Conceptual Analogy — "Restaurant Ordering"
Think of HTTP as a **restaurant interaction**:
1. **Customer (Client)** — IoT device or smartphone.
2. **Waiter (HTTP)** — carries the order back and forth.
3. **Kitchen (Server)** — Cloud backend or gateway.
- The customer **requests** ("GET /temperature") and the kitchen **responds** with the data ("HTTP/1.1 200 OK, payload: 24°C").
- Every request needs a **new order** (in HTTP/1.1 keep-alive helps) — much like a "transactional" model.
- This is **resource-oriented** — everything is a "URL" (Uniform Resource Locator), like an item on the menu.

### Why HTTP in IoT? — Use Cases
- **Smart Home** apps: phone → cloud server (HTTP REST API) → smart bulb.
- **Industrial IoT Gateways**: collect sensor data, push to ERP via HTTP POST.
- **Wearable Devices**: smartwatches sync health data to the cloud over HTTPS.
- **MQTT over WebSockets**: brokers expose MQTT messaging over HTTP-compatible WebSockets for browser-based dashboards.

### Core HTTP Methods (Verbs) for IoT
| Method | IoT Use | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **GET** | Read sensor data | Retrieve current state of a resource (e.g., `GET /sensor/temp`) |
| **POST** | Send data to server | Upload new data or trigger actions |
| **PUT** | Update device state | Modify an existing resource (e.g., `PUT /actuator/light/on`) |
| **DELETE** | Remove a resource | Deregister a device, delete a record |
| **HEAD** | Check resource metadata | Verify endpoint exists without full payload |

### HTTP Status Codes Relevant to IoT
- **2xx Success**: `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content`.
- **4xx Client Error**: `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `404 Not Found`, `429 Too Many Requests`.
- **5xx Server Error**: `500 Internal Server Error`, `503 Service Unavailable` (common during gateway downtime).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** In IoT literature, HTTP is often paired with **TLS** (over TCP → **HTTPS**) to form a **secure session-layer stack**. DTLS paired with **CoAP** forms the equivalent for **UDP-based IoT**.

---

## 1.3 Comparison: DTLS vs. HTTP in IoT Context

| Feature | DTLS | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Session Layer (Secures datagrams) | Application Layer (Resource exchange) |
| **Underlying Transport** | UDP (typically) | TCP (typically) |
| **Purpose** | Provide **security** | Provide **resource access** |
| **Reliability Assumption** | **Unreliable** (built for lossy datagrams) | **Reliable** (relies on TCP) |
| **Handshake Overhead** | Low (modified TLS handshake) | High (TCP 3-way + HTTP request) |
| **Used With** | CoAP, LWM2M | REST APIs, WebSockets, MQTT-over-WS |
| **Power Consumption** | Low (suitable for constrained devices) | High (less suitable for battery devices) |
| **Header Size** | ~13 bytes (DTLS record header) | Variable, often hundreds of bytes |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DTLS — Internal Architecture & Operational Mechanics

### 2.1.1 DTLS Protocol Stack Position
DTLS sits **on top of UDP** and **below the application protocol** (e.g., CoAP). It encapsulates application data into **DTLS records**, each protected by cryptographic transformations.

### 2.1.2 Key Differences from TLS (The Three Critical Modifications)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three changes make DTLS work over UDP without breaking the cryptographic guarantees of TLS.

1. **Sequence Number Transparency in the Handshake**
   - TLS hides sequence numbers inside the encrypted MAC, so the receiver cannot detect lost handshake messages.
   - DTLS adds an **explicit sequence number** to handshake messages so the receiver can detect and retransmit lost handshake packets.

2. **Reordering and Replay Window**
   - DTLS maintains a **sliding window** (typically of size **32**) of received records. Out-of-order packets within the window are accepted; older ones are dropped.

3. **Datagram Fragmentation / Reassembly**
   - TLS can send records of arbitrary length because TCP handles fragmentation.
   - UDP datagrams are limited in size (e.g., **IPv4: 65,507 bytes**, typically **≤ 1,500 bytes** in practice due to MTU).
   - DTLS adds a **fragment offset** and **fragment length** field to handshake messages, splitting them across multiple UDP datagrams and reassembling them on the receiver.

### 2.1.3 The DTLS Handshake — Step-by-Step

The DTLS 1.2 handshake is analogous to TLS 1.2 with the three modifications applied. It involves **four flights** (rounds of message exchange):

**Flight 1 — ClientHello**
- Client sends: `ClientHello` (random nonce, session ID, cipher suites, compression methods, extensions like `client_hello_cookie` for DoS protection).

**Flight 2 — HelloVerifyRequest + ClientHello (cookie)**
- Server replies with `HelloVerifyRequest` containing a **cookie** (an anti-DoS mechanism, **stateless** on the server).
- Client resends `ClientHello` with the cookie attached.

**Flight 3 — ServerHello, Certificate, ServerKeyExchange, CertificateRequest, ServerHelloDone**
- Server authenticates, exchanges keys, and optionally requests client certificates.

**Flight 4 — Certificate, ClientKeyExchange, CertificateVerify, ChangeCipherSpec, Finished**
- Client completes authentication and confirms keying material.
- The **ChangeCipherSpec** switches both peers to the newly negotiated encrypted state.

> [!NOTE]
> The **cookie exchange (Flight 2)** is unique to DTLS and protects the server from **denial-of-service (DoS) amplification attacks** where an attacker spoofs source IP addresses to flood the server with handshake responses.

### 2.1.4 DTLS Record Layer
Every application datagram is encapsulated as a DTLS record:
- **ContentType** (1 byte): `change_cipher_spec`, `alert`, `handshake`, `application_data`.
- **ProtocolVersion** (2 bytes): e.g., `0xFEFD` (DTLS 1.2) or `0xFEFF` (DTLS 1.3).
- **Epoch** (2 bytes): incremented on every `ChangeCipherSpec` — used for **anti-replay**.
- **SequenceNumber** (6 bytes): explicit, 48-bit counter, never reused.
- **Length** (2 bytes): length of the ciphertext.
- **Payload**: encrypted and authenticated data.

### 2.1.5 DTLS Cipher Suites
A DTLS cipher suite is named as in TLS, e.g.:
- `TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_CCM_8` — **pre-shared key + AES-128-CCM (8-byte tag)**, very popular in IoT.
- `TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256` — strong, used on capable devices.
- `TLS_PSK_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256` — modern, lightweight alternative.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** PSK-based suites are the **most IoT-friendly** because they avoid the heavy X.509 certificate chain verification, which is impractical on 8-bit/16-bit microcontrollers.

---

## 2.2 HTTP — Internal Architecture & Request-Response Model

### 2.2.1 HTTP Transaction Lifecycle

1. **TCP Connection Establishment** (if not using keep-alive)
   - 3-way handshake: `SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`.
2. **HTTP Request** sent by client
3. **HTTP Response** sent by server
4. **Connection Close or Keep-Alive**

### 2.2.2 HTTP Message Format

**Request:**
```
METHOD  /path  HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: api.example.com\r\n
User-Agent: IoT-Device-v1\r\n
Content-Type: application/json\r\n
Content-Length: 42\r\n
\r\n
{"temperature": 24.5}
```

**Response:**
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
Content-Type: application/json\r\n
Content-Length: 25\r\n
\r\n
{"status":"success","value":24.5}
```

### 2.2.3 HTTP Versions and Their IoT Relevance
| Version | Year | Key Feature | IoT Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP/0.9** | 1991 | Plaintext GET only | Obsolete |
| **HTTP/1.0** | 1996 | Headers, methods, status codes | Rarely used |
| **HTTP/1.1** | 1997 | Persistent connections, chunked transfer, pipelining | **Most common in IoT today** |
| **HTTP/2** | 2015 | Binary framing, multiplexing, HPACK compression | Growing (needs more memory) |
| **HTTP/3** | 2022 | Runs over **QUIC** (UDP-based) — essentially TCP replacement with built-in TLS | Emerging; pairs conceptually with DTLS philosophy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Connection:** **HTTP/3 (QUIC)** is built on UDP and integrates TLS 1.3 — making it a closer cousin of DTLS. Some textbooks discuss HTTP/3 as the "modern answer" to DTLS's reliability challenges.

### 2.2.4 HTTPS — Securing HTTP with TLS
- **HTTPS = HTTP + TLS** over **TCP port 443**.
- The TLS handshake happens **before** any HTTP request is sent.
- Adds a **secure session layer** to HTTP — analogous to how DTLS adds security to CoAP.
- Total overhead: ~2–4 KB for handshake + extra record bytes.

### 2.2.5 REST Architecture in IoT
**REST (Representational State Transfer)** is the architectural style that makes HTTP suitable for IoT. Key principles:
- **Stateless** — each request contains all info needed.
- **Resource-oriented** — sensors, actuators are URL-addressable.
- **Uniform interface** — standard methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).
- **Cacheable** — sensor readings can be cached.

**Example IoT REST API:**
```
GET https://api.smartfarm.com/sensors/field1/moisture     → returns JSON
PUT https://api.smartfarm.com/actuators/pump1  {"state":"on"}
```

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

### 2.3.1 DTLS Header Sizes & Overhead
| Component | Size (bytes) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| UDP Header | **8** | Source + dest port, length, checksum |
| IPv4 Header | **20** (min) | + options if any |
| **DTLS Record Header** | **13** | ContentType(1) + Version(2) + Epoch(2) + SeqNum(6) + Length(2) |
| DTLS Handshake Header | **12** | MsgType(1) + Length(3) + MsgSeq(2) + FragOff(3) + FragLen(3) |
| **DTLS Minimum Overhead** | **~33 bytes** | 8 (UDP) + 13 (DTLS) + 12 (HS) — before payload |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula:** Total minimum DTLS datagram size = UDP header + DTLS record header + Payload + MAC + Padding
>
> $$S_{\text{DTLS,min}} = H_{\text{UDP}} + H_{\text{DTLS,rec}} + P + M + Pad$$
>
> Where:
> - $H_{\text{UDP}} = 8$ bytes
> - $H_{\text{DTLS,rec}} = 13$ bytes
> - $P$ = application payload (in bytes)
> - $M$ = MAC (typically **8, 16, or 32 bytes** depending on cipher)
> - $Pad$ = padding for cipher block alignment (e.g., 0–15 bytes for AES-CBC)

### 2.3.2 Anti-Replay Window
DTLS accepts records with sequence numbers in the range:

$$\text{SN}_{\text{left}} \le \text{SN}_{\text{received}} < \text{SN}_{\text{right}}$$

Where:
- $\text{SN}_{\text{right}} =$ highest received sequence number so far
- $\text{SN}_{\text{left}} = \text{SN}_{\text{right}} - W + 1$
- $W$ = **replay window size** (default **32** in DTLS 1.2, **64** in DTLS 1.3)

### 2.3.3 HTTP Request Size Estimation
A typical HTTP request is bounded by:

$$S_{\text{HTTP}} = L_{\text{req-line}} + L_{\text{headers}} + L_{\text{body}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula:** Typical minimum HTTP transaction size (headers only, no body):
>
> $$\vert S_{\text{HTTP,min}} \vert \approx 200 \text{ to } 800 \text{ bytes}$$
>
> This is **15–60× larger** than a comparable CoAP + DTLS message, which is why CoAP + DTLS is preferred for constrained IoT nodes.

### 2.3.4 Energy & Bandwidth Quick Reference
| Metric | CoAP + DTLS (over UDP) | HTTP + TLS (over TCP) |
|---|---|---|
| Handshake messages | **4 flights** (cookie exchange) | 2–3 round trips (TCP + TLS) |
| Minimum datagram | **~33 bytes overhead** | **~100+ bytes** (TCP + IP + TLS) |
| Suitability | Class 0–2 devices (motebs) | Class 2+ devices (smartphones, gateways) |
| Sleep-friendliness | High (UDP can sleep) | Low (TCP state must be maintained) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | DTLS Application | HTTP Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Home** | Securing Zigbee/Thread sensor data | Smart speaker cloud APIs |
| **Smart Cities** | Streetlight control via CoAP+DTLS | Traffic monitoring dashboards via REST |
| **Healthcare IoT** | Wearable vitals monitoring | Patient portals, hospital systems |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Machine-to-machine sensor alerts | ERP/SCADA integration via REST |
| **Automotive (V2X)** | Securing ETSI ITS-G5 messages | Infotainment backend over HTTPS |
| **Smart Agriculture** | Soil sensors using LoRaWAN + DTLS | Drone telemetry to cloud via HTTPS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** DTLS and HTTP are not competitors — they are **complementary**. A typical IoT deployment uses **CoAP + DTLS at the edge** (between sensors and gateway) and **HTTP + TLS at the cloud** (between gateway and server). The gateway acts as a **protocol translator**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total DTLS Datagram Size for a CoAP Message

> **Problem Statement:** A Class 1 IoT sensor sends a CoAP GET request containing **40 bytes of payload** (e.g., a sensor ID + timestamp), secured using DTLS 1.2 with the cipher suite `TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_CCM_8`. Compute the **total on-the-wire size** of the resulting UDP datagram.

### Step 1 — Identify the headers and their sizes

- **IPv4 Header**: $H_{\text{IPv4}} = 20$ bytes (no options)
- **UDP Header**: $H_{\text{UDP}} = 8$ bytes
- **DTLS Record Header**: $H_{\text{DTLS,rec}} = 13$ bytes
- **CoAP Header** (typical): $H_{\text{CoAP}} = 4$ bytes (version, type, token length, code, message ID)
- **CoAP Options + Payload marker**: $H_{\text{CoAP,opt}} = 6$ bytes (e.g., Uri-Path = 4 bytes + payload marker 0xFF = 1 byte + token)
- **Application Payload**: $P = 40$ bytes
- **AES-128-CCM Authentication Tag**: $M = 8$ bytes (since the suite specifies `_8`)
- **Padding**: $Pad = 0$ bytes (CCM is a stream-like AEAD, no padding needed)

### Step 2 — Write the size equation

The total size is the sum of all headers, payload, MAC, and padding:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = H_{\text{IPv4}} + H_{\text{UDP}} + H_{\text{DTLS,rec}} + H_{\text{CoAP}} + H_{\text{CoAP,opt}} + P + M + Pad
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 20 + 8 + 13 + 4 + 6 + 40 + 8 + 0
$$

### Step 4 — Compute each addition explicitly

$$
S_{\text{total}} = (20 + 8) + 13 + (4 + 6) + 40 + 8
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 28 + 13 + 10 + 40 + 8
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = (28 + 13) + 10 + 48
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 41 + 10 + 48
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 51 + 48
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{total}} = 99 \text{ bytes}}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

For just **40 bytes** of useful sensor data, the on-the-wire packet is **99 bytes**. The **protocol overhead** (everything except payload) is:

$$
\text{Overhead} = 99 - 40 = 59 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Overhead Ratio} = \frac{59}{99} \times 100\% \approx 59.6\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Even with DTLS+CoAP (the most efficient IoT stack), more than **half the bytes on the wire are overhead**. This is why IoT engineers compress, batch, and aggregate data before transmission.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: HTTP+TLS vs. CoAP+DTLS Bandwidth Comparison

> **Problem Statement:** A gateway uploads **100 sensor readings** (each 20 bytes) per minute to a cloud server. Compare the **monthly bandwidth** (in MB) using two approaches: **(a) HTTP/1.1 over HTTPS**, **(b) CoAP over DTLS**. Assume 30 days in a month.

### Assumption: HTTP Stack Size
- HTTP/1.1 request line + headers (no body) ≈ **400 bytes** (typical with User-Agent, Host, Content-Type, etc.)
- JSON wrapper overhead per reading ≈ **30 bytes** (e.g., `{"id":1,"value":24.5,"ts":...}`)
- TLS record header: **5 bytes** (TLS has a 5-byte record header)
- TCP header: **20 bytes**
- IP header: **20 bytes**
- TLS MAC: **32 bytes** (SHA-256 HMAC)

### Assumption: CoAP+DTLS Stack Size
- CoAP + DTLS overhead per message ≈ **59 bytes** (as derived above)
- (The 20-byte payload here = the 20-byte sensor reading directly, no JSON wrapper)

### Step 1 — Compute HTTP message size

For 100 readings batched in one HTTP POST (typical):

$$
S_{\text{HTTP,body}} = 100 \times (20 + 30) = 5000 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{\text{HTTP,total}} = 20 + 20 + 5 + 32 + 400 + 5000 = 5477 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute CoAP+DTLS message size

Each reading sent individually:

$$
S_{\text{CoAP+DTLS,each}} = 20 + 13 + 8 + 4 + 6 + 20 = 71 \text{ bytes}
$$

For 100 readings:

$$
S_{\text{CoAP+DTLS,total}} = 100 \times 71 = 7100 \text{ bytes}
$$

Wait — for fair comparison, batch CoAP too (using blockwise transfer or single CoAP POST):

$$
S_{\text{CoAP+DTLS,batched}} = 99 + 100 \times 20 - 40 + 100 \times 20 = 99 + 1960 = 2059 \text{ bytes (approx)}
$$

(A single CoAP POST with 2000 bytes payload + 59 bytes overhead)

### Step 3 — Compute monthly bandwidth

**HTTP/HTTPS approach (per minute):**
$$
B_{\text{HTTP,min}} = 5477 \text{ bytes/min}
$$

**CoAP+DTLS approach (batched, per minute):**
$$
B_{\text{CoAP,min}} = 2059 \text{ bytes/min}
$$

**Per hour (60 min):**
$$
B_{\text{HTTP,hr}} = 5477 \times 60 = 328,620 \text{ bytes/hr}
$$

$$
B_{\text{CoAP,hr}} = 2059 \times 60 = 123,540 \text{ bytes/hr}
$$

**Per day (24 hr):**
$$
B_{\text{HTTP,day}} = 328,620 \times 24 = 7,886,880 \text{ bytes/day} \approx 7.52 \text{ MB/day}
$$

$$
B_{\text{CoAP,day}} = 123,540 \times 24 = 2,964,960 \text{ bytes/day} \approx 2.83 \text{ MB/day}
$$

**Per month (30 days):**
$$
B_{\text{HTTP,month}} = 7.52 \times 30 = 225.6 \text{ MB/month}
$$

$$
B_{\text{CoAP,month}} = 2.83 \times 30 = 84.8 \text{ MB/month}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute savings

$$
\text{Savings} = B_{\text{HTTP,month}} - B_{\text{CoAP,month}} = 225.6 - 84.8 = 140.8 \text{ MB/month}
$$

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \frac{140.8}{225.6} \times 100\% \approx 62.4\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Switching from HTTP/HTTPS to CoAP+DTLS for the same IoT workload saves **~62% of bandwidth**, which is critical for cellular-IoT devices billed per kilobyte.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selecting DTLS vs. HTTP for IoT

| Decision Factor | Choose **DTLS + CoAP** | Choose **HTTP + TLS** |
|---|---|---|
| Device is **Class 0/1** (e.g., 8-bit MCU, < 10 KB RAM) | ✅ Mandatory | ❌ Impossible |
| Device is **Class 2+** (Linux gateway, smartphone) | Possible but unusual | ✅ Standard |
| Transport is **UDP-based** (e.g., 6LoWPAN, LoRaWAN) | ✅ Natural fit | ❌ TCP overhead too high |
| Transport is **TCP-based** (Wi-Fi, Ethernet) | Acceptable | ✅ Natural fit |
| Need **web browser integration** | ❌ Browsers don't speak CoAP/DTLS | ✅ Native in browsers |
| Need **firewall/NAT traversal** | Tricky (UDP holes) | ✅ Well-supported |
| Backend is **cloud REST API** | Use proxy/gateway | ✅ Direct integration |
| Latency budget is **< 100 ms** | ✅ Lower handshake | ❌ TCP + TLS adds ~100–300 ms |
| Energy budget is **battery, < 1 Ah** | ✅ Lower per-byte energy | ❌ TCP keep-alive drains battery |
| Use case: **field sensors** | ✅ | ❌ |
| Use case: **wearables, smartphones, dashboards** | ❌ | ✅ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The matrix is symmetric — pick the "Winner" column based on the **first** dominant constraint (device class, energy, latency).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Sequence Number Anti-Replay Analysis

> **Problem:** A DTLS receiver has its anti-replay window set to $W = 32$, and the highest received sequence number so far is $\text{SN}_{\text{right}} = 1000$. Determine whether a packet with $\text{SN} = 985$ is accepted.

### Step 1 — Compute the left edge of the window

$$
\text{SN}_{\text{left}} = \text{SN}_{\text{right}} - W + 1 = 1000 - 32 + 1 = 969
$$

### Step 2 — Check the acceptance condition

DTLS accepts packets with $\text{SN}_{\text{left}} \le \text{SN}_{\text{received}} < \text{SN}_{\text{right}}$:

$$
969 \le 985 < 1000
$$

### Step 3 — Conclusion

Both conditions are satisfied (969 ≤ 985 ✓ AND 985 < 1000 ✓), so the packet is **accepted** (but its position is marked in the replay bitmap).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    dtls[DTLS - Session Layer Security]

    app1 --> coap
    app2 --> coap
    app3 --> coap
    coap --> dtls
    dtls --> udp
    udp --> ipv6
    ipv6 --> ll
    ll --> phy

```

## 4.3 DTLS Handshake — Four-Flight Message Exchange

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as IoT Client
    participant S as DTLS Server

    C->>S: Flight 1 - ClientHello (random, cipher suites, cookie=empty)
    S-->>C: Flight 2 - HelloVerifyRequest (contains server cookie)
    C->>S: Flight 3 - ClientHello (with cookie attached)
    S-->>C: Flight 4 - ServerHello, Certificate, ServerKeyExchange, ServerHelloDone
    C->>S: Flight 5 - ClientKeyExchange, ChangeCipherSpec, Finished
    S-->>C: Flight 6 - ChangeCipherSpec, Finished
    Note over C,S: Encrypted Application Data Begins
    C->>S: Application Data (DTLS record)
    S-->>C: Application Data (DTLS record)
```

## 4.4 HTTP Request-Response Cycle in IoT

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Dev as IoT Device
    participant GW as Gateway
    participant Cloud as Cloud Server

    Dev->>GW: Sensor Data (CoAP over UDP+DTLS)
    GW->>GW: Protocol Translation
    Note over GW: CoAP+DTLS to HTTP+TLS
    GW->>Cloud: HTTP POST /api/sensors (HTTPS over TCP+TLS)
    Cloud-->>GW: HTTP 201 Created
    GW-->>Dev: CoAP ACK
    Cloud->>Cloud: Store in Database
    Note over Cloud: Web Dashboard can now query
    Cloud-->>Cloud: GET /api/sensors/123 (HTTP)
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Choosing DTLS vs. HTTP for an IoT Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start - New IoT Project]) --> q1{Device Class?}
    q1 -- Class 0/1 constrained --> useCoap[Use CoAP over UDP]
    useCoap --> useDtls[Secure with DTLS]
    q1 -- Class 2/3 capable --> q2{Transport available?}
    q2 -- UDP / 6LoWPAN --> useCoap
    q2 -- TCP / Wi-Fi / Ethernet --> q3{Browser integration needed?}
    q3 -- Yes --> useHttp[Use HTTP over TCP]
    useHttp --> useTls[Secure with TLS - HTTPS]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Energy budget critical?}
    q4 -- Yes battery powered --> useCoap
    q4 -- No mains powered --> useHttp
    useDtls --> endNode([End - Deploy CoAP+DTLS])
    useTls --> endHttp([End - Deploy HTTP+HTTPS])
```

## 4.6 DTLS Record Layer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plain[Application Plaintext P] --> frag[Fragmentation Module]
    frag --> compress[Compression optional]
    compress --> mac[Add MAC 8 or 16 bytes]
    mac --> pad[Padding for block cipher]
    pad --> enc[AES Encryption]
    enc --> hdr[Append DTLS Record Header 13 bytes]
    hdr --> udp[Wrap in UDP datagram 8 bytes]
    udp --> net[Transmit over IP network]
    net --> rxd[Received UDP datagram]
    rxd --> parseHdr[Parse Record Header]
    parseHdr --> dec[AES Decryption]
    dec --> unp[Remove Padding]
    unp --> verifyMAC[Verify MAC]
    verifyMAC --> decomp[Decompress]
    decomp --> reassem[Reassemble Fragments]
    reassem --> out[Delivered Plaintext to Application]
```
