**Why Security is Hard in M2M (1 mark):** Millions of constrained, unattended devices need mutual auth, key management, and access control without human intervention.

**ETSI M2M Security Architecture Layers (3 marks):**

- **Transport Layer Security:** TLS / DTLS for link confidentiality.
- **Application-Layer Security:** End-to-end protection independent of the network.
- **Service-Layer Security (xSE):** Mutual authentication between SCLs, ACLs on resources, and secure key handling at the service layer.

**Trust Bootstrap Functions (3 marks):**

| Function | Role |
|---|---|
| **TBF (Trust Bootstrap Function)** | Acts as the **root of trust** during manufacturing; injects initial credentials (X.509 cert / PSK) into the device. |
| **BEF (Bootstrap Enrolment Function)** | Performs **out-of-band enrolment** of the device with its NSCL; binds the device's identity to an operator's M2M platform. |
| **MSBF (M2M Service Bootstrap Function)** | Conducts **in-band mutual authentication** between the device's DSCL and the NSCL using the credentials provisioned by BEF. |

**Mutual Authentication Flow (brief, 0 marks here but conceptually):**
Device → MSBF → NSCL → verify cert against BEF/TBF root → derive session keys → secure channel established.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

| Dimension | **ETSI M2M** | **oneM2M** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | ETSI (Europe), 2011 | Global consolidation: ETSI + CCSA + TIA + TTA + TTC + ARIB + ATIS, 2012 onwards |
| **Scope** | European, M2M-focussed | Global IoT/M2M standard |
| **Architecture** | DSCL, GSCL, NSCL | Common Services Entity (CSE) + Application Entity (AE) + Network Services Entity (NSE) |
| **Reference Points** | mIa, mId, mIm, dIa, dId | **Mca, Mcc, Mcc', Mcn, Mcx, Mra** |
| **Resource Model** | `<sclBase>/<app>/<container>/<contentInstance>` | `<CSEBase>/<AE>/<Container>/<ContentInstance>` — similar but renamed |
| **Service Capabilities** | xAE, xGC, xSE, xTE, xNO, … | **ACP, BD, CSR, CM, DMR, GMG, LOC, MG, NSE, PCH, REG, RM, SCL, SMA, SUB, TBO** |
| **Transport** | HTTP, CoAP, MQTT | HTTP, CoAP, MQTT, WebSocket |

**Evolution Path (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **2014**, ETSI M2M was **superseded by oneM2M**. ETSI, CCSA (China), ARIB (Japan), TTA (Korea), TIA (USA), and TTC (Japan) jointly created oneM2M to unify the global standard. The oneM2M architecture reuses ETSI M2M's core ideas (Service Capability Layer → Common Services Function, NSCL → IN-CSE, DSCL → AE-Device) but generalizes the reference points. ETSI M2M is now maintained in a "frozen" state for legacy deployments; **new designs should use oneM2M**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Confusing SCL and CSE:** ETSI M2M uses *SCL*; oneM2M uses *CSE*. Examiners will *deduct ½ mark per confusion*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Gateway is OPTIONAL:** Many students draw a gateway in every diagram. Only include it if devices are constrained.
> 3. **Reference points vs. interfaces:** `mIa` is the *application-facing* point, `mId` is *device-facing*. Swapping them = 1 mark loss.
> 4. **Saying "SCL is a physical layer":** It is a *logical* software layer — not a network layer.
> 5. **Skipping the BEF/TBF chain:** The three bootstrap functions are examinable — write them in order: **TBF → BEF → MSBF**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ETSI M2M = European M2M Architecture (TS 102 690, 2011)**, later evolved into **oneM2M (2014)**.
- ✅ **Three blocks:** M2M **Device Domain** (DSCL) ↔ **Gateway Domain** (GSCL, optional) ↔ **Network & Applications Domain** (NSCL + Apps).
- ✅ **SCL** = Service Capability Layer — the heart of ETSI M2M; three flavours: **D**SCL, **G**SCL, **N**SCL.
- ✅ **Reference points to memorize (MUST):** **mIa** (App↔NSCL), **mId** (Device↔NSCL/GSCL), **mIm** (NSCL↔NSCL), **dIa** (App↔Device direct), **dId** (App↔Device via DSCL).
- ✅ **Resource model is RESTful:** `<base>/<sclBase>/<app>/<container>/<contentInstance>` with CRUDN operations.
- ✅ **Service Capabilities (xSeries):** xAE, xGC, xRAR, xCS, xHD, xTE, xNO, xDM, xSE, xTR, xGR, xLO, xSM, xTX, xST.
- ✅ **Security:** xSE is the service-capability security; **TBF → BEF → MSBF** is the trust bootstrap chain.
- ✅ **Number constant to remember:** ETSI M2M has **2 reference architectures (Release 1 in 2011, Release 2 in 2013)**, **3 SCL types**, **6+ reference points**.
- ✅ **Biggest engineering use case:** **Smart Metering** — millions of meters reporting hourly to a single NSCL, with **store-and-forward in GSCL** to mask WAN outages.
- ✅ **For exam:** Always draw the **block diagram first**, then label reference points, then list SCL functions — this order maximises partial marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BBF - Security in IoT Protocols - MAC 802.15.4
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT601 - IOT – ARCHITECTURE, PROTOCOLS AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** BBF - Security in IoT Protocols - MAC 802.15.4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to BBF (Building Block Functions) for Security

**Building Block Functions (BBF)** in the context of IoT protocols refers to the **fundamental, atomic security primitives** that are layered together to construct a robust security architecture. These are not a single protocol, but rather a set of cryptographic and procedural tools — *Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Access Control,* and *Replay Protection* — that engineers select and combine to secure constrained IoT communication.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the BBF concept is introduced to teach students how security is *not* a monolithic feature, but a stack of small, well-defined functions. When applied to the **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC layer**, these BBFs define how a tiny, low-power radio frame (used in Zigbee, Thread, 6LoWPAN, WirelessHART) is encrypted, signed, and verified before it travels over the air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** *The 802.15.4 MAC layer is the de facto security chokepoint for short-range IoT. Almost every higher-layer IoT protocol (Zigbee, 6LoWPAN, Thread) ultimately depends on the security primitives defined here.*

## 1.2 Formal Definition of IEEE 802.15.4

**IEEE 802.15.4** is a technical standard maintained by the **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)** that defines the operation of **Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs)**. It specifies the **Physical (PHY) layer** and the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sub-layer** for short-range, low-bit-rate wireless communication (typically **250 kbps** in the 2.4 GHz band).

The standard supports:
- **Star** and **Peer-to-Peer (mesh)** topologies.
- Two device classes: **Full-Function Device (FFD)** and **Reduced-Function Device (RFD)**.
- 16-bit short addresses and 64-bit extended addresses.
- A maximum frame size of **127 bytes**.

The MAC sub-layer is responsible for:
- **Beacon management**, **Channel access (CSMA/CA)**, **GTS allocation**, and most importantly — **Security of frames**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Sealed Postal Letter

Imagine you are sending a confidential letter through a public post office.

1. **Confidentiality (Encryption)** — You place your message inside a locked steel box. Only the recipient who owns the matching key can open it and read the letter. This is **AES-128 encryption** in 802.15.4.
2. **Integrity (MIC)** — You seal the box with a unique wax stamp. If anyone tampers with the box during transit, the seal breaks, and the recipient knows the message was altered. This is the **Message Integrity Code (MIC)**.
3. **Authentication** — You sign the wax stamp with your personal monogram so the recipient can confirm the box truly came from you. This is the cryptographic **authentication tag**.
4. **Replay Protection (Frame Counter)** — You write a unique serial number on every box. Even if an attacker copies the exact box, the recipient rejects any duplicate serial number. This is the **frame counter** mechanism.
5. **Access Control List (ACL)** — The recipient maintains a list of trusted senders. Even a valid seal from an unknown sender is rejected.

Together, these five primitives form the **Security BBFs** of 802.15.4. Without all of them working in concert, the system is vulnerable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — BBF ≠ Algorithm.** Building Block Functions describe *what* security property we need (confidentiality, integrity, etc.). The *algorithm* used to realize each BBF in 802.15.4 is **AES-CCM*** (AES in Counter with CBC-MAC mode with extensions).

## 1.4 Core Security BBFs in 802.15.4

| BBF Name | Purpose | 802.15.4 Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Hide plaintext from eavesdroppers | AES-128 in **CTR (Counter)** mode |
| **Data Integrity** | Detect modification of payload | AES-128 in **CBC-MAC** mode |
| **Authentication** | Verify the sender's identity | Embedded in MIC computation |
| **Replay Protection** | Prevent reuse of old frames | **Frame Counter** (4 bytes) |
| **Access Control** | Decide which frames to accept | **ACL (Access Control List)** table |
| **Key Management** | Distribute & rotate keys | **Key Identifier** in frame header |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** IEEE Std 802.15.4-2006 (and later 2011, 2015, 2020 revisions) defines eight discrete *security suites*. Each suite specifies the security level — from *no security* (level 0) to *encrypted + authenticated* (level 7).

## 1.5 Why This Matters in IoT

Constrained IoT devices (sensors, actuators, smart bulbs) have:
- **8-bit to 32-bit microcontrollers** (e.g., CC2538, ESP32, ARM Cortex-M0).
- Only **4 KB to 256 KB of RAM**.
- Limited battery (often **coin cell** or energy-harvested).

They cannot afford **TLS 1.3** with RSA certificates. Therefore, **lightweight, symmetric-key security** built directly into the MAC layer (as in 802.15.4) is the practical answer. The BBF approach lets protocol designers pick exactly the security level they need — saving precious CPU cycles and bytes on the radio.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IEEE 802.15.4 Security Architecture — Layered View

The security model in 802.15.4 is defined at the **MAC sub-layer**, meaning security is applied to every MAC Protocol Data Unit (MPDU) before it is handed to the PHY for transmission. This is fundamentally different from higher-layer security (e.g., DTLS, TLS) and provides **link-layer** protection.

The architecture has three logical layers:

### Layer 1 — Security Policy Database
Each device maintains a database that stores:
- **Keys** (symmetric AES-128 keys, either pre-shared, derived, or transported).
- **Security Levels** (0 through 7).
- **Key Usage Policies** (key-to-keyidentifier mappings).
- **Device Addressing** (which addresses this key is for).

### Layer 2 — MAC Security Processing
When a frame is transmitted, the MAC layer:
1. Looks up the **key**, **security level**, and **mode** based on the destination address.
2. Constructs the **Auxiliary Security Header**.
3. Encrypts/authenticates the payload using **AES-CCM***.
4. Increments the **frame counter**.

### Layer 3 — Frame Filtering on Receive
On reception, the MAC layer:
1. Examines the **Security Enabled** bit in the Frame Control field.
2. Looks up the matching key from the ACL.
3. Verifies the **MIC**, decrypts the payload, and checks the **frame counter**.

## 2.2 The Eight Security Levels (Security Suites)

IEEE 802.15.4 defines **eight discrete security levels**, allowing protocol designers to balance security against computational cost. The standard uses **AES-CCM*** with parameter **M** (the length of the authentication tag, in bytes: **0, 4, 8, or 16**).

| Security Level | Name | Confidentiality? | Integrity (MIC) | M (bytes) | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | `SEC_None` | No | No | 0 | No security (debug only) |
| **1** | `MIC-32` | No | Yes | 4 | Integrity only — short MIC |
| **2** | `MIC-64` | No | Yes | 8 | Integrity only — medium MIC |
| **3** | `MIC-128` | No | Yes | 16 | Integrity only — full MIC |
| **4** | `ENC` | Yes | No | 0 | Encryption only (no auth) — **deprecated, risky** |
| **5** | `ENC-MIC-32` | Yes | Yes | 4 | Encrypt + short MIC |
| **6** | `ENC-MIC-64` | Yes | Yes | 8 | Encrypt + medium MIC |
| **7** | `ENC-MIC-128` | Yes | Yes | 16 | Encrypt + full MIC (max security) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** *Level 7 (`ENC-MIC-128`) is the recommended level for production IoT deployments. Level 4 (`ENC` alone) is explicitly discouraged because encryption without authentication is vulnerable to bit-flipping and chosen-ciphertext attacks.*

## 2.3 The Auxiliary Security Header (ASH)

When security is enabled, every outgoing frame carries an **Auxiliary Security Header** of 5, 6, or 10 bytes. It contains the cryptographic metadata the receiver needs to decrypt and authenticate the frame.

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Security Control** | 1 | Identifies the Security Level (3 bits) and Key Identifier Mode (2 bits) |
| **Frame Counter** | 4 | Replay protection value, monotonically incremented |
| **Key Identifier** | 0, 1, 5, or 9 | Identifies which key to use (depends on Key ID Mode) |

### Key Identifier Modes (from Security Control field)
- `Mode 00` (Implicit): Key is inferred from context — no Key ID field present (0 bytes added).
- `Mode 01` (1 byte): Index into the device's key table.
- `Mode 10` (5 bytes): 4-byte Key Source + 1-byte Key Index.
- `Mode 11` (9 bytes): 4-byte Key Source + 4-byte Key Index.

## 2.4 AES-CCM* — The Core Cryptographic Engine

**AES-CCM*** is the *extended* version of CCM (Counter with CBC-MAC). It allows the use of:
- Encryption only (level 4),
- Integrity only (levels 1–3),
- Both encryption and integrity (levels 5–7).

The `*` symbol denotes this flexibility.

**CCM = CTR (encryption) + CBC-MAC (integrity)**, both using the **same AES-128 key**.

### CCM Inputs
- **K**: 128-bit symmetric key.
- **N**: Nonce (13 bytes in 802.15.4 — derived from source address + frame counter + security level).
- **M**: Length of the authentication tag in bytes (0, 4, 8, or 16).
- **a-data**: Authenticated-only data (e.g., MAC header, ASH).
- **payload**: The data to be (optionally) encrypted and authenticated.

### CCM Outputs
- **ciphertext** (same length as payload if encrypted),
- **MIC** of length **M** bytes.

## 2.5 The MAC Frame Format (with Security Enabled)

| Octets | 2 | 1 | 0/2 | 0/2/8 | 0/2 | 0/2/8 | Variable | Variable | 2 | 1 | 4 | 1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Field | Frame Control | Sequence Number | Dest PAN ID | Dest Addr | Src PAN ID | Src Addr | **Aux Security Hdr** | Payload | FCS | Security Ctrl | Frame Counter | Key ID |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Disclaimer:** The following equations describe the *cryptographic construction* of AES-CCM*. They are tested as conceptual questions (e.g., "explain how MIC is computed") rather than numerical computation in the KTU exam.

| Concept | Equation / Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **AES block size** | $\text{Block} = 128 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ bytes}$ | Fixed for AES-128 |
| **CTR block formation** | $\text{CTR}_i = \text{Flags} \parallel \text{Nonce} \parallel i$ | $i$ is 16-bit counter, $0 \le i \le q$ |
| **Encryption (CTR mode)** | $C_j = P_j \oplus \text{AES}_K(\text{CTR}_j)$ | XOR plaintext block with keystream |
| **CBC-MAC Tag** | $T_0 = \text{AES}_K(B_0)$; $\quad T_i = \text{AES}_K(T_{i-1} \oplus B_i)$ | Iterative authentication chain |
| **MIC length** | $M \in \{0,\ 4,\ 8,\ 16\} \text{ bytes}$ | Set by Security Level |
| **Nonce (N) length** | $13 \text{ bytes}$ | Defined by 802.15.4 |
| **Nonce composition** | $N = \text{SrcAddr} \parallel \text{FrameCounter} \parallel \text{SecLevel}$ | 8-byte source + 4-byte counter + 1 byte |
| **Frame counter constraint** | $\text{FC}_{\text{new}} > \text{FC}_{\text{last\_accepted}}$ | Strictly increasing — no replay |
| **Effective key size** | $\vert K \vert = 128 \text{ bits}$ | All keys in 802.15.4 are AES-128 |
| **Max MPDU size** | $\vert \text{Frame} \vert_{\max} = 127 \text{ bytes}$ | Standard MAC frame limit |
| **Authentication overhead** | $\text{OH}_{\text{auth}} = M \text{ bytes}$ | Additional bytes per secured frame |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Nonce equation $N = \text{SrcAddr} \parallel \text{FrameCounter} \parallel \text{SecLevel}$ is the *uniqueness guarantee*. As long as the source address and frame counter never collide, the same AES key can be reused millions of times without breaking security.

## 2.7 Operational Walkthrough — Sending a Secured Frame

1. **Application layer** hands a payload (e.g., a sensor reading of `25.6 °C`) to the MAC layer.
2. MAC looks up the **security material** in its ACL using the destination address.
3. MAC increments its outgoing **frame counter** (e.g., `FC = 1042`).
4. MAC builds the **Nonce** $N = \text{SourceAddr} \parallel 00000412 \parallel \text{Level}$.
5. MAC constructs the **a-data** (Frame Control + Seq Num + Addressing + ASH).
6. MAC invokes **AES-CCM*** with $K$, $N$, $a\text{-data}$, and payload.
7. The encrypted payload and MIC are appended after the ASH.
8. PHY transmits the frame over the air.

## 2.8 Operational Walkthrough — Receiving a Secured Frame

1. PHY demodulates the frame and hands it to MAC.
2. MAC reads **Security Enabled** bit — if clear, accept normally.
3. If set, MAC reads **Security Control**, **Frame Counter**, and **Key Identifier**.
4. MAC consults the **ACL** to fetch the matching key.
5. MAC reconstructs the same **Nonce** using the source address from the frame.
6. MAC invokes **AES-CCM*** in **decrypt + verify** mode.
7. If MIC matches **and** $\text{FC}_{\text{frame}} > \text{FC}_{\text{last\_accepted}}$, accept; otherwise drop.
8. Plaintext payload is passed to upper layers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CCM Counter Block (CTR_i)

The **counter block** is the heart of CTR-mode encryption. In IEEE 802.15.4, every counter block is exactly **128 bits = 16 bytes**, constructed by concatenating three fields.

### Step 1 — Define the structure

$$\text{CTR}_i = \text{Flags} \parallel \text{Nonce} \parallel i$$

### Step 2 — Substitute field sizes

$$\text{Flags} = 1 \text{ byte (8 bits)}, \quad \text{Nonce} = 13 \text{ bytes (104 bits)}, \quad i = 2 \text{ bytes (16 bits)}$$

### Step 3 — Verify total length

$$1 + 13 + 2 = 16 \text{ bytes} = 128 \text{ bits} \;\; \checkmark$$

This confirms that $\text{CTR}_i$ fits exactly into one AES-128 block.

### Step 4 — Flags byte composition

The **Flags** byte is a packed field of **8 bits**:

$$\text{Flags}[7:3] = 0 \quad ; \quad \text{Flags}[2:0] = M - 2 \text{ (where } M \text{ is MIC length)}$$

For example:
- If $M = 4$: Flags bits 2–0 = `010` (decimal 2).
- If $M = 8$: Flags bits 2–0 = `110` (decimal 6).
- If $M = 16$: Flags bits 2–0 = `000` (decimal 0).

### Step 5 — Why is $i$ only 2 bytes?

The counter $i$ represents the **block index** of the plaintext/keystream. Because the maximum MPDU payload is **101 bytes** (after subtracting header overhead from 127 bytes), the maximum number of 16-byte plaintext blocks is:

$$q = \left\lceil \frac{101}{16} \right\rceil = 7 \text{ blocks}$$

So $i$ only needs to reach a value of $q+1 = 8$, which fits comfortably in 16 bits ($2^{16} = 65{,}536$).

## 3.2 Derivation of the CBC-MAC Tag (MIC Computation)

### Step 1 — Build the first block $B_0$

$$B_0 = \text{Flags}_0 \parallel \text{Nonce} \parallel L$$

where:
- $\text{Flags}_0 = 1$ byte indicating the data format (with/without a-data).
- $L = 1$ byte encoding the length of the payload in bytes ($1 \le L \le 255$).

### Step 2 — Build authentication blocks $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n$

The a-data and payload are concatenated, length-encoded, and zero-padded to fill **16-byte AES blocks**. If the length is not a multiple of 16, the last block is right-padded with zeros.

### Step 3 — Iterative CBC-MAC chain

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_0 &= \text{AES}_K(B_0) \\
X_1 &= \text{AES}_K(B_1 \oplus X_0) \\
X_2 &= \text{AES}_K(B_2 \oplus X_1) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
X_n &= \text{AES}_K(B_n \oplus X_{n-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Extract the MIC

$$\text{MIC} = \text{MSB}_M(X_n)$$

That is, we take the **leftmost M bytes** of the final 16-byte AES output as the authentication tag.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** CBC-MAC is *sequential* — each block depends on the previous. This is why AES-CCM* cannot be parallelized easily on the transmit side. Hardware accelerators on modern IoT chips (CC2538, EFR32) pre-compute these chains in 1–3 AES clock cycles per block.

## 3.3 Derivation of Encryption + Authentication Combined

### Step 1 — Generate keystream using CTR mode

For each plaintext block $P_j$ (where $j = 0, 1, \dots, q-1$):

$$S_j = \text{AES}_K(\text{CTR}_j)$$

### Step 2 — XOR plaintext with keystream

$$C_j = P_j \oplus S_j$$

### Step 3 — Concatenate ciphertext

$$\text{ciphertext} = C_0 \parallel C_1 \parallel \cdots \parallel C_{q-1}$$

### Step 4 — Append MIC

The MIC computed in Section 3.2 is appended directly after the ciphertext:

$$\text{Secured Payload} = \text{ciphertext} \parallel \text{MIC}$$

### Step 5 — Receiver-side verification

The receiver:
1. Re-runs **CBC-MAC** on $a\text{-data} \parallel \text{ciphertext}$ using the same key and nonce.
2. Computes $\text{MIC}_{\text{computed}}$.
3. Compares $\text{MIC}_{\text{computed}} = \text{MIC}_{\text{frame}}$ in **constant time**.
4. Only if equal, it XORs ciphertext with the same keystream to recover plaintext.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. Defense Mapping

| Attack | BBF Defense | 802.15.4 Mechanism | Failure Mode if BBF Missing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping** | Confidentiality | AES-CTR encryption (Levels 5–7) | Attacker reads all sensor data |
| **Bit-flipping** | Integrity | CBC-MAC MIC (Levels 1–3, 5–7) | Attacker alters command frames (e.g., "OFF" → "ON") |
| **Forgery** | Authentication | Embedded in MIC computation | Attacker injects fake node |
| **Replay** | Replay protection | Frame counter + ACL storage | Attacker re-sends old "unlock door" command |
| **Unauthorized device** | Access Control | ACL table | Rogue sensor joins network |
| **Key compromise** | Key separation | Key Identifier Modes + multiple keys | Single key leaks entire network |
| **Jamming (DoS)** | Out of scope | (Handled by PHY layer — LBT, CCA) | Radio silenced — link broken |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Nonce & CTR Block Construction

**Problem:** A device with 64-bit extended source address `0x8877665544332211` transmits a secured frame using Security Level 5 (`ENC-MIC-32`) with frame counter value `0x000003A7`. Construct the **Nonce N** and the **first CTR block (CTR₀)**.

### Step 1 — Determine MIC length M

For Security Level 5: $M = 4$ bytes.

### Step 2 — Determine Flags byte for CTR

$$\text{Flags} = M - 2 = 4 - 2 = 2 = 0b00000010 = 0x02$$

### Step 3 — Concatenate to build Nonce N

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \text{SourceAddr} \parallel \text{FrameCounter} \parallel \text{SecurityLevel} \\
  &= \underbrace{8877665544332211}_{\text{8 bytes}} \parallel \underbrace{000003A7}_{\text{4 bytes}} \parallel \underbrace{05}_{\text{1 byte}}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $N$ is 13 bytes: `88 77 66 55 44 33 22 11 00 00 03 A7 05`.

### Step 4 — Construct CTR₀

$$
\text{CTR}_0 = \underbrace{02}_{\text{Flags}} \parallel \underbrace{N}_{\text{13 bytes}} \parallel \underbrace{0000}_{i=0}
$$

$$\text{CTR}_0 = \texttt{02 88 77 66 55 44 33 22 11 00 00 03 A7 05 00 00}$$

This 16-byte block is now fed to $\text{AES}_K(\cdot)$ to generate the **first keystream block** for encrypting the first 16 bytes of payload.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of uniqueness:** If the same source address ever sends a frame with the same frame counter, the nonce repeats, breaking CCM security. Hence the **strict monotonic increment** of the frame counter is not optional — it is a cryptographic requirement.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison — CCM vs. CCM* vs. Other Modes

| Mode | Confidentiality | Integrity | Auth Tag Length | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AES-CBC** | Yes | No (no MAC) | N/A | Legacy systems |
| **AES-CBC + HMAC** | Yes | Yes | Variable | TLS 1.2 (heavy) |
| **AES-CCM** (RFC 3610) | Yes | Yes (combined) | 4/8/16 | Original 802.15.4 |
| **AES-CCM*** (802.15.4) | Optional | Yes (combined) | 0/4/8/16 | 802.15.4-2006 onward |
| **AES-GCM** | Yes | Yes (parallelizable) | 16 | WiFi WPA3, 5G (heavy) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CCM* and not GCM?** GCM is faster on modern CPUs because it can be parallelized. But GCM requires **hardware AES + carry-less multipliers**, which are rare on sub-$1 IoT chips. CCM* uses only standard AES — making it implementable on virtually any 8-bit microcontroller with an AES peripheral.

    end

    aclDB --> ctrl
    ctrl --> ashr

```

    buildAData["Step 4 Build a-data = MAC Header parallel Aux Security Header"]
    ctrMode["Step 5 Generate Keystream CTR_i = AES-K of Flags parallel Nonce parallel i"]
    xorEnc["Step 6 Compute Ciphertext C_j = Plaintext P_j XOR Keystream S_j"]
    cbcmac["Step 7 Compute CBC-MAC over a-data and ciphertext Chain X_i = AES-K of B_i XOR X_i-1"]
    micTrunc["Step 8 Truncate MIC to M bytes MSB-M of final X_n"]
    assemble["Step 9 Assemble MPDU = Header parallel Aux Sec Hdr parallel Ciphertext parallel MIC"]
    phyOut["Step 10 Forward to PHY for Modulation"]
    doneTx["Frame Transmitted"]

    startTx --> lookupKey
    lookupKey --> incFC
    incFC --> buildNonce
    buildNonce --> buildAData
    buildAData --> ctrMode

```

    reconstructN["Step 4 Reconstruct Nonce N from Source Address and Frame Counter"]

    dropFrame["Drop Frame Integrity Failure"]
